Readmissions are an outcome with many causes that aren't in a single department's control. Penalizing the department whose patients return shifts behavior in predictable and partly perverse ways.

The main risk patterns:

(1) Patient selection. Departments learn that taking on sicker patients raises readmission rates. The discharge planning, the choice of which patients to admit in the first place, the willingness to accept transfers — all become biased against the hardest cases. The patients who most need the department's expertise are the ones it's now most reluctant to serve.

(2) Discharge gaming. Patients can be discharged into 'observation status' or to short-stay alternatives that don't count as admissions. The next time they show up, the count starts over. Or discharge can be delayed past the threshold window (some readmission metrics count 30-day return; observe-without-admitting for 24 hours past day 30 and the readmission doesn't count). The metric improves; the underlying clinical reality doesn't.

(3) Refusal to readmit. The cleanest way to avoid being penalized for readmission is to not admit the patient back. If the same patient returns with worsening of the same condition, the department may treat them in the ED, observe, and discharge again rather than readmit. Outcomes degrade.

(4) Misattribution. A patient discharged by one department who readmits with a different condition (post-surgical complication that develops as cardiac issue, for example) may readmit under another department. The penalty hits the wrong service or no one, distorting the picture.

(5) Failure to address the actual causes. Readmissions often reflect post-discharge factors the inpatient department doesn't control: medication adherence, follow-up appointment access, home environment, social support, follow-up primary care quality. A penalty model that puts the entire weight on the discharging department creates pressure without giving the department the tools to actually reduce readmissions.

Redesigning the program:

(a) Shift from departmental penalty to shared accountability. Readmissions involve discharge planning, inpatient care, transition to ambulatory care, primary care follow-up, and patient/family education. Hold the whole pathway accountable, with funding to make the transition work — care navigators, follow-up calls, prescription support, home health where needed.

(b) Risk-adjust the metric. Hospitals serving sicker, lower-resourced populations will have higher raw readmission rates. Without risk adjustment, the penalty falls disproportionately on safety-net institutions. Risk-adjusted comparison is the minimum defensible standard.

(c) Focus on planned vs. unplanned readmissions. Some readmissions are clinically appropriate (planned chemotherapy, staged surgery). Others reflect preventable failure. The metric should distinguish.

(d) Pair the metric with investment in transitions of care. The departments most penalized are typically the ones with the least resourced post-discharge support. Pay for the support before measuring the outcome.

(e) Use the metric for learning, not just punishment. Identify the patterns — which patient subgroups, which conditions, which discharge contexts are driving readmissions — and invest in addressing them. A metric used only for punishment produces gaming; a metric used for diagnosis can produce improvement.

(f) Time-limit the lookback. Twelve-month rolling rates rather than single-month points; signal-to-noise on monthly rates for a single department is poor.

The overarching principle: readmissions are a system outcome, and a metric that pretends a single department owns them produces dysfunction. Shared accountability with investment in the transitions is more likely to actually reduce them.

The risk is that departments treating sicker or socially vulnerable patients may be penalized for factors partly outside their control. It may also create pressure to avoid admitting high-risk patients or to manipulate classification. A better design would use risk-adjusted readmission measures, examine avoidable versus expected readmissions, and pair accountability with support such as discharge planning, medication reconciliation, and post-discharge follow-up. The metric should drive root-cause improvement, not crude punishment.

queries.prompt: A company wants to replace all human approval steps in expense reimbursement with AI. Assess where automation is likely safe and where it is not.

expected_answers.answer:
Where automation in expense reimbursement is likely safe:

(1) Receipt OCR and data extraction. Reading the merchant, date, amount, and category from a receipt is a well-bounded task. Errors are usually small (a date by a day, a category misclassification) and easily corrected. Automation is a clear win — the work is mechanical, repeatable, and the AI's mistakes are detectable.

(2) Policy compliance checks for clear-cut cases. The expense fits a category with a per-meal limit, was incurred during a business trip, and is under the threshold — auto-approve. The expense exceeds a hard limit, was incurred outside the company's policy window, or violates a flat rule — auto-reject (or escalate). The boundary cases are where humans should still intervene; the unambiguous cases don't need a human.

(3) Recurring expenses with established patterns. Monthly software subscriptions, parking, recurring vendor charges that have been approved repeatedly — auto-approve based on past patterns. The exception flagging catches genuine changes.

(4) Duplicate detection. The same receipt submitted twice (by accident or otherwise) is detectable by AI and reliably blockable. The penalty for a false positive is small (a quick re-look); the benefit is real.

Where automation is unsafe or unwise:

(1) Borderline policy interpretation. 'A client meal' vs. 'a team meal' may turn on context the AI doesn't have. 'Reasonable hotel rate' depends on city, season, event. Forcing these into auto-decisions produces both false approvals (compliance risk) and false rejections (employee frustration that drives travel-and-expense quitting).

(2) Anomaly detection that becomes accusation. Flagging an unusual expense for review is fine; auto-denying it on a flag is not. The same employee may have a legitimate one-off (a flight had to be rebooked; a client dinner ran long), and treating them as policy-violators creates trust damage that outlives the saving on policy enforcement.

(3) Tax categorization for unfamiliar items. The boundary between meals and entertainment, between training and conferences, between travel and commuting — these have real tax consequences for the company, and categorization errors compound. AI is fine for the standard cases; it should escalate when context is ambiguous.

(4) Cases involving sensitive context. Reimbursement for medical, accessibility, or personal-safety-related items (the employee bought a meal at the only restaurant near their hotel; the employee took a rideshare because walking back was unsafe) require contextual judgment the AI doesn't have.

(5) Communication with the employee. Auto-generated denial messages, especially with reasons the employee finds insulting or wrong, do real damage. Even when the AI's decision is correct, the communication should be human-reviewed for tone — or at minimum templated carefully so that the auto-rejection doesn't read as accusation.

(6) Cases that imply broader misconduct. If a pattern of expenses suggests fraud, expense-claim padding, or policy-bypass schemes, the right move is human investigation, not automated denial. The investigation pattern, the chain of custody on evidence, and the way the employee is approached all matter, and the AI doesn't have the situational judgment to handle this.

The shape of a good deployment: automate the high-volume, low-stakes, well-bounded decisions (receipt extraction, clear policy compliance, recurring expense recognition, duplicate detection). Escalate the ambiguous cases to a human. Use the AI to assist the human reviewer on the escalated cases (here are the matching policies, here are similar past cases, here's what looks unusual) rather than to make the call. Provide a clear, fast appeals path. The total work goes down by maybe 70–80%, the employee experience improves, and the controls don't degrade — because the controls were never really enforced by the rote part of the work anyway. The rote part just hid that judgment was being skipped.

Automation is likely safe for low-risk, repetitive checks: extracting receipt totals, matching dates, validating required fields, applying explicit policy thresholds, and flagging obvious duplicates. It is less safe where policy interpretation is ambiguous, fraud signals are subtle, supporting context matters, or a denial affects employee trust. A sensible design automates clear approvals and routes exceptions, conflicts, and high-value claims to humans. The company should measure false approvals, false denials, review burden, and appeal outcomes before expanding scope.

queries.prompt: A bank wants to use an AI model to triage customer complaints. What performance measures matter beyond classification accuracy?

expected_answers.answer:
Performance measures beyond classification accuracy for an AI complaint-triage system:

(1) Calibration of routing confidence. The model classifies a complaint into one of N categories with some confidence. If the system says 90% confidence and is right 70% of the time, calibration is bad — the confidence isn't actionable. Bank operations need confidence levels that mean what they say, so that high-confidence routings can be acted on automatically and low-confidence ones can be flagged for human review. Measure calibration with reliability diagrams and Brier scores, not just accuracy at a single threshold.

(2) Per-category performance, especially on minority classes. Aggregate accuracy can be 95% while accuracy on the rare-but-important category (regulatory complaint, allegation of fraud, vulnerable-customer indicator) is 50%. The rare categories are where the cost of misclassification is highest — a regulatory complaint misrouted to general support can become a compliance issue. Disaggregate by category and measure both recall (did the system catch the cases that belong here) and precision (when it routes to this category, is it right).

(3) Fairness across customer segments. The bank has obligations under fair lending and consumer protection rules that include how customer complaints are handled. If the system systematically routes complaints from certain demographic groups, geographies, or languages to lower-quality resolution paths, that's a regulatory exposure regardless of intent. Measure routing distributions, resolution outcomes, and time-to-resolution across customer segments.

(4) Handling of vulnerable-customer signals. Complaints from customers who appear to be in financial distress, who mention specific situations (recent bereavement, illness, job loss), or who exhibit linguistic patterns associated with vulnerability require different handling — typically a human escalation with appropriate sensitivity. The system's recall on detecting these signals is more important than its overall accuracy, and a miss has real human and reputational cost.

(5) Latency. Time from complaint received to routed-for-action. For some complaint types (fraud reports, regulatory issues), the right time-to-action is minutes; for others, hours or days is fine. The system should be measured against per-category latency targets, not a global average.

(6) Downstream resolution quality. The metric isn't just whether the routing was correct; it's whether the routed complaint was actually resolved well. If a complaint is correctly routed to 'billing dispute' but billing-dispute resolutions in that period have a 60% customer-satisfaction rate, the system's routing didn't help. Track resolution outcome (resolved-on-first-contact, customer-satisfaction-on-resolution, complaint-reopen-rate) by routing decision.

(7) Drift detection. The pattern of complaints changes over time — new product, new external event, new fraud pattern, regulatory change. A system trained on yesterday's data degrades. Monitor input distributions and per-category accuracy over time, with automatic flags when they shift beyond a defined threshold.

(8) Adversarial robustness. Customers occasionally try to game the system — by using keywords that route to a more responsive queue, or by escalating language to trigger urgency routing. The system should be robust to this, and the metric is how often gaming attempts succeed.

(9) Explainability. For escalation paths (especially regulatory, vulnerable-customer, and disputed routing decisions), the system should be able to explain why a complaint was routed where it was. Not as a marketing virtue, but as an operational requirement — when a customer or regulator asks, the bank needs to be able to answer.

(10) Operational metrics for the human reviewers. The system creates work for downstream teams. How long does it take humans to review escalations, override routings, and provide feedback that improves the system? If the system's design creates so many escalations that the human teams can't keep up, the system has shifted the problem rather than solved it.

Accuracy is one number; what the bank actually needs to know is whether the system is operating safely, fairly, and effectively across the populations and case types that matter. The 10 measures above (or some subset chosen to match the deployment) describe that operating reality.

Beyond accuracy, the bank should measure severe misrouting rate, recall for high-risk complaint categories, time-to-resolution, escalation latency, consistency across customer groups, calibration of confidence, and whether complaints requiring regulatory attention are surfaced reliably. Because errors are asymmetric, a harmless over-escalation is not equivalent to missing a serious complaint. The evaluation should reflect those unequal costs.

queries.prompt: A startup reports that its monthly active users doubled after launching a referral program. What questions would determine whether the program created durable growth?

expected_answers.answer:
Questions to determine whether the referral program created durable growth or temporary spike:

(1) What share of the new users came from referrals, vs. from the publicity around the referral program (announcement traffic, press coverage), vs. from coincident factors (a feature launch, seasonal trend, competitor stumble)? Doubling MAU coincident with the referral launch is consistent with the program working, with the announcement creating a separate surge, or with something else entirely.

(2) Cohort retention. The headline is MAU growth in the month of launch. The diagnostic is whether the new users in that month are still active in month 2, 3, 6. Referred users sometimes retain better than other channels (they have a friend already using the product); sometimes worse (they signed up for the bonus, not the product). The cohort curve over time tells you which.

(3) Engagement intensity of new users. Do they use the product the way existing users do, or differently? Referred users who do one minimum action to claim the referral bonus and then disappear are not real growth; they're a vanity metric paid for in referral incentives.

(4) Cost per acquired user. What did the referral program cost — bonus payments to referrers, bonus payments to new users, fraud losses (referral schemes attract gaming), the engineering cost of building and running the program? Divide by the count of users actually retained at 90 days. If CAC under the referral program is similar to or higher than your other channels, the program isn't a new lever; it's just a different one.

(5) Referrer behavior. Did referrers ramp up their own engagement during the program (the incentive made them more active), or did they just spam invites? Programs where referrers stay engaged are healthier than programs where referrers churn through their address book and then disappear.

(6) New-user quality. Are the new users the same kind of customers as your existing base (in demographic, use-case, willingness to pay), or are they a different segment that may not stick? Some referral programs systematically pull in users similar to existing ones (which is good, growth in the core); some pull in different users (which may or may not be sustainable).

(7) Will the spike be reversed? Referral programs that depend on a referral bonus often see a decline in new-user acquisition once the program ends or when bonus payouts taper. If the program's growth is real (network effects, viral coefficient sustaining itself), it persists; if it's bought, it ends when the spending ends.

(8) Comparison to a counterfactual. What would have happened without the program? Some referral-style growth would have occurred organically (word-of-mouth is real even without a formal program). The marginal growth is the program's impact, not the total. If the company had a meaningful natural growth rate, much of the apparent doubling may be the organic baseline plus a smaller program contribution.

(9) Saturation. Has the program already reached the most likely referrers — the users who naturally evangelize the product? If so, subsequent months will see diminishing returns regardless of how the program is run.

(10) Side effects on existing users. Do the bonuses paid to new users (or to referrers) create a feeling among existing users that they're being underserved? Sometimes a generous referral program creates churn among the unaddressed loyal base.

Durable growth looks like: cohort retention curves for referred users that look similar to or better than other channels, sustained acquisition rate after the launch month, referrer engagement that persists, and new users whose lifetime value justifies the acquisition cost. Temporary spike looks like: month-of-launch acquisition collapses afterward, retention curves for referred users are steep, referrers stopped after first bonus, and CAC under the program is higher than other channels once you back out fraud and gaming. Most real referral programs are between these — a real but smaller-than-it-looks contribution to growth, mixed with some noise.

I would ask whether referred users retain at similar rates to prior cohorts, whether activity is meaningful rather than shallow signups, whether growth cannibalized organic acquisition, what the cost per activated and retained referred user is, and whether the referral incentive attracts low-quality behavior or fraud. Doubling MAU is impressive only if cohort quality and unit economics hold.

queries.notes: Requires moving from topline growth to quality of growth.

queries.prompt: An organization wants a single KPI for 'AI adoption.' Explain why that is a bad idea and propose a small balanced scorecard.

expected_answers.answer:
Why a single KPI for 'AI adoption' is a bad idea:

(1) The metric conflates inputs with outcomes. 'Adoption' can mean licenses purchased, employees with access, employees who logged in once, employees actively using, or employees using to do work that matters. Each is a different thing, and a single number pretends they're commensurate. The metric will be defined to be easy to grow; what grows will be the easy part (licenses, single-time use) and not necessarily the part that creates value.

(2) The metric incentivizes the wrong behavior. If 'AI adoption' is what's measured, business units optimize for the measure. The cheapest way to raise it is to push tools onto more people regardless of whether the tools fit their work. The result is widespread but shallow use, more friction (people who don't want or need the tools getting them), and a metric that goes up while value goes down.

(3) AI is not a uniform thing. The decision to adopt an AI coding assistant for engineering, an AI customer-service tool for support, an AI scheduling assistant for operations, and an AI writing tool for marketing are four different decisions with four different value cases, four different risks, and four different success conditions. Treating them as a single category for metric purposes is like treating 'software adoption' as a single number — true but useless.

(4) The metric assumes adoption is the goal. It isn't. The goal is whatever the business is trying to achieve — efficiency, growth, quality, customer experience. AI is a tool. A metric that treats tool adoption as the end produces theater (we use AI for everything!) without forcing the conversation about whether each use is producing value.

(5) The metric creates uniform pressure across heterogeneous contexts. Some functions benefit substantially from AI; some don't yet, in their current form. A single 'AI adoption' KPI applied uniformly punishes the functions where AI is not yet the right answer and rewards the ones that figured it out first — without distinguishing 'figured it out' from 'matched the easy use case.'

A better balanced scorecard, used together:

(1) Productivity-adjacent metrics in functions where AI was deployed. Not 'AI usage,' but 'outputs per FTE in the function where AI is meant to help' (with appropriate quality controls). The right granularity is by function, not by company.

(2) Quality outcomes in those same functions. Were errors, customer complaints, defects, rework affected? Productivity gains achieved by lowering quality are not gains.

(3) Employee experience. Did the affected employees feel the AI helped or hurt their work? Survey periodically. Both data are real — productivity that the workforce hates is unsustainable.

(4) Customer experience (where applicable). For customer-facing AI deployments, did satisfaction, resolution, retention move? Internal metrics are necessary but not sufficient.

(5) Risk indicators. Were there incidents — bad outputs, compliance issues, customer complaints traceable to AI decisions? A scorecard without a risk indicator under-counts the downside of deployment.

(6) Spend efficiency. Cost per produced output, not 'AI cost' or 'AI usage' alone. The dollars spent on AI relative to the dollars saved or revenue generated. This is the bottom-line check.

The scorecard is not five rolled-up indices; it's six numbers, by major business function, reported together so trade-offs are visible. The board reads it as a portfolio and asks where deployments are working, where they're failing, and where the investment should shift. That's what an honest measure of 'AI adoption' looks like — a description of the operating reality, not a single number to optimize.

A single KPI will likely collapse different phenomena into a misleading headline. Logins measure exposure, token volume measures usage, and time saved measures claimed value, but none alone shows durable business impact. A small scorecard could include active users in target workflows, repeat usage, task completion or cycle-time improvement, quality or rework measures, and user-reported trust. Adoption should mean productive integration, not just interaction volume.

queries.prompt: A public transit agency sees ridership rise after lowering fares. How should it assess whether the change was financially and socially successful?

expected_answers.answer:
Assessing whether a fare reduction was financially and socially successful:

Financially:

(1) Revenue and ridership math. Fare × riders = revenue. Lower fare per rider × more riders = ? Calculate the actual revenue change. If ridership rose enough to offset the per-rider revenue loss, revenue is flat or up; if not, revenue is down. The fare elasticity (% change in ridership / % change in fare) is the operating number — usually between −0.2 and −0.5 for urban transit, meaning a 10% fare cut produces 2–5% more riders. Riders typically don't rise proportionally to the cut, so revenue typically falls.

(2) Subsidy implications. Almost all US transit agencies are subsidized; fares cover a fraction of operating cost. The revenue from fares is one input; the larger picture is the operating budget, capital budget, and ongoing subsidy from the city, state, or federal level. A fare cut typically requires more subsidy. The financial question is whether the increased ridership creates enough collateral value (lower-cost transit per rider at higher utilization, reduced parking demand, reduced road congestion) to justify the subsidy increase.

(3) Operating cost dynamics. Higher ridership has marginal cost — additional vehicles, drivers, frequency, maintenance — but per-rider operating cost typically falls as service is better utilized. Existing routes carrying more passengers are cheaper per rider; new service to support growth is expensive. If the ridership growth concentrated on already-served routes, cost per rider drops. If it required substantial new service, cost per rider may rise.

Socially:

(4) Equity of who benefits. Lower fares disproportionately help lower-income riders, who are more fare-sensitive and for whom transit is more often the only practical option. The fare cut redistributes resources toward this group. Track ridership demographics where the data exists — by income, by neighborhood — to see who actually benefited.

(5) Modal shift. Did the new riders come from cars (which is a public benefit — congestion, air quality, climate, road wear), from walking/cycling (which is mode-neutral or negative), from new trips that wouldn't have happened (which is mostly positive), or from prior transit riders who now take more trips (which is the existing customer benefiting)? The composition of new riders matters for the public-benefit calculus.

(6) Access to opportunity. Did the lower fare connect people to jobs, healthcare, education they previously couldn't reach? This is the social-mobility argument for transit subsidy, and it's measurable through commuting patterns, employment outcomes for residents of transit-dependent areas, and survey data.

(7) Crowding and service quality. Did the higher ridership produce crowding that degrades the experience? A successful fare reduction that fills buses past comfort levels and pushes existing riders to alternatives is a different success than one that fills underutilized capacity.

The combined assessment:

The transit agency should report (a) revenue change vs. budget, (b) ridership change in absolute and percentage terms, (c) fare elasticity observed, (d) modal-shift estimate, (e) demographic composition of new riders, (f) operating cost per rider at the new ridership level, (g) crowding metrics on affected routes, (h) qualitative assessment of access expansion, and (i) the implicit subsidy per new rider (revenue lost / ridership gained × duration).

The political conversation often reduces to 'ridership is up, so it worked' or 'revenue is down, so it failed.' Neither is the right framing. A successful transit-fare reduction is one where the public benefit (modal shift, equity, access expansion) plus the operating efficiency gains plus the longer-term ridership build outweigh the revenue loss and any additional subsidy. That's a balanced judgment, not a single number — and the agency that reports it that way is being honest, not evasive.

Financially, it should compare fare revenue loss with ridership gains, operating costs from extra service needs, and any external funding goals. Socially, it should examine who gained access, whether low-income or transit-dependent riders benefited, whether traffic congestion or emissions changed, and whether service reliability held. A fare policy can be socially worthwhile even if farebox recovery falls, but the agency should state the objective rather than mixing standards opportunistically.

queries.prompt: A retailer is deciding whether to close low-margin stores. Explain why store-level margin alone may be insufficient.

expected_answers.answer:
Why store-level margin is insufficient for the closure decision:

(1) Allocations. Store-level P&L includes allocated overhead — corporate costs, IT, marketing, leadership salary — divided across stores in some way. Those costs don't disappear when a store closes; they reallocate to remaining stores, making their margins appear worse. The 'unprofitable' store may be carrying its share of fixed overhead in a way that, on closure, makes other stores' margins look bad. Look at controllable-store-margin separately from allocated overhead.

(2) Cannibalization. Closing a store may not redistribute its sales to other stores in the chain. Some customers will shop at the next-nearest store; some will switch to a competitor; some will stop shopping the brand entirely. The right number is not the closed store's revenue; it's the revenue that's actually lost to the chain. In dense urban formats, the closed store may capture little net revenue beyond what would have been earned at the next store; in suburban or rural formats with no nearby store, the closed store's revenue is mostly lost. Estimate the recapture rate.

(3) Cross-store customers and brand reach. Some customers shop at multiple stores in the chain or use the store for impulse purchases on the way to or from other destinations. Closing the store removes that touchpoint and may affect customer engagement with the chain more broadly. Loyalty data and basket-share data are the relevant inputs.

(4) Real estate. A store carries a lease that may have years remaining. Closing the store doesn't end the rent obligation; it just stops the revenue against the rent. Closure produces a lease-exit cost that needs to be factored. Conversely, the lease may have appreciated and a sublease or assignment could turn it into a capital event.

(5) Workforce. Closing a store eliminates jobs and concentrates morale damage in the affected region. Some employees may transfer; some won't. The reputational cost in local labor markets and community relationships matters for future stores and hiring.

(6) Geographic strategy. A store closure in a specific region may signal retreat from that market, with knock-on effects on the chain's positioning vs. competitors and on its ability to negotiate with suppliers or municipalities elsewhere. The strategic value of presence matters even when the unit is unprofitable.

(7) Operating-leverage opportunity. The store may be unprofitable at current cost structure, but a redesign (smaller footprint, different format, different staffing model, reduced inventory) could make it profitable. Closure forecloses that option. The right comparison is not closed-store vs. as-is store, but closed-store vs. reformatted store vs. as-is store, with realistic estimates of the cost and probability of each transition.

(8) Customer-acquisition cost. The store likely acquired customers — locals who walked in, became regulars, signed up for loyalty programs. Acquiring those customers at the next-nearest store, or online, is not free. The total customer-acquisition cost of replacing the closed store's customer base may exceed the loss.

(9) Brand and signal effects. Closures generate news (especially in the era of social media), and store closures specifically have a public signal of decline. The narrative cost is sometimes larger than the operational cost.

A reasonable decision framework:

(a) Re-derive store contribution, stripping out allocated overhead and computing the true marginal contribution (revenue minus directly-variable costs).

(b) Estimate recapture — what share of the closed store's revenue moves to other channels in the chain.

(c) Net the recapture against the avoided costs (rent, labor, inventory) to find net financial impact.

(d) Apply non-financial factors — strategic position, brand signal, employee impact — and decide whether they justify operating below margin.

(e) Test the reformat option seriously before closure. Sometimes the answer is the store works at a smaller footprint or with a different product mix.

(f) If closure is the answer, plan it well — minimize the workforce damage, preserve customer relationships through transition offers to the next store, and consider whether the lease can be repurposed or assigned. Bad closures cost more than well-managed ones.

Store-level margin is the headline; the decision-grade analysis is several layers below it.

Some stores create halo effects for online sales, serve as pickup and returns hubs, sustain brand presence, or support local marketing. Conversely, some stores may look acceptable on contribution margin while consuming scarce management attention or masking lease obligations. The decision should include direct profitability, network effects, customer behavior migration, lease exit costs, and strategic role in the portfolio.

queries.notes: Requires systems thinking beyond a local P&L.

queries.prompt: A SaaS company has strong renewal rates but weak expansion revenue. What hypotheses should leadership test?

expected_answers.answer:
Hypotheses to test, ordered by what's typically wrong when this pattern shows up:

(1) The product solves an individual workflow problem but doesn't expand to organizational use. Renewal works because individuals keep using the product; expansion fails because the product doesn't naturally add seats or grow within the account. Diagnostic: of the accounts you renewed, what share are the same number of seats or the same product configuration as at last renewal? If the answer is most of them, the product is being treated as a per-seat tool with no organic expansion path.

(2) The customer-success motion is reactive rather than expansive. The CS team is structured around renewal and issue resolution; they're not running expansion plays — multi-team adoption discussions, executive briefings, ROI conversations that surface budget for additional licenses. Diagnostic: ask CS leadership what fraction of QBR conversations include explicit expansion discussion vs. health and adoption. If most are the latter, CS is functioning as retention insurance, not as a growth function.

(3) Pricing structure discourages expansion. Per-seat pricing with no volume tiers, or feature-bundling that doesn't create natural upgrade paths, can mean that expansion isn't economically attractive to the customer. Diagnostic: examine the price-per-seat for your largest accounts. Is it the same as the price for a 5-seat account? If so, large accounts have no incentive to expand and many incentives to standardize on the most-needed seats only.

(4) Champion turnover and absence of multi-thread relationships. If your relationship in each account is with a single champion, that champion's departure (job change, role change, organizational restructuring) creates a renewal risk and a stall on expansion. Diagnostic: how many named contacts do you have per account, and at how many levels? If you average 1–2 contacts per account, the relationship is shallow and expansion is hard to land.

(5) Product-market fit is narrower than the company believes. The product fits a specific use case that the existing buyers found; trying to expand to adjacent use cases within the same account requires the product to do things it's not actually good at. Renewal works because the original use case is real; expansion fails because the adjacent use cases aren't well-served. Diagnostic: when expansion conversations happen, what specifically are customers asked to expand into? If the company keeps proposing the same set of features that don't fit naturally, the product has a fit problem.

(6) Pricing-page structure or sales motion makes expansion friction-heavy. Expansion that requires a new contract, a new procurement cycle, or a new evaluation is expansion that doesn't happen. Diagnostic: how does a customer add seats, add product, or upgrade? If the answer involves a sales contact, a re-papering process, or weeks of legal review for additions to an active contract, the operational friction is suppressing expansion.

(7) The competitive landscape has filled the expansion space. Competitors have moved into the categories you'd expand into, and your accounts have begun bringing those competitors in for the adjacent use cases. Diagnostic: what other tools do your largest customers use in adjacent categories? If competitor logos are showing up in account-mapping conversations, you've ceded expansion ground.

(8) Customer organization isn't structurally ready to expand. Some buyer organizations centralize software purchases; some leave them to teams. If your accounts are structured to limit per-team software autonomy, expansion has to go through procurement at every step, which is slow and politically expensive for any team to initiate.

The diagnostic move is to take the top 20 largest accounts and run a structured analysis: for each, identify which of these patterns explains the lack of expansion. The patterns concentrate — typically 2–3 explain most of the cases. That's where to invest: in product changes if (1) or (5) dominate, in CS retraining and pricing if (2) and (3) dominate, in customer-account-mapping and multi-thread sales if (4) dominates, in operational friction reduction if (6) dominates, in competitive response if (7) dominates.

The wrong move is to assume the answer is 'we need to sell harder.' Strong renewals and weak expansion almost always reflect a structural issue, not a motivation issue.

Leadership should test whether the product solves a bounded problem with limited seat or usage expansion, whether pricing discourages broader adoption, whether customer success is focused on retention rather than growth, whether integrations or governance features block cross-team rollout, and whether the initial buyer persona lacks authority to expand. Renewal proves baseline value; expansion requires broader organizational value and a route to capture it.

queries.prompt: A government agency wants to publish a dashboard ranking hospitals. What principles should guide metric selection so the dashboard informs rather than distorts?

expected_answers.answer:
Principles for metric selection on a public hospital-ranking dashboard:

(1) Risk-adjust. Hospitals serve different patient populations — by age, comorbidity, socioeconomic status, acuity, demographics. A dashboard that doesn't adjust will systematically punish hospitals serving harder populations. Risk adjustment isn't perfect; admit the limits. But unadjusted rankings are worse than imperfect adjustment.

(2) Choose outcomes patients care about, not process measures patients don't understand. Mortality, readmission, hospital-acquired infection, patient-reported recovery, time-to-treatment — these reflect what patients experience. Process measures (compliance with checklists, screening rates) are useful operationally but should be aggregated into outcome categories on a public dashboard.

(3) Disaggregate by service line. 'Best hospital' is a meaningless category. A hospital may be excellent for orthopedics and poor for cardiac care, or excellent for routine surgery and poor for complex cases. Ranking by service line or condition gives patients usable information; ranking overall hides differences that matter.

(4) Report sample size and uncertainty. A hospital that performed 8 of a procedure in a year and had 1 bad outcome is statistically indistinguishable from one that performed 8 and had 0. Don't rank as if a 12.5% complication rate is meaningfully different from 0% at that sample size. Show confidence intervals; bin hospitals into ranges where statistically distinguishable; suppress rankings for cells with too few cases.

(5) Account for case selection. A hospital that turns down complex cases will look better than one that accepts them. Some metrics should include a measure of case complexity or refusal rate. Otherwise the dashboard rewards cream-skimming.

(6) Update with appropriate frequency. Annually for most metrics, semi-annually for things that change faster. Real-time is too noisy to be useful and creates pressure for short-term gaming. Lagged enough that the data is reliable.

(7) Include access. A hospital that's high-quality but inaccessible to half the population (geography, insurance, language, hours) is not the same kind of public good as one that's slightly less high-quality but accessible. Report accessibility alongside quality.

(8) Be transparent about methodology. Public dashboards that don't publish their adjustment models, weighting, and definitions invite manipulation and erode trust. Hospitals being ranked should be able to verify inputs and challenge errors. Patients (and journalists) should be able to understand how the rankings work.

(9) Distinguish ranking from labeling. A dashboard that ranks #1 through #N invites the 'who's first' question and produces gaming. A dashboard that labels hospitals into bands (e.g., 'meets quality threshold for service line X' vs. 'does not meet') is more useful — it communicates what patients can act on without overstating precision.

(10) Pair the dashboard with improvement infrastructure. If the only thing the dashboard does is shame poorly-ranked hospitals, the result is pressure without help — which produces gaming, not improvement. Pair it with technical assistance, quality-improvement programs, and resources for hospitals at the bottom of the rankings. The dashboard's purpose should be patient information plus operational improvement, not just public exposure.

(11) Engage hospitals on definitions before publication. The hospitals being ranked know which definitions and methods are most likely to misrepresent reality. Consultation isn't capture; it's quality-control on the methodology. Disagreements that survive consultation should be disclosed.

(12) Recognize that gaming will happen. Every public-facing performance metric in healthcare produces some adaptation. The principle is to choose metrics where the adaptation either improves real outcomes (the desired behavior) or is detectable (so it can be addressed). Don't choose metrics where gaming is profitable and undetectable.

The right test: does the dashboard help a patient make a better decision about where to seek care? If yes, it's earning its keep. If it's mostly producing reports for headlines, it's a regulatory exercise dressed up as transparency, and it will eventually distort behavior in ways the patient bears the cost of.

Metrics should be understandable, clinically meaningful, risk-adjusted where appropriate, resistant to gaming, and accompanied by uncertainty or sample-size context. The dashboard should distinguish outcomes, process measures, and patient experience rather than combining them opaquely. It should avoid false precision in rank order when differences are trivial. The agency also needs to anticipate how public reporting may change provider behavior.

queries.prompt: A team argues that since its AI summarizer saves employees time, it should be rolled out immediately. What additional tests would keep the decision honest?

expected_answers.answer:
Additional tests to keep the deployment decision honest:

(1) Establish the counterfactual. 'Saves employees time' relative to what? The state without the AI? The state with better non-AI tooling? The state where the employee does a different version of the task that produces a different result? Without a clear counterfactual, 'saves time' is just a comparison to whatever's most convenient — usually the worst case.

(2) Distinguish time saved from value created. An hour of time saved on a low-value task is worth less than a half-hour saved on a high-value one. The question isn't just how many hours are saved; it's whether the freed time goes to higher-value work or is dissipated. In practice, time savings from individual-productivity tools often dissipate — the employee fills the time with the next available task, which may be similar to what they were doing before. Measure where the time actually goes.

(3) Quality of the AI output. Is the summarizer's output as good as the underlying source? If it omits things, gets things wrong, or shapes things differently than the source intended, the time savings come at a cost in fidelity. Sample outputs across cases and have humans rate them against the original, blind to which is the AI-generated version.

(4) Downstream effects on the work. Summaries get read instead of source documents. What's the impact on the quality of decisions downstream? If executives stop reading the underlying notes because summaries are good enough, do their decisions get better, worse, or unchanged? Decisions made on AI-summarized inputs may be faster but lower-quality, or faster and indistinguishable — the answer is empirical and matters more than the time-saving headline.

(5) Differential effects by user. Some employees will benefit enormously from the tool; some will work around it because their work doesn't fit it; some will use it badly and produce worse outputs than they would have without it. The average masks this heterogeneity. Look at the distribution.

(6) Skill atrophy over time. AI tools that handle a class of work can erode the skill associated with that work. New employees onboarded after the tool may never learn to do the work themselves. Some atrophy is fine (most of us don't manually balance our checkbooks anymore); some is costly (the loss of skill that lets you check the AI's work). Distinguish.

(7) Drift in output quality. The vendor updates the model; the model's behavior changes. Without monitoring, output quality can drift in ways that go undetected because the employees using it now trust it. Build measurement that catches drift.

(8) Adversarial use and failure modes. What happens when the input is unusual — an edge-case document, a politically sensitive topic, a multilingual source, content the model wasn't trained on? The pilot likely tested the common cases; the actual deployment includes the long tail. Test the long tail.

(9) Cost vs. savings. License cost, integration cost, ongoing tuning, change management, training. The all-in cost has to be compared to the realized time savings monetized appropriately. The team's enthusiasm for the tool doesn't mean the math works.

(10) Privacy, security, and compliance. Where does the data being summarized go? Is it appropriately handled? Are the employees who upload work using the tool aware of the disclosure surface they're creating? Compliance review should be part of the deployment decision, not an afterthought.

(11) Reversibility. If the tool stops working (vendor pricing changes, model degrades, regulatory issue), can the team revert? If employees have lost the habit or skill of doing the work without the tool, the deployment has created a dependency that has its own cost.

The shape of an honest evaluation: take the team's enthusiastic claim seriously as a hypothesis, design tests that could disprove it, run those tests, and decide based on results — not based on the qualitative feel of the people who built or use the tool. The decision should leave a paper trail that justifies expansion or limits scope; either is fine, as long as the basis is clear.

The team should test summary fidelity, omission of important caveats, whether users over-trust the output, whether the time saved translates into better throughput or merely more review elsewhere, and whether performance varies by document type. A time-savings claim is incomplete if errors create hidden downstream costs. Rollout should be gated by task-level quality and severity of mistakes, not speed alone.

queries.prompt: A company wants to move from annual planning to rolling quarterly planning. Analyze the main benefit, the main risk, and one operating discipline needed for success.

expected_answers.answer:
Main benefit of rolling quarterly planning: responsiveness. Annual plans, built once and executed for twelve months, accumulate gaps between plan and reality. Markets shift, customer needs evolve, competitive moves change the landscape, and the plan that made sense in November is increasingly mismatched by July. Rolling quarterly planning surfaces the gaps every three months and forces adjustment, which keeps the company aligned with current reality rather than with the picture from when the plan was written. The cumulative benefit is real — companies that practice rolling planning generally adapt faster to genuine change and ship more relevant work over time.

Main risk: planning churn replacing actual work. The danger is that quarterly planning becomes a process that consumes everyone's time without producing better decisions. The team finishes one quarter's plan, immediately begins the next quarter's planning, evaluates the prior quarter, presents to leadership, debates priorities — and the people who do the work spend their time in meetings rather than doing it. Worse, frequent re-planning can produce strategic incoherence: priorities shift before initiatives have a chance to land, the same projects get re-scoped and re-launched repeatedly, and the team loses the ability to commit to anything long enough to see results. Annual planning's deficiency was rigidity; quarterly planning's deficiency is volatility, and both extremes destroy value.

Operating discipline needed: the company has to distinguish between planning the work, doing the work, and reviewing the work. Quarterly planning works when (a) each cycle is bounded and time-budgeted — a fixed amount of leadership time, a fixed agenda, a clear decision output, with the rest of the quarter spent executing rather than re-discussing; (b) commitments have appropriate longevity — some work commits for the quarter, some for the year, some for the half. Not everything is re-evaluated every quarter. The longest-cycle work — infrastructure, multi-quarter product builds, strategic positioning — is committed at the year scale; tactics within those are re-evaluated quarterly. (c) Re-planning is triggered by material change, not calendar — the calendar is a check, not an invitation to revisit. If nothing material changed, the conversation is brief and the plan continues. (d) The output of each planning session is a small number of decisions and changes, not a complete re-doing. (e) Leadership commits to and defends decisions made at planning time, rather than allowing every operating discussion to reopen them.

Without that discipline, the company has 'rolling quarterly planning' as a name and continuous-planning chaos as a reality. With it, rolling planning is a meaningful improvement over annual.

The main benefit is adaptability: resources and priorities can be updated as market conditions change. The main risk is organizational churn, where priorities never stabilize long enough for execution. A necessary discipline is to separate strategic direction from tactical resourcing: the company can update near-term allocations quarterly while preserving a small number of durable annual choices. Without that discipline, 'agility' becomes indecision.

queries.prompt: A philanthropic fund wants to maximize 'lives improved' across very different programs. Why is this hard, and how should it proceed anyway?

expected_answers.answer:
Why this is hard:

The core problem is that 'lives improved' isn't a unit. Programs differ on the dimensions of life they affect (health, education, income, agency, safety), on the depth of effect per person (small improvement vs. transformation), on the time horizon over which effects manifest (immediate vs. decades), on the certainty of the effect (well-evidenced vs. hopeful), on whose lives are affected (counterfactually improved at the margin vs. lives that would have improved anyway), and on the moral weight that the funder assigns to each dimension. Comparing 'literacy improvement for a Bangladeshi village child' to 'malaria prevention in sub-Saharan Africa' to 'mental-health support for low-income teens in Brooklyn' requires converting each to a common unit that the underlying realities resist.

The field has developed approaches:

(1) Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) and Quality-Adjusted Life Years (QALYs) try to convert health benefits to a common unit. These work reasonably well within health and are increasingly used across health-adjacent areas, with the limit that non-health benefits don't naturally fit.

(2) Cost-effectiveness analysis with explicit weights. The funder makes its values explicit: how many DALYs of malaria prevention equal one extra year of education for a child equal one bed-net distributed. The weights are subjective; the discipline is in making them transparent and revisable rather than implicit.

(3) GiveWell-style evidence-rated comparison. Programs are evaluated on three dimensions: evidence strength (how confident are we that the program produces the outcome), cost-effectiveness (cost per outcome unit), and room for more funding (would another dollar produce another outcome). This avoids forcing all programs onto a single scalar by giving the funder a multi-dimensional view to weigh.

(4) Theory of change and milestones. Some funders forgo aggregate comparison and evaluate each program against its own theory of change — did the inputs produce the outputs, did the outputs produce the intermediate outcomes, did the intermediate outcomes lead to the ultimate impact. This produces internal evaluation but not cross-program ranking.

How to proceed anyway:

(a) Be explicit about values. The funder has a theory — implicit if not explicit — about what counts as a life improved and which improvements matter most. Making it explicit doesn't make it less subjective; it makes it visible and challengeable.

(b) Distinguish 'evaluating each program well' from 'ranking programs against each other.' The first is methodologically tractable; the second is methodologically contested. Be honest about which one you're doing.

(c) Triangulate. Where possible, look at multiple measures — quantitative outcomes, qualitative outcomes, beneficiary feedback, downstream signal — rather than reducing each program to a single number. The number gives the illusion of comparability; the texture preserves what the number flattens.

(d) Weight by the strength of the evidence base. A program with strong evidence of moderate impact may deserve more funding than one with weak evidence of huge impact. Risk-adjustment isn't pessimism; it's honesty about what's known.

(e) Allocate within program categories first, across them last. Compare ten health programs against each other; compare ten education programs against each other. Then make the harder cross-category allocation, but make it as a portfolio decision, not as a single-objective optimization.

(f) Recognize the limits of measurement. Some valuable programs produce diffuse, hard-to-measure benefits (civic engagement, social capital, cultural infrastructure). Avoiding them because they don't fit the framework is a choice. Including them at some weight is also a choice. Make the choice explicitly.

(g) Pre-commit to changing allocations only when evidence justifies it. The temptation is to chase the newest, most charismatic program. The discipline is to stay with programs whose evidence has held up, and to allocate marginal new funding to high-evidence high-impact opportunities.

The summary: the question can't be reduced to a single optimization without choosing — implicitly — a set of moral and empirical weights. The funder's job is to make those choices explicit, defensible, and revisable, and to allocate accordingly. The alternative — pretending the comparison is objective — produces decisions that reflect the analyst's hidden values dressed up as math.

It is hard because programs create different kinds of value: mortality reduction, income gains, educational improvement, dignity, resilience, and public goods that are not perfectly commensurable. Any single conversion factor embeds moral assumptions. The fund should still use comparable evidence where feasible, state its value judgments explicitly, separate high-confidence quantitative comparisons from qualitative ones, and test whether rankings are robust to reasonable changes in assumptions.

queries.notes: Requires cross-domain comparison under value pluralism.

queries.prompt: A factory manager proposes maximizing equipment utilization. Explain why this can reduce overall system performance and what to optimize instead.

expected_answers.answer:
Why maximizing equipment utilization can reduce overall system performance:

A factory is a system of stages. Each stage has its own throughput, variability, and constraint. When one stage's utilization is pushed to 100%, several things happen:

(1) The stage becomes the bottleneck. Throughput of the whole system is determined by the slowest stage. If every other stage runs faster and is forced to wait for this one, the system's output is the bottleneck's output. Pushing the non-bottleneck to 100% utilization just builds inventory in front of the bottleneck without increasing throughput.

(2) Queueing at the bottleneck explodes. When the upstream stage runs faster than the bottleneck can process, work-in-process inventory accumulates. Storage cost grows. Cycle time (from order to finished product) lengthens. Quality suffers because inventory sits — components corrode, exposure to handling errors increases, the wrong items get pulled by mistake.

(3) The system loses capacity to absorb variability. Real production has variation — material quality differs, machines need unplanned maintenance, operators are absent, demand spikes. A system at 100% utilization has no slack to absorb the variation. Every variation propagates downstream as a delay; recoveries become impossible because there's no idle capacity to use.

(4) Equipment fails more often. Machines run at higher utilization wear faster, have less time for preventive maintenance, and experience more breakdowns. The headline utilization rises temporarily; the long-term effective capacity falls as failure rate rises.

(5) Quality degrades silently. Pushing throughput often comes at the cost of inspection, calibration, and routine quality work. Defect rates rise. The defects produce rework downstream, which consumes capacity that should have produced new units. The utilization metric rises while the value-creating throughput falls.

What to optimize instead — drawing on the Theory of Constraints and related operations theory:

(1) Throughput of the bottleneck. Identify the slowest stage and maximize its output. Everything else in the system serves it — feed it, never starve it, never let it work on the wrong things.

(2) Subordinate the rest of the system. Non-bottleneck stages should run at the rate the bottleneck needs, not at their maximum. They should have slack — deliberate underutilization — to absorb variability. A stage that's 70% utilized produces the same throughput as one at 100% (because they're both feeding the bottleneck), but with much less queueing.

(3) Elevate the bottleneck. Invest in adding capacity at the bottleneck — additional shifts, equipment, training. The investment pays back because every unit of bottleneck capacity translates to system throughput; investment in non-bottleneck capacity doesn't.

(4) Manage flow, not utilization. The right operating metric is flow — cycle time, throughput, on-time delivery — not local efficiency. Equipment utilization, machine setup time, operator productivity at each stage are diagnostic information, not the goal. The goal is the system producing the right products on time at the right quality.

(5) Build buffers strategically. A small inventory buffer in front of the bottleneck protects it from upstream variability. Buffers elsewhere are waste. Identify the right buffer locations and size them deliberately.

The broader principle: optimizing local efficiency at each stage of a system is a category error. The system's value comes from its end-to-end flow, and pushing each local stage to maximum produces predictably worse end-to-end results than running each stage in a way that supports the overall flow. Plant managers who measure themselves by equipment utilization are sub-optimizing in a way that's visible in the wrong dashboard — and invisible in the customer's experience until the late deliveries, quality complaints, and inventory write-downs start arriving.

Maximizing utilization at every station can create excess work-in-process, longer queues, and delayed flow, especially near bottlenecks. A non-bottleneck machine working constantly may only pile inventory in front of the true constraint. The factory should optimize throughput, lead time, quality, and bottleneck flow rather than local utilization everywhere.

queries.prompt: A city wants to use predictive analytics to decide where to inspect rental properties. Identify the potential value and the fairness risk.

expected_answers.answer:
Potential value: a focused-inspection program serves more residents at lower cost. Predictive analytics — trained on inspection history, complaints, building characteristics, ownership patterns — can rank properties by predicted violation probability, letting limited inspector time concentrate on the highest-risk buildings. Coverage of substantive violations rises, operational cost per consequential finding falls, and tenants in dangerous properties get attention faster than under uniform-random sampling.

Fairness risk: the historical inspection data the model trains on is not a neutral record of where violations occur. It reflects where the city has chosen to inspect, where tenants have complained (which correlates with tenant resources and bureaucratic literacy), where past enforcement was concentrated. A model trained on this will recapitulate the historical pattern — and call it 'prediction.' If past inspections concentrated on lower-income neighborhoods, the model will direct future inspections there too, even if violations are equally distributed elsewhere. The disparity reproduces with a veneer of objectivity that's harder to challenge than discretionary decisions because 'the model said so.'

The deeper problem: 'violations found' is the outcome the model optimizes for, and that outcome is partly a function of where the city looks. A property in a wealthy neighborhood with the same actual housing conditions is inspected less, so violations there are recorded less, so the model learns those properties are lower-risk, so the model directs inspections elsewhere — self-reinforcing.

Mitigations that take the risk seriously: (1) Use ground-truth data, not historical-inspection data, for training where possible. Random-sample inspections across the entire housing stock produce unbiased ground truth, even if expensive. (2) Audit model outputs by neighborhood, income, race/ethnicity of residents. (3) Reserve a meaningful share of inspector capacity for random sampling, not model-directed targeting, to detect violations in areas the model doesn't predict. (4) Make complaint-based inspection independent of the model — every credible complaint is investigated regardless of model score. (5) Treat the model as decision support, not decision-maker; inspectors override and the override pattern is tracked. (6) Publish methodology and accept external audit.

The move is defensible if the city builds the safeguards. It's not defensible as a pure efficiency play that 'lets the data decide,' because the data is downstream of historical discretion.

The value is better prioritization of limited inspection capacity toward properties with higher likelihood of serious violations, potentially protecting tenants faster. The fairness risk is that historical complaint and enforcement data may reflect unequal reporting, selective scrutiny, or neighborhood bias, causing the system to reinforce disparities. The city should validate outcomes, test for disparate error patterns, retain human oversight, and provide transparent review mechanisms.

queries.prompt: A media company claims a new homepage layout 'improved engagement' because average session duration rose. Give alternative interpretations and a better test.

expected_answers.answer:
Alternative interpretations:

(1) Users got lost. The new homepage made it harder to find what users came for, so they spent more time looking. Time on site increased because the path to the desired content lengthened. This is the most common alternative for session-duration metrics that improve after redesigns — typically the one teams don't want to consider.

(2) Measurement window changed. Session boundaries are defined by inactivity, typically 30 minutes between actions. If the new homepage encourages more time on the landing page (slower loading, autoplaying content, more scroll) without users actually doing more, average duration rises mechanically without value increase.

(3) Selection effect. The new homepage may attract different users — better SEO bringing in more browsing-mode traffic and less goal-directed traffic. Browsers stay longer; goal-directed visitors come and go quickly. Audience composition shifts raise average duration without anything changing in user experience.

(4) Distribution shift. Average is the wrong summary statistic. A small share of long sessions can pull the average up dramatically; the median experience may be unchanged or worse.

(5) Pages per session declined. A 'better' homepage may hold users on a single page longer rather than encouraging navigation. Time on site can rise while pages-per-session falls.

A better test:

(1) Define an outcome metric, not a process metric. What does the company want users to do? Read articles to completion, sign up for newsletters, subscribe, return tomorrow, share? Measure those, not the proxy of time.

(2) A/B test new vs. old, with users randomly assigned, over several weeks for steady state.

(3) Measure outcomes: read-through rates on articles linked from the homepage, conversion to newsletter signup, return-visit rate within 7 days, share of session spent on actual articles rather than the homepage itself.

(4) Disaggregate by user type — new vs. returning, subscriber vs. non-subscriber, mobile vs. desktop. The new homepage may work for some and harm others.

(5) Examine the distribution, not the mean. What share of sessions are short, medium, long? Did short-session counts fall (users not finding what they wanted) and long-session counts rise (users engaged)?

(6) Qualitative review. Sample 20 sessions and watch them (session replay, with consent). The gut-check often reveals the difference between 'engaged' and 'lost' that aggregate metrics conflate.

The headline 'engagement rose' is a hypothesis, not a conclusion.

Longer sessions could mean readers found more valuable content, but they could also mean navigation became confusing or pages loaded more slowly. A better test would look at article completion, onward meaningful clicks, subscriber conversion or retention, return visits, and survey feedback, ideally in an A/B experiment. Engagement should be tied to user value, not interpreted mechanically.

queries.notes: Requires ambiguity diagnosis and experimental thinking.

queries.prompt: A manufacturer wants to outsource a component that is currently made in-house. Frame the decision beyond simple unit cost.

expected_answers.answer:
Frames beyond unit cost:

(1) Strategic differentiation. Is the component core to differentiation or commodity? A motor company outsourcing engines outsources what customers buy them for; outsourcing door panels outsources something fungible. Depends on whether the component carries differentiating value or is something the company manufactures only because it always has.

(2) Knowledge retention. Manufacturing teaches the company things — materials, tolerances, failure modes, supplier base, production economics. Outsourcing transfers that knowledge to the supplier and atrophies it internally. The cost is invisible at the time and surfaces years later when the company tries to redesign or negotiate with the supplier and finds it no longer has the institutional capacity to do either.

(3) Switching cost and supplier power. Once outsourced, the supplier accumulates leverage. The next price negotiation looks different — the in-house alternative no longer exists or would take years to rebuild. A single supplier may be the only viable option for a custom component, and they know it. Unit cost saved in year one becomes unit cost paid in year five.

(4) Quality and reliability. The supplier runs its own QC, which the company sees through audits but doesn't directly control. Supplier quality issues become customer-facing problems with a layer of indirection. Cost shows up in warranty claims, returns, brand damage — none of which is in the unit-cost comparison.

(5) Supply-chain risk. A single supplier of a single component creates a single point of failure. Multi-supplier strategies reduce risk but raise cost. Recent years have repeatedly shown supply-chain interruption can cost more than years of saved unit costs.

(6) Capital and labor. Closing in-house production frees capital (equipment sale, repurposed space) and labor (redeployed or reduced). One-time gains. Recurring savings must be compared against recurring loss of capability.

(7) Speed of design iteration. In-house manufacturing supports faster iteration — engineers walk to the floor, prototype quickly, change specs without renegotiating. Outsourced manufacturing introduces friction. For companies whose advantage rests on product evolution, this is consequential.

(8) Geopolitical and regulatory exposure. Supplier location matters. Politically unstable country, deteriorating relationship, export controls, tariffs — all add risk. Unit-cost comparisons that don't include these are systematically optimistic.

(9) Reversibility. Outsourcing is easier to do than reverse. Rebuilding in-house capacity — equipment, hiring, training, certifications — can take 2–5 years and cost more than the original capacity was worth. The asymmetry should be explicit.

(10) Total cost over 5–10 years. Compare in-house cost trajectory (learning gains, productivity, capital aging) to outsourced trajectory (supplier price changes, multi-sourcing premiums, switching costs, quality and supply-chain risk costs). The point comparison at year one is the most flattering moment for outsourcing.

For commodity components with stable demand and multiple capable suppliers, outsourcing is usually defensible. For differentiating components, thin supplier markets, or rapidly iterating specifications, in-house is often right even at higher nominal unit cost. 'Cheaper to outsource' is true at moment-in-time and often false over the relevant horizon.

The decision should include quality control, lead-time reliability, intellectual property exposure, supplier concentration risk, demand flexibility, capital freed, workforce implications, and the strategic importance of the component. A lower quoted unit cost may be inferior if it creates stockouts, hidden coordination cost, or dependence on a supplier with weak resilience. The firm should compare total landed and risk-adjusted cost, not purchase price alone.

queries.prompt: A nonprofit celebrates that program enrollment doubled, but completion rate fell sharply. What conclusion should leadership avoid, and what analysis should it perform?

expected_answers.answer:
Conclusion to avoid: 'enrollment doubled, so the program is succeeding.' Enrollment is the input; completion is the output. Doubled enrollment with sharp completion decline could mean (a) the program scaled beyond its capacity to serve participants well, (b) the second tranche was less prepared, motivated, or suited than the first, (c) the program lowered intake standards to drive numbers, (d) operational quality degraded under volume, or (e) outreach succeeded in reaching the right people but the program never worked as well as the early data suggested and the original completion rate was the unusual one. Most likely it's some combination.

Leadership should pursue:

(1) Disaggregate enrollment and completion by cohort. The doubled enrollment comprises cohorts — intake periods, recruitment channels, geographies. Look at completion within each, not as an overall average. The first cohort completed at rate X; the second at rate Y; the difference is the diagnostic.

(2) Compare the populations. Demographics, prior preparation, motivation indicators, baseline circumstances. If the new cohort is meaningfully different, the lower completion may reflect the population, not the program — meaning the program works for its original target but doesn't generalize.

(3) Examine the operational change. What did the program do differently to scale? New staff, new sites, different recruitment channels, simplified application, more participants per cohort? Operational changes typically correlate with quality changes. Trace which shifts map to completion decline.

(4) Investigate dropout reasons. Why did non-completers leave? Logistical (couldn't make it, schedule conflicts), motivational (lost interest), curricular (program didn't match), interpersonal (didn't fit). Reasons cluster, and the cluster points at the lever.

(5) Compare to a counterfactual. What would new enrollees have done without the program? Did the program create durable change for completers, regardless of the completion rate? If completers do well, the program may be valuable even at lower completion — just expensive per outcome.

(6) Cost per outcome. The original program produced X graduates at cost Y; the scaled program produces 2X enrollees and 1.4X graduates at cost 2Y. Cost per graduate may be similar, higher, or lower. Calculate.

(7) Decide what the program is for. Serve more people, even at lower completion? Or maintain the original completion rate as a quality bar? Both defensible; they imply different operational decisions. The board can't decide without knowing what they're actually trying to do.

The right action depends on the answers. If diagnosis is operational degradation under volume, fix operations. If misaligned recruitment, refine targeting. If the original rate reflected an unusually well-suited population that doesn't generalize, decide whether scaling is the right strategy at all. The wrong action: celebrate enrollment and hope completion comes back.

Leadership should avoid declaring unqualified success based only on enrollment. The program may have reached more people but served them less effectively, or it may have broadened access to harder-to-serve participants. Analysis should examine total completions, subgroup completion rates, reasons for dropout, capacity constraints, and whether the marginal entrants differ meaningfully from earlier cohorts.

queries.prompt: A company wants to benchmark its AI assistant against a frontier model using user preference votes alone. Explain what this misses.

expected_answers.answer:
User preference votes measure one thing: which output the user prefers, presented side-by-side, in the moment they're asked. Useful but incomplete:

(1) Preference doesn't measure truth. Users prefer the more articulate, confident, polished answer — even when wrong. Frontier models produce smoother prose; smaller models often produce blunter, less elaborate output that's more accurate in the specific case. Preference favors polish, not accuracy. For a customer-support assistant where wrong answers create real cost, critical blind spot.

(2) Preference is myopic. The user judges the answer in front of them, not downstream consequences. An answer slightly off but easy to act on may produce a worse outcome than one correct but harder to parse. Preference captures immediate reaction, not actual value.

(3) Preference depends on prompt framing. The same model can win or lose preference votes depending on how the question is asked, how much context is provided, what the comparison output is. Side-by-side may favor whichever model handled the specific phrasing better, not the one that would handle the actual workload better.

(4) Preference doesn't capture distribution of failures. A model that gets 95% right and catastrophically wrong on 5% may win more preference votes than one at 90% right and approximately right on 10%. The 5% catastrophic rate is a much bigger problem than preference suggests.

(5) Sample bias in who votes. Engineers who like AI vote more; cautious users participate less. Aggregate signal reflects the enthusiast, not the typical user.

(6) Preference doesn't include latency or cost. A user comparing two outputs side-by-side judges quality, not the trade-off against speed or cost. A smaller model that's 90% as good, 5× faster, and 10× cheaper may be right for many workloads — preference doesn't capture this.

(7) Preference rewards verbosity. Longer answers often win even when shorter ones would be more appropriate. A model that says less because the question only needs less may lose to a model that pads.

(8) Cross-task confounding. A single assistant is used for coding, summarization, support, drafting, analysis. Preference aggregates across these. The smaller model may win on most queries and lose on a few important categories, with the aggregate masking the failure pattern.

What's missing from complete evaluation: task-specific accuracy on benchmark queries with known correct answers; factual correctness graded against ground truth, not another model; failure-mode analysis — what does the model do badly, how often, with what severity; latency and cost-per-query alongside quality; calibration of confidence; distribution of outcomes across the actual workload, not just queries users chose to compare; long-term user behavior — did customer-resolution rates change, did escalations increase, did downstream metrics shift.

Preference is one signal worth having. It's not sufficient because it measures what users feel about an answer in isolation, while the deployment decision should turn on what the model produces across the full workload at the actual cost and quality trade-off.

Preference votes can capture readability and perceived helpfulness, but they often miss factual errors, unsupported reasoning, subtle omissions, and whether an answer satisfies the task under a hidden rubric. A cheaper model may sound just as good while quietly dropping critical details. The benchmark should pair blind preference with reference-based factuality, task success, severity-weighted failure analysis, and adversarial cases where plausibility is not enough.

queries.notes: Directly relevant to routing benchmark design.

queries.prompt: A logistics firm wants to reduce late deliveries by promising longer delivery windows. Is that improvement or metric manipulation?

expected_answers.answer:
Metric manipulation. The firm is changing the definition of 'on time' rather than improving operational performance. The same delivery that previously arrived 'late' against a 3-day window now arrives 'on time' against a 5-day window — but the customer is still receiving the package on day 4 or 5, which is the experience that matters.

Why this is manipulation:

(1) Customer experience hasn't changed. The package arrives when it would have arrived before. What's changed is the company's promise. The customer who waited four days is still waiting four days.

(2) The customer may have planned around the earlier promise. Some customers buy from this firm expecting 3-day delivery — time-sensitive purchases, holiday gifts, business needs. Widening the promise loses those use cases; the firm has shifted competitive position downward.
