AI may help assemble structured facts, locate policy language, and draft within tightly controlled templates after a human or rules-based process has made the underlying determination. The safer alternative is document assembly with constrained language and human accountability, not open-ended generative justification for adverse decisions.

queries.notes: Requires strong stance on a high-stakes use case.

queries.prompt: A logistics company wants to replace dispatchers with an optimization engine because routing software beats humans on average travel time. Diagnose what that misses.

expected_answers.answer:
What the average-travel-time metric misses:

(1) Variance and reliability. Optimization can achieve a better mean while increasing variance — some routes much faster, some much slower. Customers care about predictability. A delivery promised at 2:00 that arrives at 1:30 is good; one that arrives at 3:45 is a failure. Average improvement that produces more bad days is a worse customer experience.

(2) Real-time exceptions. Dispatchers make calls about what's happening right now — a vehicle broke down, a driver called in sick, a customer changed their delivery preference, a weather event blocked a route. Optimization engines operate on the plan; dispatchers operate on the day. Replacing dispatch with optimization means losing the layer that handles what wasn't in the plan.

(3) Driver and customer relationships. Dispatchers know which drivers can handle which routes, which customers need flexibility, which deliveries can be re-sequenced without complaint, which can't. This relational knowledge isn't in the data the optimizer uses. The optimizer produces 'efficient' routes that wouldn't pass the dispatcher's smell test.

(4) Long-term constraints. Drivers have hours-of-service rules, fatigue patterns, preferred routes, family obligations. Dispatchers manage these over time — not making this driver work the bad shift every week, ensuring rotation, accommodating personal commitments. Optimization can include these as constraints, but the dispatcher's flexibility to handle one-off situations isn't in the constraint set.

(5) Customer service. A delivery in trouble (driver lost, customer not home, package damaged) requires a real-time decision: re-route, return, hold, escalate. The optimizer didn't anticipate this; the dispatcher manages it in real time. Without the dispatcher, the resolution path is unclear.

(6) Communication. Dispatchers talk to drivers, customers, warehouse staff, customer service. Translation across these audiences, with the judgment about what to say to whom. Optimization software produces routes; it doesn't manage the human network.

(7) Edge cases compound. Optimization works well on the predictable middle of the distribution. Real operations have many edge cases — unusual customers, unusual products, unusual conditions. The dispatcher handles these; the optimizer struggles.

(8) Trust and accountability. Drivers and customers trust the dispatcher because there's a human accountable for decisions. Replacing with software changes the relationship. Drivers don't trust software the same way; customers don't either. The morale and customer-experience effects are real.

(9) Recovery from optimizer failures. Software has bugs, outages, integration failures, data problems. When the optimizer fails, what happens? Without dispatchers, the operation has lost the human muscle that recovers from system failures.

(10) Learning and improvement. Dispatchers notice patterns — this customer's deliveries always have problems, this lane is more difficult than the system thinks, this season has different dynamics. The patterns inform changes to procedures, training, or system configuration. The optimizer doesn't notice; it just produces outputs.

(11) Compliance and safety. Hours of service, weight limits, driver safety, hazardous-materials regulations — much of compliance is enforced by dispatcher attention. Optimizer-driven operations can be compliant in design but fail in practice when conditions change.

(12) Strategic relationships with customers. Dispatchers handle the conversation with the customer who's important to the business — the customer who needs a favor today, the customer who's about to give a big contract, the customer who's complained and needs special attention. Strategic flexibility lives at the dispatcher level.

What the right deployment looks like:

(1) Use optimization for the routing and scheduling math. Generate the proposed routes, handle the combinatorics, propose adjustments when conditions change. This is what software does well.

(2) Dispatchers review and modify. The dispatcher sees the optimizer's proposal, understands why specific routes were chosen, and adjusts based on context the optimizer doesn't have. Modifications are tracked; over time, patterns of modification inform optimizer improvement.

(3) Real-time event handling. The dispatcher is the operator who handles events as they happen. The optimizer supports them with re-optimization on demand.

(4) Customer and driver communication. Dispatchers handle the human side. The optimizer handles the routing math.

(5) Performance metrics that go beyond average travel time. On-time delivery rate, customer-reported satisfaction, driver retention, complaints, accident rate, compliance metrics. The optimizer's contribution is measured against the full set.

(6) Dispatcher productivity improves. With optimization handling the math, dispatchers can manage more deliveries with less manual planning. The role shifts from generating routes to managing exceptions and relationships. Span of control widens.

(7) Investment in dispatcher capability. With optimization handling the routine, dispatchers can be trained on higher-judgment skills — customer management, complex problem-solving, optimization-tool fluency.

The diagnostic: average travel time is a real metric, but it's a partial one. Replacing dispatchers with an optimizer is replacing a multi-functional role with a single-function tool. The replacement looks like a win on the measured metric and produces deterioration across the unmeasured ones. The right deployment uses the optimizer as a tool that dispatchers operate, not as a replacement for them. Companies that have tried the full replacement typically reverse course within 12–24 months as the costs of losing dispatcher capabilities accumulate.

Average travel time is only one dimension of dispatch quality. Dispatchers manage disruptions, driver constraints, customer promises, local tacit knowledge, fairness of workload, safety, and exceptions that the model may not represent well. An optimization engine can be powerful for route suggestions and scenario analysis, but replacement requires proving resilient performance in messy edge cases, not just better average routing on stable data. The better target is often dispatcher augmentation with explicit override and feedback capture.

queries.notes: Requires distinguishing optimization metric from full job.

queries.prompt: A university wants to use AI to screen scholarship essays. Assess the risk of optimizing for polish rather than merit.

expected_answers.answer:
The risk is significant: AI screening of scholarship essays optimizes for what the model can readily score, and the readily-scorable features tend to be polish — grammar, syntactic complexity, vocabulary, surface coherence, and apparent sophistication of structure. Merit, the thing scholarships are supposed to reward, is fundamentally a different construct: it includes capability, character, potential, drive, demonstrated achievement against odds, and authentic voice. Polish correlates with merit weakly; AI scoring polish strongly may select against many of the students scholarships exist to support.

Mechanisms of the construct-validity failure:

(1) Polish reflects resources, not ability. Students with access to writing tutors, college-prep programs, supportive English teachers, and parental editing produce more polished essays. The polish reflects the support system, not the student's underlying capability. Selecting on polish selects for the well-supported.

(2) Authentic voice is penalized. Students writing in their own voice — non-native cadences, dialect features, less practiced syntactic structures — score lower than students who have been coached toward a generic 'good essay' style. AI scoring rewards conformity to a model voice; merit may require divergent voice.

(3) AI penalizes AI-detectable styles. Essays that pattern-match to AI training data score either too generic (formulaic) or too unusual (low confidence in scoring). The model can't distinguish; both signals get conflated.

(4) Non-native English speakers. Documented bias against non-native writing — flagged as both 'AI-generated' (a different error) and 'less proficient.' These students are precisely the population scholarships often aim to support; the screening disadvantages them.

(5) Cultural narrative conventions. Different cultures structure narratives differently — the placement of the main point, the use of specific examples, the relationship between personal story and argument. Models trained on dominant-culture writing reward the dominant-culture conventions. Students writing in other conventions score lower.

(6) Adversity essays. Students writing about adversity often produce more emotionally raw, less polished work — the rawness is part of the authenticity. AI scoring conflates rawness with weakness. The students whose stories are most compelling for merit reasons score lower.

(7) Originality at risk. The most original essays — the ones with unusual framings, unexpected angles, unconventional structures — are out-of-distribution for the model. They may score as confused, unfocused, or unconventional. Conventional essays in the well-scored center of the distribution are rewarded.

(8) Iterative coaching produces better scores. Students who can run their essays through AI tools, get feedback, and iterate produce higher-scoring outputs. The students with this access also tend to be the better-resourced ones. The pre-screening becomes another stage of advantage compounding.

(9) Aggregate calibration vs. individual evaluation. AI scoring may have decent aggregate accuracy on a validation set, but individual decisions are what matter. A scholarship decision affects a real student. Aggregate accuracy doesn't license individual injustice.

(10) Loss of human judgment. Scholarship committees, at their best, identify students whose essays show character, growth, potential — judgments that require human reading and discussion. AI screening removes the candidates from human consideration based on shallow features. The students who would have impressed a human reader never reach the human.

What thoughtful deployment looks like:

(1) Don't use AI to screen out. AI may sort or organize for human review, but the binary 'this essay passes / doesn't' decision is human. The AI's role is to support the committee's process, not to gatekeep.

(2) Surface specific features for human review. The AI can flag essays that mention specific themes (first-generation status, adversity, leadership), that come from specific contexts, that have specific structural features. The committee uses this to organize, not to score.

(3) Calibrate against actual outcomes. Does AI scoring correlate with the student's eventual academic and career outcomes? If not, the scoring is invalid for the purpose. Most validation efforts find weak correlations; many find none.

(4) Audit for demographic and linguistic patterns. Are AI scores systematically different across groups in ways that don't reflect underlying merit? If so, the screening is biased and shouldn't be used.

(5) Use AI for processing, not judgment. Extracting structured information, organizing essays by topic, identifying logistical issues (length, completeness). Administrative support without evaluation.

(6) Train committees to recognize AI screening's limitations. If AI is part of the process, committee members understand what it can and can't do, and override appropriately.

(7) Transparency to applicants. Applicants know how their essays will be evaluated. The committee's process is public; AI's role is disclosed.

(8) Direct quality measurement. Do the students selected through the process succeed? Compare across selection methodologies, including ones that don't use AI.

(9) Equity monitoring. Track demographic, geographic, and linguistic distribution of award recipients. If patterns suggest the selection process is reproducing structural inequality, the process is failing its mission.

(10) Acknowledge limits. The university should be candid about what AI screening can and can't do. The marketing language ('AI-driven fair evaluation') is usually wrong; honest language ('AI assists committee organization') is more defensible.

The broader point: AI is a powerful tool for many evaluation tasks where the construct being measured is well-defined and AI features track it. Scholarship merit isn't that construct. The features AI can readily score correlate weakly with what scholarships are meant to identify, and the selection produces predictable bias against the students the scholarships often exist to serve. The university should not use AI as a screening filter for scholarship essays; using it as a backend organization tool, with human judgment at every decision point, is the sensible deployment.

AI scoring may reward fluency, conventional narrative structure, or style correlated with coaching and privilege rather than the qualities the scholarship intends to recognize. It can also penalize unconventional but authentic voices. If used at all, AI should not be a decisive merit judge; it might assist with administrative triage, formatting checks, or helping human reviewers manage volume. The institution should define the construct it values, validate scoring against that construct, and examine disparate error patterns. Essay polish is not the same as deservingness.

queries.prompt: A CFO wants to approve generative AI projects only if they show hard dollar savings within six months. Explain why this may be disciplined in one sense and shortsighted in another.

expected_answers.answer:
The 'six-month hard-dollar savings' criterion is disciplined in some ways and shortsighted in others.

Disciplined in:

(1) Forces specificity. Vague AI proposals ('we'll use AI to improve customer experience') don't pass the criterion. Proposals have to specify what process changes, what cost savings, what time horizon. Specificity is good discipline.

(2) Filters out AI-as-marketing projects. Many AI proposals are sponsored by enthusiasm rather than economics. The CFO's criterion forces the question 'what's the actual business case.' This filters out the projects whose justification is novelty.

(3) Creates accountability. The proposal that promises six-month savings has a measurable test. Six months later, the savings either showed up or didn't. The team that pushed the project is accountable for the delivery.

(4) Aligns with capital discipline. Companies have limited capital for new initiatives. Forcing each project to demonstrate ROI within a short horizon ensures that scarce capital goes to projects with demonstrable return. Especially during periods of cost pressure, this is rational governance.

(5) Avoids vague benefits. Soft benefits — 'employee productivity,' 'better decisions,' 'customer satisfaction' — are real but hard to verify. Hard dollars are verifiable. Requiring them prevents budget capture by projects whose value can't be measured.

(6) Tests for genuine business engagement. A project that doesn't have business sponsors who'll commit to the savings is a project the business isn't really invested in. The criterion surfaces this.

Shortsighted in:

(1) Most valuable AI projects don't pay back in six months. Real AI deployments have learning curves — initial deployment, evaluation, iteration, scaling, change management. The first six months are typically investment-heavy. Savings show up in months 9–18. The criterion rejects projects that would have paid back, just not on the requested timeline.

(2) Hard-dollar savings is one type of value. Revenue growth, risk reduction, regulatory compliance, capability building, customer retention, employee retention, strategic optionality — all are forms of value. Restricting to cost savings ignores the categories where AI often has higher leverage.

(3) Easy wins get prioritized. Projects that meet the criterion are typically incremental — automating a known cost line, reducing a specific labor element, simple efficiency plays. Strategic AI capabilities — the kind that build durable competitive advantage — typically don't meet the criterion and don't get funded.

(4) Capability investment is starved. Building enterprise AI capability requires foundational investments — data infrastructure, MLOps, governance, talent. None of these directly produces six-month hard dollars. They're prerequisites for projects that do, but they themselves get rejected.

(5) Risk aversion replaces strategy. The portfolio that emerges from the criterion is risk-averse: low-impact projects with quick payback. The high-impact, higher-risk projects that would change the company's competitive position don't get funded. Over years, the company falls behind competitors that took different bets.

(6) Encourages gaming. Project sponsors learn to estimate savings within the six-month window — even when the savings are speculative — to get approval. Post-hoc, the savings often don't materialize as projected. The criterion has produced the wrong projects.

(7) Customer-experience and revenue projects suffer. AI in customer service, sales enablement, product features — revenue and retention effects compound over years. Six-month measurement misses most of the value.

(8) Misses learning value. Some projects' value isn't the project itself but what the company learns. The first AI deployment teaches the organization things that the second and third can leverage. The first project may not pay back; the second pays back faster because of what was learned. The criterion punishes the first project.

(9) Forces premature scaling. Projects with longer-term payback get reshaped to fit the criterion — scaled before they're ready, deployed before they're tested, optimized for short-term measurement rather than sustainable value. The criterion produces deployments that look like wins at six months and reveal problems at eighteen.

(10) Conflates the project with the broader investment. A specific AI deployment may not produce six-month savings, but combined with other deployments, the platform investment, the talent build, and the operational learning, the total payback is positive. Project-by-project gating misses the portfolio view.

Balanced capital-allocation logic:

(1) Different criteria for different project types. Tactical cost-saving projects: short payback horizon, hard-dollar measurement. Strategic capability projects: longer horizon, capability and option-value measurement. Customer-revenue projects: revenue and retention impact measurement on appropriate timescale. The same standard for all produces a portfolio biased toward one type.

(2) Portfolio approach. The company funds a mix of project types. Some yield in six months; some in two years; some build platform value that doesn't directly yield. The portfolio's combined return is what matters.

(3) Stage gates rather than upfront ROI. Larger projects approved in stages — proof of concept, pilot, scale. Each stage has its own approval criteria. Early stages don't need six-month payback; they need to demonstrate learning and feasibility.

(4) Clear distinction between investment and operational expense. AI capability development is investment, evaluated like capital. Operational AI use is operating expense, evaluated on different criteria. Confusing the two produces bad governance.

(5) Honesty about soft benefits. Soft benefits exist but should be characterized, not quantified falsely. A project's case can include both hard-dollar and soft components, with the soft components described in operational terms (capability built, risk reduced, options preserved) rather than fabricated dollar values.

(6) Track-record-based discipline. As the company learns to evaluate AI projects, the criteria can be adjusted. Track records of projected vs. realized savings inform future approval. The discipline is empirical, not theoretical.

The CFO's instinct toward discipline is right. The specific criterion — six months, hard dollars only — is too narrow. A more sophisticated capital-allocation framework preserves the discipline (specificity, accountability, measurement) while expanding the lens to include the kinds of value AI actually produces. The disciplined company funds the right projects with the right governance; the shortsighted version of discipline produces the wrong portfolio with the right paperwork.

The discipline is useful because AI projects are prone to inflated narratives and pilots that never operationalize. Requiring measurable value prevents endless experimentation without accountability. But a blanket six-month hard-savings rule can underfund strategically important work: governance foundations, data readiness, workflow redesign, or customer-experience improvements whose value materializes later or partly through revenue and risk reduction. A better portfolio approach separates near-term efficiency bets from longer-horizon strategic or enabling investments, with different proof standards.

queries.prompt: A company wants to improve its semantic router by training on historical user prompts labeled with the model employees manually selected. What bias might that create?

expected_answers.answer:
The bias: training data labeled by the model employees manually selected will replicate the employees' selection patterns, including their biases, blind spots, and historical errors. The router learns to mimic past behavior, not to make optimal routing decisions.

Specific forms of bias:

(1) Selection by familiarity. Employees route queries to the model they've used most. The training data reflects familiarity rather than optimal fit. The router learns 'route to the familiar model,' not 'route to the appropriate model.'

(2) Cost-blind selection. Employees with no exposure to inference cost route freely to expensive models. The training data over-represents expensive-model selection. The router learns the expensive-model preference even when cheaper would suffice.

(3) Confidence overestimation. Employees may have over- or under-estimated the smaller models' capabilities. If they routed to the frontier model for queries the smaller model would have handled fine, the router learns to over-route. If they under-routed (using small models for hard queries), the router learns to under-route.

(4) Recency and trend effects. Employee selection patterns may have evolved over time — early in deployment, more conservative routing; later, more aggressive as confidence built. Training data that mixes these periods doesn't reflect any current operating policy; it reflects an average that nobody actually wants.

(5) Self-fulfilling prophecy. Employees who routed cautiously to the frontier saw good outcomes (the model handled the query). They didn't observe what the smaller model would have produced. The training data lacks the counterfactual. The router learns the cautious policy without seeing whether it was necessary.

(6) Population bias. Employees handle specific types of queries — the ones they happen to receive. The query mix in training data may not reflect production query distribution. The router learns to route the training mix well; it may handle the production mix poorly.

(7) Confident-but-wrong labels. Employees confidently selected models for queries where the selection was wrong (e.g., used a smaller model where the user actually got an inadequate answer that they accepted without complaint). The training data treats these as correct labels. The router learns to repeat the mistake.

(8) Demographic and content patterns. Different employees handle different query types. The selection patterns may correlate with the query types — and through them, with the demographic and content patterns of the originating users. The router learns biased associations that the manual selection wasn't intended to teach.

(9) Vocabulary drift. Employees label queries verbally, in chat, or through their selection actions. The labels reflect the language of the moment. Over months, the language drifts; the trained router becomes brittle to the new language.

(10) Adversarial reinforcement. If routing decisions are visible to employees (they see the model that handled their query), and employees adjust their queries to get the model they want, the training data reflects employee gaming rather than optimal routing.

(11) Coverage gaps. Some query types are rare in the employee selection data — either they didn't happen, or employees handled them outside the routing system. The router has no training signal for these. In production, it routes them unpredictably.

(12) Over-representation of certain workflows. Some employees route more queries than others. Their preferences dominate the training data. The router learns the most-active employees' policy, not the firm's optimal policy.

How to design around this:

(1) Label by outcome, not by selection. Don't train on 'employee picked model X.' Train on 'model X produced a sufficient answer to query Y.' Sufficiency is the target, not selection. This requires defining sufficiency and grading outputs, but it produces a router that learns the right thing.

(2) Use blinded outcome labels. The same query is run through multiple models; outcomes are graded by humans who don't know which model produced which output. The router is trained on these unbiased labels.

(3) Audit selection patterns for bias. Before training, examine the manual selections for the patterns above. If employees systematically over-selected or under-selected, the training data needs to be re-labeled or supplemented.

(4) Augment with synthetic data. Train on a representative query distribution (not just employee-selected queries), with outcome labels rather than selection labels.

(5) Cross-validation by query type. Make sure the trained router performs well across all query types in production, not just the ones in training data. Disaggregated evaluation surfaces coverage gaps.

(6) Decouple training from production behavior. After training, evaluate the router on a held-out set with outcome-based metrics. If it's just reproducing the manual selection pattern, the training failed.

(7) Cost-aware training. Include cost in the loss function. The router learns to balance cost against quality, not just to mimic selection.

(8) Periodic re-training with current data. Selection patterns drift; query distributions drift. The router needs to be retrained on fresh data; the original training is a baseline, not a final answer.

(9) Counterfactual logging. For some queries, run multiple models and observe outcomes. This builds the counterfactual data the manual selection didn't provide.

(10) Active learning on hard cases. Route uncertain queries to all tiers; observe outcomes; use these to improve the router. The router's areas of uncertainty are where the training data is thin; targeted data collection addresses this.

The core point: the obvious approach (label with what employees did) produces a router that's good at mimicry and bad at optimization. The better approach (label with outcomes) is more demanding but produces a router that learns the right objective. The dataset-design choice is consequential.

Those labels reflect user habits, availability of options, UI defaults, fear of low-quality answers, and organizational folklore about model capability. They do not necessarily reveal the minimum sufficient tier. Training on them may teach the router to reproduce over-routing rather than optimize it. A better dataset would include expert sufficiency labels and blind quality comparisons across tiers for the same prompt, so the router learns capability requirements instead of legacy user behavior.

queries.notes: Highly relevant to router benchmark design.

queries.prompt: A company asks whether AI-generated summaries of customer interviews can replace reading the transcripts. Give the honest answer.

expected_answers.answer:
Honest answer: no, AI-generated summaries of customer interviews are not an adequate substitute for reading the transcripts, though they can be a useful complement for specific purposes.

Why summaries are inadequate as substitute:

(1) Summaries compress, losing what mattered. Customer interviews contain detail — specific phrasings, hesitations, what the customer brought up unprompted, what they avoided, how they responded to follow-up questions. The summary captures the central thread; the substance is in the texture. A researcher who reads only summaries doesn't have the data to interpret what's being summarized.

(2) The summary reflects the summarizer's frame. AI summaries have implicit weighting — they emphasize certain types of content, structure information in particular ways, normalize toward common patterns. The same interview summarized by different models produces different summaries. The summary is one rendering of the content, not the content.

(3) Unexpected findings get smoothed away. Customer research is most valuable when it surfaces surprises — things that contradict the researcher's hypothesis, that don't fit the dominant pattern, that point to issues the company didn't know existed. Summaries tend to flatten outliers into the central narrative. The researcher who depends on summaries misses what they were running the research to discover.

(4) Quotation accuracy. A customer's exact wording often matters — the metaphor they used, the way they framed a problem, the specific words they chose. Summaries paraphrase. The researcher loses access to the linguistic data that's part of customer-research output.

(5) Emotional content. How customers feel about a topic — the energy, frustration, enthusiasm, indifference — isn't well captured in text summaries. Voice tone, pacing, hesitation are present in the transcript or recording but not in the summary. Emotional signal is part of the data.

(6) Follow-up patterns. What the interviewer did matters. Did they probe a topic? Did they accept a vague answer? Did they let the customer lead? The interviewer's choices shape what the customer said. The summary doesn't capture this; the transcript does.

(7) Cross-interview synthesis. Researchers find patterns by holding many interviews in their head — connecting what customer A said to what customer B said, noticing recurring themes, identifying tension between perspectives. The deep work is across the corpus. Summaries support shallow cross-interview pattern-matching; the deep version requires the full data.

(8) Calibration of what to take literally. Customers say things they don't fully mean, ask for features they wouldn't use, complain about issues that aren't actually important to them. Experienced researchers calibrate against these patterns by reading the full interview. Summaries lose the calibrating context.

(9) Misinterpretation propagates. An AI summary that gets a point slightly wrong becomes the basis of the researcher's understanding. Without reading the transcript, the researcher can't catch the error. Decisions get made on misunderstood data.

(10) The researcher's own thinking degrades. Reading transcripts is hard work. Researchers who do it develop intuition, vocabulary, and pattern recognition. Researchers who depend on summaries skip the work that produces the skill. The next research project is harder.

Where summaries help:

(1) Initial overview before reading. A summary lets the researcher know what to expect in the transcript, what to read for, what's likely to be there. The summary is a navigation tool, not a substitute.

(2) Cross-interview indexing. Summaries help locate which interviews are relevant to a specific question. The researcher reads the relevant transcripts in full; the summary directed them to the right ones.

(3) Stakeholder communication. People outside the research team (executives, product managers, marketers) often need digested versions. Summaries can serve as input to a researcher-curated synthesis for these audiences, with the researcher having read the underlying transcripts.

(4) Coding and tagging. AI can help with applying codes to transcripts, identifying themes, surfacing patterns. This is preliminary analysis; the researcher validates and interprets.

(5) Search and retrieval. Searching across many transcripts for specific topics, mentions, or patterns. Useful for analysis at scale.

(6) Mechanical extraction. Counting mentions of a feature, extracting demographic information, identifying when specific events happened. Tasks where the summary's compression doesn't lose signal.

What the deployment should look like:

(a) Summaries supplement, never replace. The researcher reads transcripts; summaries are aids for navigation, indexing, and stakeholder communication.

(b) Multiple summary types. Different summaries for different uses — short executive summary, themed summary, quote-rich summary, comparison summary. Each serves a specific need.

(c) Source linkage. Every claim in a summary links back to the specific moment in the transcript. The researcher can drill in to verify.

(d) Acknowledge limits. The team is honest with stakeholders about what the summaries can and can't do. Decisions made on summaries alone are decisions made on partial data.

(e) Quality review. Sample summaries against transcripts; identify systematic errors; calibrate the summarization process.

(f) Researcher capability investment. Train researchers to read transcripts efficiently. The skill is valuable; AI can support it but can't replace it.

The broader principle: customer research is qualitative analysis. The data is the customer's full voice; the researcher's job is to find pattern in that. AI summaries are compressions of the data, not the data itself. A research operation that runs on summaries is doing different work — faster, cheaper, less informative. Whether that's acceptable depends on what the research is for. For low-stakes confirmation, summaries may suffice. For deep insight, product strategy, or unexpected discovery, the transcripts are the deliverable. Honest framing for decision-makers: 'AI summaries help me work with more interviews and communicate findings; they don't replace reading the transcripts where the actual analysis happens.'

They can reduce triage burden and help analysts identify themes, but they should not be treated as a perfect substitute for raw qualitative evidence, especially when nuance, contradiction, emotion, and edge cases matter. Summaries compress; compression inevitably reflects judgment. For exploratory research, humans should read a purposeful sample of original transcripts, audit summaries against source material, and use AI as an indexing and synthesis aid rather than as a filter that silently decides what is important.

queries.notes: Requires understanding of qualitative analysis and compression risk.

queries.prompt: A regulator wants an AI system to explain why loan applications were denied. What is the difference between a persuasive explanation and a legally or operationally faithful one?

expected_answers.answer:
A persuasive explanation is one the loan applicant accepts. A legally or operationally faithful explanation is one that accurately represents the reasoning behind the decision. These can be the same; often they aren't.

The distinction:

(1) Faithfulness is about correspondence with the actual decision process. The decision was driven by specific factors (income, debt-to-income ratio, credit history, employment stability, collateral value, etc.); a faithful explanation describes which factors most influenced the outcome and how. If the model weighted income heavily and debt-to-income lightly, a faithful explanation reflects that. The explanation tracks the cause.

(2) Persuasiveness is about what the applicant accepts. An explanation that the applicant finds reasonable, that doesn't trigger appeal, that aligns with their existing understanding. Persuasive explanations are often simpler, more emotionally satisfying, and may reference factors the applicant can intuit ("your credit score was below our threshold") whether or not those were the actual drivers.

Why they diverge:

(1) Model decisions aren't directly explainable. Modern credit models use many features in non-linear combinations. The decision can't be reduced to 'X caused the denial' — it's a function of many features interacting. A persuasive explanation has to simplify; the simplification may not faithfully represent the model's reasoning.

(2) Faithful explanations sometimes confuse. If the actual cause is something like 'a combination of recent credit inquiries plus debt-to-income ratio plus the specific composition of credit accounts,' explaining this faithfully produces a response the applicant finds incomprehensible. The persuasive simplification ('your credit utilization was too high') is more satisfying but isn't the actual reason.

(3) Faithful explanations sometimes reveal what the model can't justify. The model may have weighted features in ways that reflect statistical patterns rather than substantive judgments. A faithful explanation that says 'people with your zip code and income profile have higher default rates in our data' is faithful but legally and ethically problematic. The persuasive explanation that hides this is easier; the faithful one creates problems.

(4) Disparate-impact and discrimination risk. Faithful explanations may surface that the model's decisions correlate with protected characteristics — through proxies, through training data, through historical patterns. The lender's legal exposure is greater for faithful explanation than for persuasive obfuscation. There's a regulatory incentive to keep explanations vague.

(5) Operational impact. Faithful explanations let applicants understand what they could change. 'Lower your debt-to-income ratio' is actionable. Persuasive explanations may not be actionable, leaving applicants without a path to address the denial.

(6) Appeal and contestation. Faithful explanations support contestation — the applicant can identify whether the model's reasoning was correct. Persuasive explanations don't. The lender that wants to minimize appeals prefers persuasive over faithful.

What regulators should require:

(1) Faithfulness to the decision process. The explanation must reflect the actual drivers of the decision, not a post-hoc rationalization that's easier for the applicant to accept. This is necessary for fair-lending compliance.

(2) Specificity. 'Your credit score was below our threshold' is too generic. 'Your credit utilization on revolving accounts was X%, above our threshold of Y%, which is the primary factor in this decision' is specific and actionable.

(3) Actionability. The applicant should know what they could change. Faithful explanations support this; vague explanations don't.

(4) Audit-friendly. The explanation should be reproducible — the same model on the same case should produce the same explanation. Different explanations for the same decision suggest the explanation system is not faithful.

(5) Coverage of contributing factors, not just top one. Decisions often have multiple causes; the explanation should reflect that. Reducing to one factor can be misleading.

(6) Distinguishable from rationalization. The model that decides and the system that explains should be designed so the explanation isn't a post-hoc story. Ideally, the explanation comes from interpreting the actual decision logic, not from a separate model trained to generate plausible-sounding explanations.

(7) Honest about uncertainty. If the model's decision was based on patterns the model recognized but can't articulate (typical of black-box models), the explanation should acknowledge this rather than producing false specificity.

(8) Subject to fairness review. Explanations should be reviewable for whether they reveal disparate-impact patterns. The lender can't hide behind 'the explanation says credit score' if the actual driver was protected-class-correlated.

(9) Disclosure of model behavior, not just specific decision. Applicants benefit from knowing how the model in general weights factors. Specific decisions are easier to interpret with that context.

(10) Limits on persuasive design. The explanation system shouldn't be optimized for 'reduces appeals' or 'increases applicant satisfaction.' It should be optimized for 'accurately describes the decision.' Designing for persuasiveness corrupts the function.

What the lender should do:

(a) Choose models with interpretability. Simpler models with explicit decision rules are more interpretable than black-box models. The trade-off with predictive accuracy is real but worth considering for high-stakes decisions.

(b) Invest in faithful explanation systems. SHAP values, counterfactual explanations, decision-rule extraction — technical methods for producing explanations that reflect model reasoning. These are more demanding than persuasive-text generation.

(c) Validate explanations against the model. Sample decisions; verify that the explanation matches the model's actual reasoning. Discrepancies signal a failed explanation system.

(d) Audit for disparate impact. Examine patterns in explanations by demographic group. Patterns that suggest different reasoning for protected classes are red flags.

(e) Don't trade faithfulness for adoption. If explanations have to be vague to be acceptable, the model should be different rather than the explanations dishonest.

(f) Provide structured contestation. Applicants who believe the decision was wrong should have a path to challenge it. The path should be effective, not just nominal.

The distinction matters because regulators are increasingly explicit about it. 'Explainable AI' that produces persuasive-but-not-faithful explanations is increasingly recognized as compliance theater. The faithful version is harder, but it's the only one that genuinely respects applicants and supports fair lending.

A persuasive explanation sounds plausible and coherent to a human reader. A faithful explanation accurately reflects the actual decision logic, the governing policy or model factors, and the specific applicant information used. In regulated decisions, persuasiveness without faithfulness is dangerous because it can manufacture comforting narratives detached from reality. The system should generate explanations only from traceable decision data and approved rationale structures, with validation that the text maps to the real basis of action.

queries.prompt: A business unit wants to deploy its own AI tool immediately, arguing central governance is too slow. Give a response that acknowledges the frustration but defends a sane control model.

expected_answers.answer:
Response that acknowledges the frustration and defends a sane control model:

'I understand the frustration. The pace of governance feels mismatched with the pace of opportunity, and waiting weeks or months to get clearance for a deployment that could be running in days is genuinely costly. The business unit's frustration is real, and the current process should be examined honestly to see where it's adding value and where it's adding friction without value.

That said, the response shouldn't be 'deploy without central governance.' It should be 'fix the governance to be faster and lighter-weight on the dimensions where speed is achievable, while preserving the controls that matter.'

Why central governance exists, and what would happen without it:

(1) Data exposure. Without central governance, business units sign individual contracts with AI vendors. Each contract has different data-handling provisions; some may have terms that send customer or employee data to places the company shouldn't be sending it. The company discovers this when a compliance audit, a breach, or a regulatory inquiry surfaces it — by which point the data is already exposed and the contracts can't be retroactively fixed.

(2) Vendor proliferation. Each business unit picks its own vendors, often duplicating capabilities. Five years later, the company has 47 AI tools, none of which work well together, all of which are paid for separately, none of which has consistent security or governance. Procurement leverage is lost; integration is impossible; switching is prohibitive.

(3) Inconsistent risk management. Business units have different risk tolerances and different abilities to assess AI risk. Some will deploy carefully; others will deploy recklessly. The reckless deployments create incidents that affect the whole company — reputational damage, regulatory exposure, legal liability — and the careful business units bear part of the cost of the reckless ones.

(4) No defense against vendor failure. Vendor goes bankrupt, gets acquired, changes terms, raises prices, has a security incident. The business unit that deployed locally has no fallback. The company has no leverage to negotiate with the vendor at the company level, because each business unit's contract is its own.

(5) Compliance fragmentation. Regulators expect the company to know what AI is deployed, how, with what data, against what risks. A company that can't answer those questions because deployment was decentralized is in trouble at the next audit.

(6) Cross-business-unit value lost. AI capabilities developed in one business unit could often benefit others — different problem, similar tools. Without central visibility, the cross-unit learning doesn't happen. Each unit re-invents.

(7) Reputational damage. An incident in one business unit becomes a company-wide story. The business unit that deployed quickly bears some blame; the central function that should have provided governance bears blame too. Both narratives are bad.

(8) Equity and bias propagation. Without central oversight, business units may deploy models that produce disparate impact. The pattern goes undetected because no one is looking across deployments.

(9) Customer experience inconsistency. AI-mediated customer interactions across business units have different tones, capabilities, and trust profiles. Customers experience the company as fragmented.

(10) Talent inefficiency. Without central capability, each business unit hires and trains its own AI specialists. The talent market for AI is tight and expensive; replicating capability across units is wasteful.

What sane control should look like:

(1) Tiered approval based on risk. Low-risk deployments (internal experimentation, well-bounded use cases, established vendors with approved terms) get rapid approval — days, not weeks. High-risk deployments (customer-facing, regulated, sensitive data, novel vendors) get more rigorous review. Risk-tiered governance is faster on average than uniform-rigor governance.

(2) Pre-approved vendors and patterns. Central governance maintains a list of approved vendors and deployment patterns. Business units using these don't go through a new review — they use the approved path. New vendors or novel patterns trigger review.

(3) Self-service for routine cases. Business units can deploy approved capabilities without per-deployment approval. The control is at the capability level (is this approved for this kind of use), not at the deployment level (do we approve this specific instance).

(4) Clear and visible criteria. Business units know what triggers review and what doesn't. The criteria are documented, not opaque.

(5) Response-time commitments. The central function commits to specific turnaround on reviews. Two weeks for low-risk; six weeks for high-risk. Predictability lets business units plan.

(6) Escalation paths. When central governance is too slow or wrong, business units have a way to escalate. The CEO or COO can override on legitimate business-need grounds, with documentation.

(7) Investment in the governance function. If the function is too slow, the answer is to invest in it (staff, automation, tooling) rather than to bypass it. The cost of investment is far less than the cost of incidents.

(8) Regular review. The governance framework itself is reviewed. What's working, what's slowing things down without adding value, what's missing. Adjustment over time.

(9) Cross-functional partnership. Central governance doesn't operate in isolation. It partners with business units to understand their use cases, propose paths forward, support their goals. The relationship is collaborative, not adversarial.

(10) Honest about trade-offs. The governance function acknowledges where it's slow and works to be faster. The business unit acknowledges where speed without controls produces risk. Both work toward a model that respects both.

The response to the business unit:

'Your frustration with the pace of governance is valid, and I'll work with you to address it. We can fast-track this deployment through a risk-tiered process: if it's using an approved vendor with an approved deployment pattern, we can clear it in days. If it's novel, we need to go through review, but I'll commit to a specific timeline and we'll work with you to address requirements in parallel rather than serially. Bypassing governance isn't an option, because the risks the central function is designed to address are real, and skipping it creates exposures that ultimately come back to you and to the rest of the company. Let's figure out the right path together.'

The substantive point: control isn't the enemy of speed. Badly designed control is. The response to the business unit's frustration is to make governance faster on the cases where speed is achievable, not to abandon it.

The frustration may be valid: centralized governance often becomes a bottleneck when it demands perfection before learning. But unmanaged deployment creates duplicated spend, security risk, inconsistent vendor terms, and failures that damage the whole enterprise. The answer is not no governance; it is tiered governance. Low-risk experiments should have a fast path with pre-approved tools and clear boundaries. High-risk or production use cases should face stronger review. Central teams should provide reusable guardrails, evaluation templates, and a service-level expectation for decisions so governance enables responsible speed rather than indefinite delay.

queries.prompt: A news organization wants to use LLMs to generate headlines. Design a policy that captures the efficiency while protecting editorial integrity.

expected_answers.answer:
Policy for using LLMs to generate headlines:

(1) Scope. The policy covers headlines for articles published on the news organization's properties (web, print, mobile, social media). Other AI-assisted content (research, transcription, image processing) is governed separately.

(2) Permitted use. Reporters and editors may use AI to draft, suggest, and refine headline candidates. The AI is a tool in the editorial process, not the final headline producer.

(3) Required human approval. No headline is published without human editorial approval. The reporter writes the article and proposes headlines (possibly with AI assistance). An editor reviews, modifies if needed, and approves the published headline. The chain of accountability is clear.

(4) AI involvement disclosure (internal). The CMS records which headlines involved AI assistance and at what stage. This is for internal tracking and post-hoc analysis, not for reader-facing disclosure on individual articles.

(5) Reader-facing disclosure (policy-level). The organization publishes a transparency statement explaining that AI tools assist in headline generation, with human editorial oversight. The statement is part of the organization's broader transparency commitments.

(6) Specific prohibitions. AI may not:

(a) Generate headlines for breaking news where verification is incomplete. Headlines on developing stories require editor judgment about what's confirmed; AI may produce headlines that sound confident on unconfirmed details.

(b) Generate headlines that name living individuals in negative contexts without specific editor review. The reputational risk is high.

(c) Generate headlines on sensitive topics (tragedy, conflict, vulnerable populations) without dedicated editor review. The tone calibration is too important to delegate to AI.

(d) Generate headlines that include numbers, statistics, or specific factual claims without verification against the underlying article. AI may hallucinate plausibly-wrong specifics.

(e) Generate headlines designed to maximize engagement at the cost of accuracy. The optimization function is editorial value, not click-through rate.

(7) Editorial standards apply. The same standards that apply to human-written headlines apply to AI-assisted ones — accuracy, fairness, restraint, freedom from misleading framing, respect for subjects. No relaxation of standards because AI was involved.

(8) Accuracy testing. Headlines are spot-checked against articles. Does the headline accurately represent the article's content? Discrepancies are addressed; patterns suggest training or policy adjustment.

(9) Diversity of voice. AI tools have stylistic biases. Over-reliance produces homogeneous headline voice. The policy includes attention to preserving editorial voice variation — different sections, different writers, different stories can sound different.

(10) Audience consideration. Headlines for the print edition may need different treatment than for the website, which may differ from social media. The policy is adapted to channel; AI use varies accordingly.

(11) Banned manipulation patterns. AI cannot be used to generate clickbait, deceptive teasers, headlines that contradict the article, or headlines designed to provoke partisan reaction. Editors are trained to recognize these patterns and adjust AI suggestions accordingly.

(12) A/B testing constraints. The organization may test multiple headline variants. Variants must all meet editorial standards. No 'most engaging headline wins' that ignores accuracy or framing.

(13) Reader complaint handling. Reader complaints about headlines are handled through the normal corrections process. AI involvement isn't a defense; it isn't an aggravation either. The headline is the organization's responsibility regardless.

(14) Periodic audit. Sample published headlines for accuracy, voice consistency, treatment of subjects, alignment with editorial values. Audit results inform policy adjustment.

(15) Tool governance. The specific AI tools used are approved by the technology and editorial functions jointly. Vendor terms, data handling, and security are reviewed. Changes to the tool stack go through the same review.

(16) Training. Reporters and editors are trained on the tool's capabilities, limitations, and the policy's requirements. New hires receive the training as part of onboarding.

(17) Feedback loop. Reporters and editors provide feedback on the tool's outputs — what works, what doesn't, what should be adjusted. The tool stack and the policy evolve based on the feedback.

(18) Bias and stereotyping. AI tools can produce headlines that reinforce stereotypes — gender, racial, national, religious. The policy explicitly requires attention to this; editors review for problematic patterns; the organization commits to address them.

(19) Style-guide consistency. AI tools should be configured (where possible) to follow the organization's style guide. Stylebook entries are part of the tool's prompt or fine-tuning.

(20) Special-event handling. During elections, crises, or other high-stakes periods, AI-assisted headline generation may be restricted or subject to additional review. The policy includes triggers for heightened scrutiny.

Governance:

(a) The policy is owned jointly by the editorial leadership (editor-in-chief or equivalent) and the technology/operations leadership. Neither owns it alone.

(b) The policy is reviewed quarterly for the first year, then annually. AI capabilities evolve; the policy must too.

(c) Editorial board reviews high-profile cases — controversial headlines, complaints, errors — and uses them to refine practice.

(d) The organization is willing to retract or adjust headlines, including AI-assisted ones, when warranted. Public corrections include the same disclosure standards as the original publication.

(e) The organization may publish, periodically, transparency reports on AI use in the newsroom — what tools, what proportions of work, what learnings, what changes.

The substantive principle: AI can capture real efficiency in headline production. The efficiency is captured well when AI is a drafting tool, editorial judgment remains primary, accountability is clear, standards are uniform, and the organization is willing to be transparent about how it works. The efficiency is captured badly when AI is treated as a substitute for editorial judgment, when standards are relaxed for AI-generated output, when AI is used to optimize for engagement rather than journalism, or when accountability dissolves into 'the AI did it.' The policy is what makes the difference.

The system can propose headline options, especially for routine formats and A/B testing candidates, but editorial staff should approve final copy. The policy should prohibit fabricated specificity, emotional inflation beyond the article's substance, and wording that materially changes the claim. Sensitive areas such as allegations, legal matters, health, and politics should receive stricter review. The newsroom should measure not only click performance but correction rates, reader complaints, trust metrics, and mismatch between headline and article. The efficiency is drafting speed; the protected asset is credibility.

queries.prompt: A company wants to quantify the value of faster internal decision-making from AI. Give a method that is more credible than asking executives for anecdotes.

expected_answers.answer:
More credible method than executive anecdotes:

Executive anecdotes have systematic problems — they're self-reported, attribute success to whatever was salient (often the new tool), suffer from confirmation bias (the executive remembers the cases that worked), and conflate the AI's contribution with other factors (the executive's experience, the team's capability, the timing of the decision). 'I made that decision faster because of the AI' often means 'I made that decision under time pressure with the AI in the room; absent the AI I'd have made the same decision in roughly the same time.'

Method:

(1) Define what 'faster decision-making' actually means operationally. Decisions are heterogeneous. Some decisions you want fast (operational responses); some you want slow (strategic commitments). The right metric is not 'time from question to answer' but 'time from need-for-decision to decision-effective,' for decisions of comparable consequence. Define the scope.

(2) Identify a decision type that's measurable and recurring. Examples: pricing decisions, escalation decisions, vendor selections, hiring decisions, budget reallocations. Pick a category with frequent instances so you can build a sample.

(3) Establish a baseline. Decisions of this type made before AI deployment. Measure: time from first identification of the decision-need to the decision-made (or to the decision being effectively executed). This is your baseline.

(4) Establish a comparison group. Decisions of this type made after AI deployment, by users who had AI access vs. users who didn't (if possible). The comparison isolates the AI's contribution.

(5) Measure decision quality, not just speed. Faster decisions are only valuable if they're not worse. Track outcomes — did the decision produce the intended result? Were there downstream problems? Did the decision-maker need to revisit or revise? Quality combined with speed is the actual measure.

(6) Identify the AI's actual role. Did it gather information? Surface options? Analyze trade-offs? Produce the recommendation? Just facilitate? The substantive role matters. 'AI played some role' is too vague to be credible.

(7) Cost the decision-making process. Time of the decision-maker, time of supporting staff, time of others consulted, cost of waiting on a decision, cost of reversing a bad decision. Total time and total cost — speed and cost together, not just speed.

(8) Compare against alternatives. AI is one option for accelerating decision-making. Better information systems, clearer decision rights, smaller decision-making bodies, pre-authorized templates — all of these affect speed. The AI's contribution should be compared to other interventions that could produce similar effects.

(9) Use a structured experimental design where possible. Some decisions can be randomized — AI assistance available for some decision-makers and not others, on otherwise comparable cases. Pre-registered design, transparent measurement, statistical analysis. Hardest to do but most credible.

(10) Use a difference-in-differences design where randomization isn't feasible. Compare changes in decision-time before and after AI deployment, for decision-makers with AI access vs. those without. Control for other changes happening at the same time.

(11) Survey decision-makers, but use specific questions. Not 'did AI help?' but 'on the most recent decision of type X, what specifically did the AI do?' and 'how would the decision have proceeded without the AI?' Specific questions yield more truthful answers than general ones.

(12) Capture decisions that didn't happen. Some value of AI is making it possible to consider decisions that weren't viable before — too data-intensive, too synthesis-heavy. The AI expanded the decision-making capacity; the count of decisions made may rise, not the speed of any individual one. Measure both.

(13) Look at organizational throughput. Are more strategic decisions being made? Are decisions being made at appropriate levels (lower in the hierarchy with better data)? Are decisions being executed faster after they're made? Speed of individual decisions is one of several outcomes that combine to organizational throughput.

(14) Tracking dashboard. Set up a dashboard that tracks decision velocity by type, with quality outcomes attached. Track over months. The trend is informative.

(15) Validate against business outcomes. Did the company achieve better operational, financial, or strategic outcomes after deployment? Decision-making speed should eventually show up in performance — if not, the speed gain wasn't meaningful.

What to avoid:

(a) Counting interactions with the AI as decisions made. Many AI interactions are exploratory; they don't lead to decisions.

(b) Crediting AI for decisions where the AI's contribution was peripheral. Be honest about the marginal contribution.

(c) Aggregating across decision types. The AI may help with some kinds of decisions and not others; the aggregate hides this.

(d) Using survey data alone. Self-report is biased; behavioral data is more credible.

(e) Optimizing for the metric. If 'time to decision' becomes a metric, decision-makers will declare decisions made earlier in the process. The measurement should be designed to resist gaming.

(f) Ignoring decisions that didn't happen because of the AI. AI may make some decisions easier; it may also make some decisions seem unnecessary, or postpone them. The full effect includes the avoided decisions.

A defensible quantification would look like: 'Within the category of pricing decisions, decision-makers with AI access made decisions in an average of 3.2 days, vs. 4.7 days for decisions of comparable scope made before deployment. Quality outcomes (revenue impact, customer retention, downstream issues) were comparable. The AI's role was to provide rapid analysis of competitive pricing and customer-segment elasticity. The 32% time savings, when valued at the cost of the decision-maker's time plus the value of faster pricing changes (estimated at $X per pricing cycle), produces an estimated $Y of annual value.' The number is specific, traceable, falsifiable, and tied to operating reality. That's what credible quantification looks like — and it's what executives' anecdotes cannot deliver.

Start with decisions that repeat and leave observable traces: pricing approvals, proposal reviews, incident response, contract triage, or research cycles. Define timestamps from request to decision, rework loops, number of handoffs, and quality outcomes after the decision. Compare pre- and post-AI periods cautiously, or use staggered adoption if possible. Interviews can identify mechanisms, but the value estimate should rest on measurable cycle-time reduction tied to decisions where delay has plausible economic cost. Otherwise 'faster decisions' becomes an attractive phrase without a defendable number.

queries.notes: Requires operationalization of an intangible value claim.

queries.prompt: A cloud vendor says its smaller model matches a frontier model on 'most enterprise tasks.' What evidence would make that claim credible?

expected_answers.answer:
Evidence required to make 'matches a frontier model on most enterprise tasks' credible:

(1) Define 'most.' 70%? 90%? Defined how — by number of tasks, by weighted importance, by volume of queries in production? Without specification, 'most' is rhetoric. The vendor should commit to a specific operational measure.

(2) Define 'enterprise tasks.' Customer support, code generation, document summarization, search, decision support, content moderation, contract review, data analysis, sales enablement, marketing copy — each is different. 'Enterprise tasks' is heterogeneous; the claim has to specify the scope.

(3) Define 'matches.' Identical outputs? Statistically indistinguishable quality? Within a defined tolerance? Differentiated on which dimensions? The matching standard is what makes the comparison meaningful.

(4) Define the frontier model. The frontier moves. Which model, which version, which deployment configuration is the comparison? A claim against a specific model from 12 months ago is different from a claim against the current state-of-the-art.

(5) Benchmark methodology. What benchmark was used? Standard public benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, etc.) have known limitations — leakage, distribution mismatch, single-turn evaluation. Enterprise-relevant benchmarks may not exist; the vendor may have constructed one. The construction details are critical.

(6) Task selection. Are the tasks representative of enterprise workloads? Cherry-picking tasks where the smaller model performs well produces inflated headline results. The selection criteria, the rejected tasks, and the distribution of task difficulty matter.

(7) Quality grading. How were outputs graded — by automatic metrics, LLM-as-judge, human raters? Each has biases. Single-method grading is suspect; multi-method grading is more credible.

(8) Adversarial and edge-case performance. The aggregate performance on typical tasks is one thing. Performance on adversarial inputs, edge cases, novel formulations, and the long tail is another. Smaller models often degrade more sharply on the tail.

(9) Multi-turn and context-handling. Many enterprise tasks involve sustained context, multi-turn interaction, integration with external data. Single-turn benchmarks miss this. Performance on full workflows, not isolated queries, is what matters for enterprise deployment.

(10) Distribution awareness. Average performance is a starting point; segment performance is the substantive question. Does the smaller model match the frontier on every segment, or does it match on most segments while failing on a few? The failure segments matter for enterprise deployment decisions.

(11) Confidence calibration. When the smaller model is wrong, does it know it's wrong? Frontier models often have better calibration on uncertainty. A smaller model that's confidently wrong is more dangerous than a frontier model with the same error rate.

(12) Replication by independent parties. Vendor-reported benchmarks are systematically rosier than independent reproductions. Credibility requires that third parties can run the same evaluation and get the same result. Provide the benchmark, the methodology, and the data so this is possible.

(13) Public availability. The benchmark and its results should be publicly accessible — published, documented, with the model weights or API access available for independent testing. Closed claims aren't claims; they're marketing.

(14) Production-relevant testing. Lab benchmarks don't always predict production performance. Has the smaller model been deployed in enterprise contexts? What did production users find? Reference customers willing to be specific about their experience are stronger evidence than benchmark numbers.

(15) Failure-mode disclosure. The vendor should describe where the smaller model fails. Specific failure modes documented and discussed are evidence of honest evaluation; absence of acknowledged failures is suspect.

(16) Cost and latency comparison. If the smaller model matches on quality, what are the cost and latency trade-offs? Sometimes the smaller model is cheaper but slower, or faster but with higher resource use. The full operational comparison is part of the case.

(17) Stability over time. Does the smaller model's performance hold up as the input distribution evolves, as edge cases accumulate, as adversarial pressure mounts? A snapshot at deployment isn't the same as sustained performance.

(18) Comparison to multiple frontier models, not just one. Frontier models vary in their strengths. Matching one frontier model doesn't mean matching another. The claim should be specific about which frontier.

(19) Differential analysis. Where does the smaller model lose to the frontier — what types of tasks, what kinds of queries, what user segments? The areas of loss are operationally important; sweeping them under 'matches on most' is misleading.

(20) Sustained engagement. The vendor should be willing to engage on follow-up questions — about methodology, about specific cases, about edge cases. Vendors who are evasive on specifics aren't presenting credible claims.

What's needed for the claim to be defensible:

A published, peer-reviewed (or at least independently reproducible) benchmark using a representative set of enterprise-relevant tasks, evaluated with multi-method quality grading by human raters, including adversarial and edge cases, on the current frontier model, with disaggregated results by task category, showing that on a specifically-defined subset of tasks the smaller model performs within a specifically-defined tolerance of the frontier. Reference customers with comparable production deployments confirming the performance in their use cases. Honest disclosure of where the smaller model loses.

What's typically presented: a few selected benchmarks, vendor-controlled methodology, aggregate scores without segmentation, no acknowledgment of failure modes, no independent reproduction.

The difference is the substantive credibility of the claim. An enterprise evaluating the claim should ask for the demanding evidence and treat the typical claim as marketing rather than substantive proof.

It would need a transparent task taxonomy, representative enterprise workloads rather than cherry-picked demos, blinded comparisons, clear scoring rubrics, confidence intervals or uncertainty where possible, and disclosure of where the smaller model loses. 'Most tasks' must be quantified: most by count, token volume, business value, or frequency? The vendor should show results by task class and failure severity, not a single aggregate. Credibility comes from visible boundaries, not from pretending the gap has disappeared.

queries.notes: Requires skepticism and standards for strong claims.

queries.prompt: A law firm wants to use AI to produce first drafts of contract redlines. Give a cautious deployment position.

expected_answers.answer:
Cautious deployment position for AI-generated contract redlines:

The deployment can be valuable but carries professional, ethical, and operational risks that require explicit management.

In-scope (phase one):

(1) Standard contract types. NDAs, vendor service agreements with established templates, standard employment agreements, common commercial contracts. Variability is low; deviations from template are identifiable; risks are well-characterized.

(2) Standard clauses within complex contracts. Limitation of liability, indemnification, governing law, payment terms, termination, IP assignment, confidentiality. These clauses appear consistently across contracts; identifying departures from standard is tractable.

(3) Specific issue identification. Flagging clauses that diverge from the firm's preferred positions, identifying missing protections, surfacing risks the contract assumes. The AI's role is detection; the lawyer's role is judgment.

(4) Drafting initial redline suggestions. The AI proposes redline modifications based on the firm's playbook (preferred language, fallback positions, deal-breakers). The lawyer reviews each, approves, modifies, or rejects.

(5) Comparison to template. Highlighting how the counterparty's draft differs from the firm's standard template. The AI extracts and presents; the lawyer interprets.

(6) Initial redline drafts for routine deals. Low-stakes deals with standard counterparties where the contract is mostly template anyway. The AI accelerates the first pass; the lawyer reviews quickly and engages substantively where the AI flagged issues.

Out of scope (phase one):

(1) Novel contract types. M&A documents, financing agreements, joint ventures, licensing of significant IP. These involve too much variability and stake for AI to handle reliably.

(2) High-stakes commercial deals. Where the contract is critical to the deal economics, terms are heavily negotiated, and the lawyer's substantive judgment is the central value-add. AI may support; it doesn't draft.

(3) Disputed or litigation-adjacent contracts. Where positions are adversarial, language matters enormously, and small word choices have legal consequences. AI's drafting may miss the strategic implications.

(4) Regulated contracts. Contracts in heavily regulated areas (healthcare, financial services, securities, government contracting) where compliance with specific regulations governs language choices. The AI may not have current knowledge of regulatory requirements.

(5) Cross-jurisdictional contracts. Different legal regimes have different requirements; AI may not handle multi-jurisdictional considerations correctly.

(6) Novel issues. New regulatory areas (AI, data, ESG, emerging technology), recent case law, evolving practice. AI's training may be out of date; the lawyer needs to bring current knowledge.

(7) Sensitive matters. Contracts involving privileged communications, confidentiality, or sensitive business strategy. The data handling implications of AI involvement may be problematic.

Governance:

(1) Human review of every output. AI doesn't produce a contract redline that goes to the client without lawyer review. The lawyer is responsible for the work product.

(2) Bar-association compliance. Many bar associations have specific requirements about AI use in legal practice — disclosure, supervision, competence. The deployment must comply with the relevant requirements.

(3) Client consent and disclosure. Clients are informed about AI use in their matter. Consent or opt-out is offered. The terms of engagement reflect the AI's role.

(4) Data handling and privilege. Where do the contracts go for AI processing? On-premise, vendor cloud, third-party API? What's the vendor's data retention? Is attorney-client privilege preserved? These have to be settled with the GC and IT before any client matter is processed.

(5) Vendor governance. The specific AI tool used is approved at the firm level. Updates to the tool are validated before deployment. Tool changes don't roll into production without re-validation.

(6) Audit and logging. Every AI-assisted work product is logged — input contract, model output, lawyer review, edits, final version. The audit trail supports compliance and quality review.

(7) Quality monitoring. Sample work products are reviewed by senior lawyers for quality, accuracy, and appropriate scope of AI involvement. Issues identified inform training and tool adjustment.

(8) Lawyer training. Lawyers using the tool are trained on its capabilities, limitations, and the firm's policies. New users are onboarded before they handle client work.

(9) Failure mode response. When AI errors are identified, the response is structured — immediate correction, identification of affected matters, root-cause analysis, policy adjustment. Errors are not buried.

(10) Periodic external audit. Independent review of the firm's AI use, by competent third parties. Results inform improvement.

Professional-responsibility considerations:

(1) Competence. Lawyers using AI tools must understand the tool well enough to supervise it competently. Blind reliance is below the competence standard.

(2) Confidentiality. Client information processed by AI must remain confidential. Vendor agreements, data handling, and access controls protect this.

(3) Privilege. Attorney-client privilege survives AI involvement only with appropriate handling. Inappropriate disclosure of privileged material to AI vendors can waive privilege.

(4) Supervision. The lawyer is responsible for the work product. AI is a tool; supervision standards apply to AI-assisted work as to junior-lawyer-assisted work.

(5) Disclosure. Some jurisdictions require disclosure to clients of AI use; others don't. The firm complies with applicable rules.

(6) Billing. AI-assisted work raises questions about billing — is the time saved billed to the client at the same rate? Are AI usage fees passed through? The firm's billing practices are transparent and ethical.

(7) Liability. If AI-assisted work product is wrong, the lawyer is responsible. Malpractice exposure depends on the lawyer's supervision and judgment, not on whether AI was involved. Insurance carriers may have specific requirements.

What to defer to phase two:

After phase one demonstrates the tool's value, reliability, and operational fit, scope can expand — to broader contract types, to higher-stakes matters, to more independent AI work. Each expansion is its own decision based on demonstrated performance.

The substantive position: AI in contract redlining can produce real efficiency and consistency gains in appropriate scopes. The risks (confabulation, scope creep, professional-responsibility issues, client trust) are managed through narrow phase-one scope, rigorous human review, clear governance, and explicit communication with clients. The firm that gets this right captures the value over time; the firm that deploys broadly without appropriate controls accumulates malpractice exposure, client trust erosion, and professional-responsibility problems that exceed the productivity gains.
