(9) Lower-confidence interpretation by the model. Constrained to brevity, the model may produce more hedged or vague answers. 'It might be A or could be B' is shorter than a thorough analysis of when it's A vs. B. The user has to do the analysis themselves.

(10) Reduced citations and grounding. Shorter answers cut citations to support the brevity. The user can't verify; trust falls; the user verifies elsewhere. The model's speed was paid for by the user's time.

(11) Misaligned with user expectations. Users who ask substantive questions expect substantive answers. A query that takes effort to formulate ('explain the trade-offs of architecture A vs. B for our use case') deserves a substantive response. A brief response feels dismissive; the user re-asks more verbosely, hoping for a real answer.

(12) Confused with refusal. The user can't tell if the model gave a short answer because the question was easy or because the model is hedging on a hard question. The ambiguity itself produces hesitation.

(13) Loss of helpful structure. Long answers can include structure — headers, bullet points, sections — that helps the user navigate. Shorter answers often lose the structure, making the dense remaining text harder to read.

(14) Cumulative effects in multi-turn conversations. A conversation built on shorter answers may require more turns to reach the same outcome. The product leader's metric (latency per turn) looks good; the user's total time is worse.

(15) Reduced trust over time. Users who repeatedly need to extract more from the model develop reservations. They may use the system less, or use it more cautiously, or default to verifying elsewhere. The long-term cost is engagement.

What 'actual task efficiency' should measure:

(1) Time from query to task completion. End-to-end, including any back-and-forth.

(2) Number of turns required to complete the task. Multi-turn requirements indicate the model isn't answering completely the first time.

(3) User behavior after the response. Did they act on it? Did they re-ask? Did they go to another source?

(4) Task completion rate. Of users who started a task, what fraction completed it?

(5) Quality of completed tasks. A faster task done wrong isn't efficient.

(6) Self-reported user efficiency. Do users feel the system helps them get things done?

(7) Specific failure mode: re-ask rate. Users coming back with a clarification or follow-up suggests the first response wasn't sufficient.

What reasonable shortening can do:

(1) Remove gratuitous verbosity. Models often pad with throat-clearing phrases ('certainly, I can help with that') that add latency without adding value. Removing these is genuine efficiency.

(2) Tighten unnecessary repetition. Restating the question, repeating points, summarizing what was just said. Reductions here improve speed without cost.

(3) Match output length to user-expected length. Brief queries with simple intent get brief responses; complex queries get substantive ones. Calibration is good; uniform shortening isn't.

(4) Reduce safety-theater. Excessive warnings, repeated disclaimers, unnecessary hedging. Some of this is appropriate; much of it is performance.

(5) Optimize for first-glance utility. The answer is structured so the most important information is at the top; users who need depth can read further. Front-loading is good; truncation is bad.

What the product leader should hear:

'Shortening responses can produce real efficiency gains where the length was verbose padding. But blanket shortening hurts task efficiency by removing context users needed, increasing follow-up turns, and degrading the user's ability to act on the response. The right optimization is calibrated length — shorter where shortening saves time without cost; longer where length serves the user's task. The metric that matters isn't response latency; it's task efficiency end-to-end, measured by completion rate, time-to-completion, follow-up rate, and user-reported helpfulness. If you optimize against the right metric, you'll sometimes shorten responses and sometimes lengthen them, and you'll produce a system that's faster for users to use — which is the goal — rather than just faster to generate output, which is an intermediate metric.'

The substantive principle: optimization metrics should reflect what users actually care about, not what's easy to measure. Response latency is easy to measure; task efficiency is harder but more meaningful. Optimizing the wrong one produces local wins on the metric and global losses on the outcome.

Shorter answers generate fewer tokens and can feel faster, but if they omit necessary reasoning, caveats, or next steps, users spend more time asking follow-ups or making mistakes. Task efficiency should be measured by time to satisfactory resolution, not only time to first completed response. The right answer length depends on task demand; a trivial lookup should be short, while a decision-support answer may need enough structure to prevent rework.

queries.notes: Requires distinguishing response latency from task latency.

queries.prompt: A company wants to score routed answers by textual similarity to the frontier answer. Explain why that is tempting and why it is flawed.

expected_answers.answer:
Why scoring routed answers by textual similarity to the frontier answer is tempting:

(1) Mechanically simple. Cosine similarity on embeddings, BLEU/ROUGE on text, edit distance. Easy to compute, easy to scale, no human judgment required.

(2) Reproducible. Same comparison produces same score. Different evaluations can be compared on a consistent metric.

(3) Anchored to a quality reference. The frontier model is taken as the standard; the routed answer's closeness to it is a proxy for quality.

(4) Computational cheap. Once embeddings are computed, comparison is fast. Evaluating thousands of queries is tractable.

(5) No need for ground truth. The frontier answer serves as reference; no human gold answers needed.

(6) Aligns with the deployment decision. The router's goal is to identify queries where the small model can substitute for the frontier; similarity-based scoring directly tests substitution.

Why it's flawed:

(1) Different right answers exist. Many queries have multiple valid responses. The small model may produce a different valid answer; the textual similarity to the frontier is low; the small model is judged inferior when it's actually equivalent.

(2) Surface similarity != quality similarity. Two responses can use similar words and reach different conclusions; two responses can use different words and reach the same conclusion. Embedding similarity captures lexical proximity, not substantive equivalence.

(3) Penalizes paraphrase. The small model that says the same thing in different words scores poorly. Paraphrase is a normal feature of good responses, not a quality problem.

(4) Penalizes brevity (or verbosity). If the frontier produces a 500-word answer and the small model produces a 100-word answer that captures the same key points, similarity is low. The small model may have been more efficient; the metric punishes it.

(5) Rewards verbosity if the frontier is verbose. If the frontier model tends to over-explain, the metric rewards small models that also over-explain. The user-preferred conciseness is penalized.

(6) Rewards mimicry of frontier style. The metric optimizes for sounding like the frontier, not for serving the user. Small models tuned to score well on similarity start producing frontier-stylistic outputs even when shorter or differently-structured outputs would be better.

(7) Frontier errors propagate. If the frontier model is wrong, similarity to it is wrong. The small model that gets the right answer differently scores poorly; the small model that gets the same wrong answer scores well.

(8) Frontier biases propagate. The frontier may have specific framings, omissions, or biases. Similarity-based scoring perpetuates them and selects against models that don't share them.

(9) Style differences mask quality differences. Two answers that say the same thing in different styles can have very different user impact. Similarity scoring may treat them as equivalent.

(10) Doesn't measure correctness. Embedding similarity doesn't know if the response is right. It only knows if the response uses similar tokens to the reference.

(11) Adversarial gaming. A small model can be trained to maximize similarity to the frontier without learning to answer well. The metric becomes the objective, and the objective is mimicry.

(12) Loss of dimensions. Quality is multi-dimensional (accuracy, completeness, clarity, tone, helpfulness, calibration). Similarity collapses these into one score.

(13) Misleading on edge cases. The metric is dominated by typical queries. On edge cases (which matter operationally), similarity-based scoring is even less informative — both models may fail; both may succeed differently; the similarity is uncalibrated.

(14) Doesn't predict user behavior. The metric correlates with user-perceived quality only roughly. Users may prefer a less similar response if it's clearer or more useful.

(15) Sensitive to artifacts. Punctuation, capitalization, formatting differences move similarity scores without affecting quality. Different models produce different formatting defaults that aren't substantive.

What to use instead:

(1) Rubric scoring by trained judges. The judges score outputs on defined dimensions; the dimensions reflect what users actually care about. More expensive but more valid.

(2) Pairwise comparison. Two outputs compared directly; the judge decides which is better, or rates them equivalent. Pairwise judgments are easier than absolute scoring and more sensitive to quality differences.

(3) Sufficiency evaluation. Did the response serve the user's purpose? Behavioral measures (user accepted, re-asked, escalated, abandoned) directly measure sufficiency, which similarity can't.

(4) Multi-judge LLM-as-judge with calibration. An LLM judge trained on human ratings can scale to many queries. Calibration against human raters ensures the judge isn't itself just measuring similarity.

(5) Distinct metrics for distinct dimensions. Accuracy (fact-checked against ground truth), completeness (rubric-based coverage), structure (defined criteria), tone (rubric or sentiment), calibration (predicted vs. actual confidence). Use multiple metrics; report each.

(6) Failure-mode-specific metrics. Hallucination rate, refusal appropriateness, off-topic rate. These can be measured directly without similarity comparison.

(7) User behavior in deployment. The strongest signal — what users do with the response. Re-ask rate, escalation rate, satisfaction, retention. Behavior is the truth-test.

(8) Counterfactual: would the answer have served the user equally well? Where possible, evaluate against user goals, not against frontier-style.

(9) Independent reference creation. If a reference is used, it should be created by independent qualified humans, not by the frontier model alone. Removes the self-reference problem.

Why this matters for the deployment decision:

(1) Routing decisions made on similarity-based scoring will be wrong on systematically the same kinds of queries — those where the small model takes a different valid path. The deployment will look successful on the metric and fail on user experience.

(2) Cost optimization based on similarity-based routing will be miscalibrated. Queries that look 'fine' (similar to frontier) may actually be inadequate for users; queries that look 'bad' (different from frontier) may be perfectly good. The metric guides poor decisions.

(3) The team running the evaluation will be confident in the wrong things. Believing the metric is informative produces complacency about routing quality. Discovering the metric is broken often happens after deployment, in production.

(4) The metric is gameable. Models tuned for similarity look great on the benchmark and behave worse than the metric suggests in production. The optimization corrupts the underlying capability.

The candid framing for the team: similarity-based scoring is appealing because it's cheap, but it's a poor proxy for what you actually care about. Use it as one signal among many — perhaps as a coarse filter for major divergence — but don't make routing decisions on it. The deployment-critical decisions require more substantive evaluation: rubric scoring by humans (on samples), behavioral measures in deployment, multi-dimensional quality assessment, failure-mode analysis. Spending more on evaluation infrastructure is the right trade against making routing decisions on a metric that doesn't measure what you need to know.

It is tempting because similarity is cheap to compute and seems objective. It is flawed because a lower-tier answer can be materially sufficient while using different wording, order, or level of detail, and conversely can mimic surface language while missing a key caveat. Routing evaluation should score semantic sufficiency and task success, not closeness of phrasing. Similarity may be a weak auxiliary signal for some bounded tasks, but it should not be the primary judge.

queries.notes: Directly relevant to planned downstream comparison.

queries.prompt: A legal department wants to let business users ask an AI whether a contract clause is 'standard.' What answer behavior would be responsible?

expected_answers.answer:
Responsible answer behavior for 'is this contract clause standard':

The AI is being asked a question whose 'right' answer involves legal interpretation. Standard answer behavior would either over-promise (give a confident verdict the AI can't actually deliver) or under-deliver (refuse anything useful, leaving the user without help). The responsible middle is:

(1) Provide context, not verdict. The AI describes how the clause compares to common patterns in similar contracts, what the clause is typically used for, what variations exist, and what considerations the business user should think about. The AI doesn't pronounce 'standard' or 'non-standard' as a verdict.

(2) Explicit scope. The AI tells the user what 'standard' could mean — standard in this industry, standard for this contract type, standard in the firm's prior contracts, standard relative to template language. The user understands that 'standard' isn't a single concept.

(3) Identification of unusual features. The AI flags specific aspects of the clause that diverge from typical patterns. 'This clause includes X, which is less common than alternative phrasings such as Y.' Specific observations are useful; binary judgments are not.

(4) Practical questions the user should consider. 'Does this clause give the counterparty rights you don't want to grant?' 'Is the carve-out broad enough for your typical use?' Helps the user think rather than substituting AI judgment for theirs.

(5) Honest about limitations. 'I can describe how this clause compares to common patterns; I can't tell you whether it's legally appropriate for your specific situation, what your firm's preferred position is, or what risks are most relevant to your matter. For those questions, please consult your legal team.'

(6) Pointing to qualified review. The AI doesn't replace legal review; it supports the business user's preparation for legal review. The user goes to legal with informed questions rather than blank ones.

(7) Source grounding where possible. If the AI is referencing standard contract language from the firm's playbook, public reference (e.g., ABA contract guides), or specific contract databases, it should cite those sources. The user can verify and dig deeper.

(8) Recognition of contract context. 'Standard' in an NDA is different from 'standard' in an M&A purchase agreement. The AI surfaces this and asks for context if not provided.

(9) Calibrated confidence. The AI's response is more confident on common patterns and less confident on edge cases. 'This is a common clause' is different from 'This appears to be a relatively unusual formulation; the legal team can confirm.'

(10) Useful flags. The AI may identify specific things worth highlighting to legal: 'This clause uses unusual termination language,' or 'This appears to give the counterparty a broader right than the standard market position.' Practical signal for the user's conversation with legal.

What the AI should not do:

(1) Pronounce verdicts. 'This is non-standard' or 'This is standard' as conclusions. The user takes this as authoritative; the AI's basis for the conclusion is weaker than 'authoritative' implies.

(2) Recommend specific changes. 'You should redline this to read X.' That's legal advice; the AI isn't a lawyer; specific drafting recommendations require legal judgment about the matter.

(3) Predict outcomes. 'This clause won't be enforced.' 'This will protect you in court.' Legal prediction requires legal judgment; the AI's prediction is unreliable and potentially harmful if relied on.

(4) Replace legal counsel. The AI should make clear that its observations don't substitute for the firm's legal team's review. The business user with a contract to sign needs lawyer input.

(5) Confabulate references. If the AI claims a clause is 'common in industry contracts,' it should be able to support this with reference to documented patterns. Fabricated assertions of standardness are dangerous.

(6) Mistake unusual phrasing for substantive divergence. Two different ways of saying the same thing may both be standard. The AI should distinguish surface variation from substantive variation.

(7) Treat the contract as standalone. Many clauses' meaning depends on the rest of the contract — definitions, cross-references, governing-law clause, dispute resolution. The AI should acknowledge that clause-level analysis is partial.

(8) Apply one jurisdiction's standards universally. 'Standard' varies by jurisdiction. The AI should note jurisdictional considerations rather than assuming one set of norms.

(9) Reveal sensitive contract content. The AI should treat the contract content as confidential. If the AI is hosted by a vendor, the data-handling implications matter. The legal department should approve the deployment configuration.

(10) Get distracted by adversarial inputs. Users may try to get specific legal advice through clever framings ('hypothetically, if I were dealing with this clause...'). The AI maintains the boundary regardless.

What governance supports this:

(1) System prompt and training. The AI's behavior on contract questions is shaped by the system prompt and any fine-tuning. The legal department reviews and approves the configuration.

(2) Boundary enforcement. The AI is monitored for outputs that drift toward legal advice; deviations are addressed.

(3) User disclosure. Users know what the AI can and can't do. The interface makes clear it's a support tool, not a replacement for legal counsel.

(4) Routing to humans. If the user's question goes beyond what the AI can responsibly answer, the system routes to the legal team. The escalation is fast and friction-free.

(5) Logging. AI interactions on contracts are logged. Patterns of inappropriate use (users seeking specific legal advice; AI providing it) are surfaced for review.

(6) Periodic review. The legal department samples interactions and reviews for appropriateness. Patterns inform training and policy.

(7) Confidentiality protections. Contract content processed by the AI is handled per the firm's data policy. Vendor agreements are appropriate.

(8) Audit trail. Every AI-generated analysis is traceable to its inputs, the model version, and any reference data. Useful for both governance and post-hoc validation.

Why this matters:

(1) Business users have a legitimate need for self-service contract understanding. They negotiate contracts, agree to terms, and operate under contractual obligations every day. They benefit from AI that helps them understand what they're signing.

(2) Legal departments have a legitimate need to manage exposure. AI-generated legal advice that turns out wrong creates malpractice exposure (for the firm) and bad outcomes (for the user). Bounded AI behavior protects both.

(3) The AI's role is augmentation, not replacement. Business users still need legal counsel for substantive matters; AI helps them prepare and ask better questions; lawyers continue to provide the substantive judgment.

(4) Confidence in the AI depends on its honesty about limits. An AI that pretends to give legal verdicts undermines trust when those verdicts are wrong. An AI that's honest about limits builds trust over time.

The substantive principle: 'is this standard' is a question whose responsible answer is descriptive, not verdictive. The AI describes patterns, surfaces considerations, flags unusual features, and directs the user to qualified review. The user gains useful context for their own thinking and for their conversation with legal. The boundary between helpful description and impermissible legal advice is held. The deployment is valuable; the risks are managed.

The AI should clarify the reference standard: standard relative to the company's playbook, a specific market practice dataset, or a generic expectation? It should identify the clause, compare it to approved positions if those are available, surface deviations, and avoid giving definitive legal advice when the source base is insufficient. A responsible response may say, 'This appears to deviate from our approved template in these ways; counsel should review.' The key is bounded comparison with provenance, not freeform legal judgment.

queries.prompt: A company wants to route product-review summarization to small models. What failure modes should the benchmark include beyond obvious sentiment mistakes?

expected_answers.answer:
Failure modes the benchmark should include beyond obvious sentiment mistakes:

Product-review summarization looks like a simple task, but several non-obvious failure modes affect production quality. A benchmark that catches only sentiment errors will miss the failures that actually matter for users.

(1) Aspect coverage. A review covers multiple aspects (product quality, shipping, customer service, value, fit). A summary that captures sentiment but misses specific aspects loses information users need. The benchmark should evaluate which aspects the summary surfaces, not just the overall valence.

(2) Specific complaint detail. Reviewers complaining about specific defects ('the seam came apart after one wash,' 'the battery dies within an hour') give specific information that's useful for purchase decisions. A summary that captures 'negative reviews' without preserving the specific complaints loses utility.

(3) Distinguishing severity. Some reviews mention minor inconveniences; some describe product failures. Summaries that flatten severity ('mixed reviews about quality') hide what matters. The benchmark should test whether the summary preserves severity distinctions.

(4) Frequency representation. If one issue is mentioned in 70% of negative reviews, the summary should reflect that prevalence. If the summary mentions five issues with equal weight when one dominates, it misrepresents the data. The benchmark should test for proportional representation.

(5) Differentiated user populations. Reviews come from different user types — first-time buyers, experienced users, gift buyers, users with specific needs. Summaries that treat all reviewer experiences as equivalent miss important context. The benchmark should test whether the summary preserves user-segment patterns.

(6) Edge cases and outliers. A small number of reviews may describe unusual but important issues (allergic reaction, safety concern, regulatory issue). Summaries that smooth these into the aggregate miss critical signal. The benchmark should test for outlier detection.

(7) Sarcasm and irony. 'Absolutely loved waiting 6 weeks for a defective product.' Sentiment models can mis-classify sarcastic positives as positives. The benchmark should include sarcastic reviews and test for correct interpretation.

(8) Mixed sentiment within reviews. A reviewer may love the product's design but hate its durability. Summarization that captures only the net sentiment loses the textured information. The benchmark should test for multi-aspect sentiment preservation.

(9) Comparison with previous versions or competitors. 'Better than V1 but not as good as the alternative from brand X.' Summaries that strip comparative context lose useful information.

(10) Conditional praise or criticism. 'Great if you have a small space; useless for larger rooms.' Summaries should preserve the conditions.

(11) Authenticity and credibility signals. Some reviews look like astroturfing (paid reviews, organized campaigns); some are obviously authentic. Summaries should ideally weight credibility, or at least not treat astroturfing as equivalent to authentic feedback. The benchmark should include known-astroturfed reviews and test the summary's handling.

(12) Update-relevant information. Recent reviews often discuss updates (firmware, design revisions, supply chain changes). Summaries that flatten timeline miss important recency information.

(13) Geographic and cultural variation. Reviews from different markets may emphasize different aspects (e.g., sizing differs by region; expectations differ by culture). Summaries should preserve regional patterns where they matter.

(14) Language and dialect handling. Reviews in different languages, dialects, or English varieties (UK vs. US, formal vs. informal) need handling. Summaries that systematically underweight non-dominant English speakers' reviews are biased.

(15) Honesty about uncertainty. Where review evidence is mixed, the summary should reflect mixed views, not produce a confident-sounding consensus where none exists.

(16) Specific number preservation. 'Most reviewers report battery life of 2-3 hours, less than the 8 hours advertised.' Specific numbers are useful; summaries that strip them lose information.

(17) Linkable details vs. unverifiable claims. Reviews making specific verifiable claims (product weight, dimensions, included accessories) are useful inputs to a summary. Reviews making unverifiable emotional claims are different. Summaries should preserve verifiability distinctions where possible.

(18) Detection of contradictory evidence. Different reviewers may make contradictory claims about the same feature. A summary that picks one and ignores the other misrepresents; a good summary surfaces the disagreement.

(19) Coverage of expected lifecycle. Some issues appear after long use (durability, support over time). Summaries based on early reviews miss these; the benchmark should test whether the summary acknowledges incomplete information about long-term performance.

(20) Spam and fake-review detection. Some reviews are obviously generated by bots or paid services. The benchmark should include known-fake reviews and test whether the summary excludes them or flags them.

(21) Privacy and identifiability. Reviews sometimes contain personal details (names, locations, specific personal circumstances). Summaries should generalize, not preserve identifying information.

(22) Bias toward extreme reviews. Reviews skew toward 5-star and 1-star; users with moderate experiences review less. Summaries that report 'mostly 5-star or 1-star' miss that the actual experience distribution includes more moderate cases. The benchmark should test for distributional honesty.

(23) Vendor-response handling. Some review systems show vendor responses to reviews. Summaries should distinguish customer voice from vendor voice, not blend them.

(24) Topic drift in reviews. A review titled 'great product' that is mostly about shipping complaints. A summary using only titles or first sentences would miss the actual content. The benchmark should test for content-based vs. surface-based summarization.

(25) Linguistic patterns of generated content. AI-generated reviews and astroturfed reviews share linguistic patterns. The benchmark can include synthetic generated reviews and test whether the summarization is robust to their presence.

(26) Calibration with rating distribution. Summary should correlate with the actual rating distribution. A summary suggesting overwhelmingly positive sentiment when the average rating is 2.5 stars is wrong; same for the reverse.

(27) Specific actionability. 'Most reviewers say sizes run small' is more useful than 'Mixed feedback on sizing.' The benchmark should reward specificity.

(28) Robustness to review-bombing. Coordinated negative reviews (or positive) shouldn't dominate the summary disproportionately. The benchmark should include coordinated-review scenarios.

(29) Adversarial summaries (the benchmark perspective). Can summaries be manipulated by inserting specific reviews designed to bias the summary? The benchmark should include adversarial inputs.

(30) Cross-review pattern detection. The summary should detect patterns across reviews that no single review surfaces — 'multiple reviewers complain about post-purchase support specifically.'

The benchmark should evaluate not just whether the summary captures average sentiment, but whether it preserves the dimensions of information that users actually care about when reading reviews. The deployment that catches sentiment well but flattens specifics, severity, populations, and outliers produces summaries that look reasonable and miss what users came to the reviews to learn. The benchmark that includes these failure modes is much more demanding than a sentiment-classification benchmark — but it reflects the actual operational task, which is what evaluation should test.

It should include rare but material complaints, mixed reviews with opposing pros and cons, sarcasm, evolving product versions, representative versus outlier themes, and cases where frequency matters more than emotional intensity. A model that captures general sentiment but misses a recurring safety issue or overweights a vivid outlier is not sufficient. Summarization quality should be judged on salience, proportionality, traceability to reviews, and omission severity.

queries.prompt: What is recursion? Give a one-sentence definition and an example.

expected_answers.answer: Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem; e.g., `factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1)` with base case `factorial(0)=1`.

Recursion is a technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller version of that problem, with a base case to stop the calls; for example, factorial can be defined as `n * factorial(n - 1)` until `n == 0`.

queries.prompt: Explain what a REST API is.

A REST API is a web interface organized around resources, such as users or orders, that clients access through URLs using standard HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE. RESTful designs are typically stateless, meaning each request carries the information the server needs to process it.

queries.prompt: Write a Python function to check if a string is a palindrome.

expected_answers.answer:
```python
import re

def is_palindrome(text: str) -> bool:
    normalized = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9]", "", text.lower())
    return normalized == normalized[::-1]
```
This version ignores case, spaces, and punctuation, so it correctly treats phrases such as `A man, a plan, a canal: Panama` as palindromes.

queries.prompt: Briefly explain Newton's three laws of motion.

expected_answers.answer: (1) An object at rest stays at rest, and one in motion stays in motion, unless acted on by a net force. (2) F = ma — net force equals mass times acceleration. (3) Every action has an equal and opposite reaction.

Newton’s first law says an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force. The second law states that force equals mass times acceleration, `F = ma`. The third law says every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force.

queries.prompt: What is the difference between '==' and '===' in JavaScript?

expected_answers.answer: '==' compares values with type coercion (e.g., `0 == '0'` is true). '===' is strict equality and also requires the same type (`0 === '0'` is false). Prefer '==='.

`==` performs loose equality and may coerce types before comparing values, while `===` performs strict equality and requires both value and type to match. For example, `0 == '0'` is `true`, but `0 === '0'` is `false`. In most application code, prefer `===`.

queries.prompt: Give 3 tips for writing a good cover letter.

First, tailor the letter to the role instead of sending a generic summary of your résumé. Second, connect one or two specific accomplishments to the employer’s stated needs, using evidence rather than adjectives. Third, keep it concise, usually one page or less, and close by expressing interest in discussing the fit.

queries.prompt: Explain what 'big O notation' is.

expected_answers.answer: Big O describes how an algorithm's running time or memory grows as input size grows, focusing on the dominant term and ignoring constants — e.g., O(n²) means roughly quadratic growth.

Big O notation describes how an algorithm’s resource use, usually time or memory, grows as input size increases. It focuses on asymptotic scaling rather than exact runtime, so an algorithm described as `O(n²)` grows roughly with the square of the input size in the large-input limit.

queries.prompt: How do I read a file line-by-line in Python?

expected_answers.answer:
Use a context manager so the file is closed automatically:
```python
with open('file.txt', 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.rstrip('\n'))
```
Iterating over the file object reads it lazily line by line, which is preferable to loading the entire file into memory for large files.

queries.prompt: What's the difference between TCP and UDP?

expected_answers.answer: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable — it guarantees ordered, error-checked delivery (e.g., web, email). UDP is connectionless and unreliable but fast and lightweight (e.g., video calls, DNS, games).

TCP is connection-oriented and prioritizes reliable, ordered delivery. It retransmits lost data, performs flow and congestion control, and is used for workloads such as web traffic, file transfer, and email. UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or duplicate suppression, which keeps overhead low and latency smaller. It is commonly used where timeliness matters more than perfect delivery, such as DNS, live media, and many multiplayer games.

queries.prompt: Write a Python one-liner to reverse a list called 'xs'.


queries.prompt: List the steps to change a flat tire on a car.

(4) Remove lug nuts and the flat tire. (5) Mount the spare, hand-tighten lug nuts. (6) Lower the car, then fully tighten lug nuts in a star pattern.

Park on level ground away from traffic, turn on hazard lights, set the parking brake, and place wheel chocks if available. Loosen the lug nuts slightly before lifting the vehicle. Position the jack at the manufacturer-approved lift point, raise the car, remove the lug nuts and flat tire, mount the spare, and hand-tighten the lug nuts. Lower the vehicle until the tire touches the ground, tighten the lug nuts in a star pattern, then lower it fully and recheck torque. Drive cautiously on a temporary spare and replace or repair the damaged tire promptly.

Write a Python function that performs binary search on a sorted list and returns the index or -1.

expected_answers.answer:
def bsearch(a, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(a) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if a[mid] == target: return mid
        if a[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1

queries.prompt: Explain the difference between a process and a thread, including memory and scheduling.

expected_answers.answer: A process has its own isolated memory space (code, heap, stack, file descriptors). A thread is a unit of execution within a process; threads share the parent process's heap and file descriptors but have their own stack and registers. Processes are heavier to create and isolate failures; threads are cheaper and communicate via shared memory but require synchronization to avoid races.

A process is an independent execution environment with its own virtual address space and operating-system-managed resources. A thread is a schedulable execution path inside a process; threads in the same process share memory and many resources but have separate stacks, registers, and instruction pointers. Processes provide stronger isolation, while threads are cheaper to create and communicate through shared memory more easily, at the cost of synchronization hazards.

queries.prompt: Write a Python implementation of merge sort.

expected_answers.answer:
def merge_sort(a):
    if len(a) <= 1: return a
    m = len(a) // 2
    L = merge_sort(a[:m]); R = merge_sort(a[m:])
    out, i, j = [], 0, 0
    while i < len(L) and j < len(R):
        if L[i] <= R[j]: out.append(L[i]); i += 1
        else: out.append(R[j]); j += 1
    return out + L[i:] + R[j:]

```python
def merge_sort(values):
    if len(values) <= 1:
        return values[:]

    mid = len(values) // 2
    left = merge_sort(values[:mid])
    right = merge_sort(values[mid:])

    merged = []
    i = j = 0
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:
            merged.append(left[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            merged.append(right[j])
            j += 1

    merged.extend(left[i:])
    merged.extend(right[j:])
    return merged
```
Merge sort runs in `O(n log n)` time and uses `O(n)` auxiliary space.

queries.prompt: Explain how a hash table handles collisions, covering at least chaining and open addressing.

expected_answers.answer: Chaining: each bucket holds a list (or tree) of entries that hash to it; insert/find traverse that list. Open addressing: collisions cause probing to other slots in the same array (linear, quadratic, or double hashing) until a free or matching slot is found. Chaining handles high load factors better; open addressing has better cache locality.

A hash table maps keys into buckets using a hash function. With chaining, multiple entries that land in the same bucket are stored in a secondary structure such as a list or tree. With open addressing, all entries remain inside the table array, and collisions are resolved by probing for another slot. Chaining is simpler under high load and supports straightforward deletion; open addressing often has better cache locality but degrades sharply as the table gets crowded.

For matrix `[[4, 1], [2, 3]]`, solve
`det([[4-λ, 1], [2, 3-λ]]) = 0`.
That gives `(4-λ)(3-λ) - 2 = λ² - 7λ + 10 = 0`, which factors as `(λ - 5)(λ - 2)`. The eigenvalues are `5` and `2`.

def hanoi(n, src='A', dst='C', aux='B'):

```python
def hanoi(n, source='A', target='C', auxiliary='B'):
    if n < 0:
        raise ValueError('n must be non-negative')
    if n == 0:
        return
    hanoi(n - 1, source, auxiliary, target)
    print(f'Move disk {n} from {source} to {target}')
    hanoi(n - 1, auxiliary, target, source)
```
The algorithm uses `2^n - 1` moves, which is optimal.

queries.prompt: Explain the CAP theorem.

expected_answers.answer: CAP says a distributed data system can guarantee at most two of: Consistency (every read sees the latest write), Availability (every request gets a non-error response), and Partition tolerance (the system keeps working when network partitions occur). Since partitions are unavoidable in practice, systems pick between CP and AP under partition.

The CAP theorem says that during a network partition, a distributed system must choose between strong consistency and full availability. Consistency means clients see a single up-to-date view of the data; availability means every request receives a non-error response. Partition tolerance is not realistically optional in distributed systems, so practical designs are often discussed as CP or AP under failure, though real systems expose more nuanced tradeoffs.

expected_answers.answer: The limit is 1. Geometric/Taylor argument: sin(x) ≈ x − x³/6 + ... for small x, so sin(x)/x → 1 as x → 0. (Or use the squeeze theorem with cos(x) ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1.)

queries.prompt: Explain garbage collection: mark-and-sweep vs. reference counting.

expected_answers.answer: Mark-and-sweep: from GC roots, mark all reachable objects, then sweep the heap freeing unmarked ones. Handles cycles but introduces pauses. Reference counting: each object tracks how many references point to it; freed when count hits zero. Low latency and incremental but can't reclaim reference cycles without extra mechanisms.

Reference counting tracks how many live references point to each object and reclaims it when the count reaches zero. It gives prompt cleanup but cannot reclaim reference cycles without extra machinery. Mark-and-sweep starts from a set of roots, marks all reachable objects, and reclaims everything unmarked. It handles cycles naturally but usually runs periodically and may introduce pause or scheduling overhead.

queries.prompt: Compute the gradient of f(x, y) = x² · y + sin(xy).

expected_answers.answer: ∂f/∂x = 2x·y + y·cos(xy). ∂f/∂y = x² + x·cos(xy). ∇f = (2xy + y cos(xy), x² + x cos(xy)).

For `f(x, y) = x²y + sin(xy)`, compute each partial derivative:
`∂f/∂x = 2xy + y cos(xy)`
and
`∂f/∂y = x² + x cos(xy)`.
Thus,
`∇f(x, y) = (2xy + y cos(xy), x² + x cos(xy))`.

```python
import requests

url = 'https://example.com/api'

try:
    response = requests.get(url, timeout=10)
    response.raise_for_status()
    payload = response.json()

    print(f'Expected nested value was missing: {e}')
```

queries.prompt: Explain TLS handshake at a high level (TLS 1.3).

expected_answers.answer: Client sends ClientHello with supported ciphers and a key share. Server replies with ServerHello (chosen cipher, its key share), Certificate, CertificateVerify (signed proof of private key), and Finished — all encrypted from ServerHello onward. Client verifies cert and signature, sends Finished. Both derive symmetric session keys via ECDHE from the exchanged key shares. 1-RTT vs the older 2-RTT in TLS 1.2.

In TLS 1.3, the client begins with a `ClientHello` that includes supported cryptographic parameters and an ephemeral key share. The server replies with a `ServerHello`, selects the parameters, provides its certificate and handshake authentication data, and both sides derive shared traffic keys from the key exchange. Once the handshake keys are established and verified, application data is encrypted. TLS 1.3 removes several legacy round trips and weak negotiation patterns from older versions, so a new full handshake usually completes in one network round trip.

So after 3 years, the account would hold about `$11,614.72`, assuming no additional deposits, withdrawals, fees, or taxes.

queries.prompt: Explain the difference between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, with one example each.

expected_answers.answer: Supervised: trained on labeled (input, output) pairs to predict outputs — e.g., spam classification. Unsupervised: finds structure in unlabeled data — e.g., k-means clustering of customers. Reinforcement: an agent learns a policy by taking actions in an environment to maximize cumulative reward — e.g., a game-playing agent like AlphaGo.

Supervised learning trains on labeled examples, such as predicting home prices from historical homes with known sale prices. Unsupervised learning searches for structure without labels, such as clustering customers into behaviorally similar groups. Reinforcement learning trains an agent through interaction and reward signals, such as learning a control policy for a robot or a game-playing agent.

Keeping terms through `x⁶` gives
`cos(x) ≈ 1 - x²/2 + x⁴/24 - x⁶/720`.

queries.prompt: Write a regex to validate an RFC 5322-lite email (simple version) and explain each part.

expected_answers.answer: ^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$ — local part allows letters/digits and . _ % + −; @ separator; domain labels allow letters/digits/hyphen/dot; TLD ≥ 2 letters. This is a pragmatic subset, not full RFC 5322.

A practical lightweight email regex is:
```regex
^[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$
```
The first class matches a simplified local part, `@` separates local and domain portions, the second class matches domain labels in a permissive way, and the final portion requires a dot followed by a letter-only top-level domain of at least two characters. This is useful for basic validation but does not fully implement RFC 5322, which is far more permissive and complex.

queries.prompt: Implement a simple in-memory LRU cache class in Python with O(1) get and put.
Explain index selectivity and when an index helps a query.

expected_answers.answer: Selectivity is the fraction of rows a predicate filters — highly selective predicates return few rows. An index helps most when (a) the indexed columns are highly selective for the query, (b) the predicate is sargable (e.g., uses =, IN, range on indexed columns without wrapping them in functions), and (c) the planner expects fewer rows than a scan threshold. For non-selective queries, a full scan is often cheaper than an index lookup + table fetches.

Index selectivity describes how well an indexed column narrows the result set. A highly selective predicate, such as `customer_id = 12345` in a large table, often benefits from an index because only a small fraction of rows match. A low-selectivity predicate, such as `is_active = true` when nearly every row is active, may not benefit because scanning the table can be cheaper than using the index and then fetching most rows anyway.

queries.prompt: Given P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A ∩ B) = 0.2, compute P(A | B) and tell me whether A and B are independent.

expected_answers.answer: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B) = 0.2/0.5 = 0.4. Since P(A|B) = P(A) = 0.4, A and B are independent.

Conditional probability is
`P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B) = 0.2 / 0.5 = 0.4`.
Since `P(A | B) = P(A) = 0.4`, events A and B are independent. Equivalently, `P(A)P(B) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.2 = P(A ∩ B)`.

expected_answers.answer: Separable: dy/y = 2x dx → ln|y| = x² + C → y = A·e^{x²}. y(0)=1 ⇒ A=1. Solution: y = e^{x²}.

Separate variables:
`dy/y = 2x dx`.
Integrate:
`ln|y| = x² + C`, so `y = Ce^{x²}`.
Apply `y(0) = 1`: `1 = C`, hence
`y = e^{x²}`.

queries.prompt: Explain how OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow works at a high level.

expected_answers.answer: (1) Client redirects user to authorization server with client_id, scopes, redirect_uri, and a state token. (2) User authenticates and approves. (3) Auth server redirects back with an authorization code. (4) Client exchanges the code (plus client secret) at the token endpoint for an access token (and optionally refresh token). (5) Client calls the resource API with the access token. PKCE adds a code_verifier/challenge to mitigate code interception on public clients.

In the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow, the client redirects the user to the authorization server with requested scopes and a redirect URI. After the user authenticates and consents, the authorization server redirects back with a short-lived authorization code. The client exchanges that code, usually together with a client secret or PKCE verifier, for access and possibly refresh tokens. The access token is then used to call the resource server on the user’s behalf.

queries.prompt: Write Python code that uses asyncio to make 10 HTTP GETs concurrently with aiohttp.

expected_answers.answer:
import asyncio, aiohttp
async def fetch(s, u):
    async with s.get(u) as r: return await r.text()
async def main(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:
        return await asyncio.gather(*(fetch(s, u) for u in urls))
asyncio.run(main(['https://example.com']*10))

```python
import asyncio
import aiohttp

URLS = ['https://example.com'] * 10

async def fetch(session, url):
    async with session.get(url, timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=15)) as response:
        response.raise_for_status()
        return await response.text()

async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        results = await asyncio.gather(*(fetch(session, url) for url in URLS))
    print(f'Fetched {len(results)} responses')

asyncio.run(main())
```
The requests are scheduled concurrently rather than sent one after another.

queries.prompt: Explain bias and variance in machine learning, and the tradeoff.

expected_answers.answer: Bias is error from wrong assumptions in the model — too simple, underfits (e.g., linear fit to a curve). Variance is error from sensitivity to training data — too complex, overfits (e.g., high-degree polynomial on noisy data). Total error ≈ bias² + variance + irreducible noise. Increasing model capacity usually reduces bias but raises variance; regularization, more data, and ensembling rebalance them.

Bias is systematic error from a model that is too simple or too constrained to capture the underlying relationship. Variance is error from a model that changes too much with the training data and therefore overfits noise. High bias often causes underfitting; high variance often causes poor generalization. The tradeoff is managed through model capacity, regularization, dataset size, and validation discipline.

queries.prompt: Write a Python class implementing a thread-safe counter using a Lock.

expected_answers.answer:
```python
from threading import Lock

class ThreadSafeCounter:
    def __init__(self, initial=0):
        self._value = initial
        self._lock = Lock()

    def increment(self, amount=1):
        with self._lock:
            self._value += amount
            return self._value

    def value(self):
        with self._lock:
            return self._value
```
The lock prevents race conditions when multiple threads read or modify the counter concurrently.

queries.prompt: Compute the inverse of the 2x2 matrix [[2, 1], [5, 3]].

expected_answers.answer:
For matrix `[[2, 1], [5, 3]]`, the determinant is `2·3 - 1·5 = 1`. The inverse of a 2×2 matrix `[[a, b], [c, d]]` is `(1/det) [[d, -b], [-c, a]]`, so the inverse is
`[[3, -1], [-5, 2]]`.

queries.prompt: Explain SQL JOIN types (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER) with a brief example each.

expected_answers.answer: Given A and B: INNER returns rows with matches in both. LEFT returns all rows from A plus matches from B (NULLs where no match). RIGHT mirrors LEFT (all from B). FULL OUTER returns all rows from both, with NULLs where one side has no match. Example: `SELECT * FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id` keeps employees with no department.

An `INNER JOIN` returns only rows with matches on both sides, such as customers that have orders. A `LEFT JOIN` returns every row from the left table plus matching rows from the right, so customers with no orders still appear with null order columns. A `RIGHT JOIN` is the mirror image, preserving every row from the right table. A `FULL OUTER JOIN` preserves unmatched rows from both sides. Example pattern:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id;
```

queries.prompt: Implement quicksort in Python with random pivot.

expected_answers.answer:
import random
def qsort(a):
    if len(a) <= 1: return a
    p = random.choice(a)
    lo = [x for x in a if x < p]
    mid = [x for x in a if x == p]
    hi = [x for x in a if x > p]
    return qsort(lo) + mid + qsort(hi)

```python
import random

def quicksort(values):
    if len(values) <= 1:
        return values[:]

    pivot = random.choice(values)
    less = [x for x in values if x < pivot]
    equal = [x for x in values if x == pivot]
    greater = [x for x in values if x > pivot]
    return quicksort(less) + equal + quicksort(greater)
```
Random pivot selection helps avoid consistently bad partitions on adversarially ordered input, though this version allocates extra lists rather than sorting in place.

Use the chain rule. The outer function is `ln(u)`, whose derivative is `1/u`, and the inner function is `u = x² + 1`, whose derivative is `2x`. Therefore,
`f'(x) = 2x / (x² + 1)`.

queries.prompt: Write a Python script that watches a directory for new files and prints their names (using watchdog).
Briefly compare TCP congestion control algorithms Reno and CUBIC.

expected_answers.answer: Reno uses AIMD (additive increase, multiplicative decrease) with cwnd growing linearly per RTT after slow start, halving on packet loss. CUBIC grows cwnd as a cubic function of time since the last loss, which scales better on high-bandwidth, high-latency links and is fairer when many flows compete. CUBIC is the default in Linux.

TCP Reno uses additive increase and multiplicative decrease, reacting primarily to packet loss as a congestion signal. It grows the congestion window cautiously and cuts it sharply after inferred congestion. CUBIC, used widely in modern Linux systems, grows the window according to a cubic function of time since the last congestion event, which makes it more aggressive and more scalable on high-bandwidth, high-latency links while still backing off after loss.

queries.prompt: Find all critical points of f(x) = x³ − 3x + 2 and classify them.

expected_answers.answer: Differentiate: `f'(x) = 3x² - 3 = 3(x² - 1)`, so critical points occur at `x = -1` and `x = 1`. The second derivative is `f''(x) = 6x`. At `x = -1`, `f''(-1) = -6`, so there is a local maximum; `f(-1) = 4`. At `x = 1`, `f''(1) = 6`, so there is a local minimum; `f(1) = 0`.

queries.prompt: Write a Python function that converts a Roman numeral string to an integer.
Compute the variance and standard deviation of [2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9].

expected_answers.answer: Mean = 40/8 = 5. Squared deviations: 9, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 4, 16 → sum 32. Population variance = 32/8 = 4; std dev = 2. Sample variance = 32/7 ≈ 4.571; sample std dev ≈ 2.138.

The mean is `5`. The squared deviations sum to `32`. Using the population formula, variance is `32/8 = 4` and standard deviation is `2`. Using the sample formula instead, variance is `32/7 ≈ 4.5714` and standard deviation is about `2.1381`.

queries.prompt: Implement Python code to memoize a function with `functools.lru_cache` and explain when it helps.
Compute the cross product of u = (1, 2, 3) and v = (4, 5, 6).
Explain consistent hashing and where it's used.

expected_answers.answer: Consistent hashing maps both keys and nodes to points on a circular hash space; each key is owned by the next node clockwise. Adding or removing a node only remaps the keys in that node's adjacent arc — typically ~K/N keys — instead of nearly all keys as in modulo-based hashing. Used in distributed caches (e.g., Memcached, Cassandra) and load balancers to minimize churn on topology changes.

Consistent hashing maps keys and servers onto a conceptual ring so that each key is assigned to the next server clockwise. When a server is added or removed, only a fraction of keys need to move, unlike simple modulo-based sharding that can remap nearly everything. It is used in distributed caches, partitioned key-value stores, and load-distribution systems where membership changes should cause minimal churn.

queries.prompt: Implement a Python function that returns all permutations of a list (without using itertools).

expected_answers.answer:
```python
def permutations(items):
    items = list(items)
    result = []

    def backtrack(start):
        if start == len(items):
            result.append(items[:])
            return

        for i in range(start, len(items)):
            items[start], items[i] = items[i], items[start]
            backtrack(start + 1)
            items[start], items[i] = items[i], items[start]

    backtrack(0)
    return result
```
This uses in-place swapping plus backtracking. For a list of length `n`, it returns `n!` permutations, so factorial output size is the real cost.

queries.prompt: A drug test is 99% sensitive and 98% specific. 0.5% of the population uses the drug. Given a positive test, what's the probability the person uses?

expected_answers.answer:
Use Bayes’ rule. Out of 100,000 people, about 500 use the drug and 99% of them, or 495, test positive. Of the 99,500 non-users, 2% false-positive, or 1,990, test positive. So among all 2,485 positive tests, only 495 are true positives.

\[
P(	ext{user} \mid +) = \frac{0.99 \cdot 0.005}{0.99 \cdot 0.005 + 0.02 \cdot 0.995} \approx 0.199
\]

The posterior probability is about **19.9%**, not 99%. The low base rate dominates.

queries.prompt: Explain pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference, and which one Python uses.

expected_answers.answer: Pass-by-value: a copy of the argument's value is passed; changes don't affect the caller. Pass-by-reference: a reference (alias) is passed; changes affect the caller's variable. Python uses 'pass-by-object-reference': the function receives a reference to the same object, so mutating a mutable object (list, dict) is visible to the caller, but rebinding the parameter doesn't affect the caller's binding.

Pass-by-value means a function receives a copy of a value, so rebinding or mutation inside the function does not affect the caller’s original object. Pass-by-reference means the function receives direct access to the caller’s variable or storage location.

Python is best described as **call by object sharing** or **pass-by-assignment**. A function receives a reference to the same object, but the local parameter name is its own binding. Mutating a passed-in mutable object can affect the caller; rebinding the parameter itself does not.

```python
def mutate(xs):
    xs.append(4)   # caller sees this

def rebind(xs):
    xs = [1, 2, 3] # caller does not see this
```

queries.prompt: Compute ∇·F for F(x, y, z) = (x²y, yz, z²x).

expected_answers.answer: Divergence = ∂(x²y)/∂x + ∂(yz)/∂y + ∂(z²x)/∂z = 2xy + z + 2zx.

For
\[
F(x,y,z) = (x^2y,\ yz,\ z^2x),
\]
the divergence is
\[

abla \cdot F =
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2y)
+
\frac{\partial}{\partial y}(yz)
+
\frac{\partial}{\partial z}(z^2x).
\]

That gives
\[
2xy + z + 2zx.
\]

So,
\[
\boxed{
abla \cdot F = 2xy + z + 2xz}.
\]

queries.prompt: Implement Python code to compute SHA-256 of a file in 64KB chunks.

expected_answers.answer:
import hashlib
h = hashlib.sha256()
with open(path, 'rb') as f:
    for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(65536), b''):
        h.update(chunk)
print(h.hexdigest())

```python
import hashlib
from pathlib import Path

def sha256_file(path: str | Path) -> str:
    digest = hashlib.sha256()

    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        while chunk := f.read(64 * 1024):
            digest.update(chunk)

    return digest.hexdigest()
```
Reading in 64 KB chunks avoids loading large files fully into memory.

queries.prompt: Briefly explain MVCC and how Postgres uses it.

expected_answers.answer: Multi-Version Concurrency Control gives each transaction a snapshot of the database at a point in time, so readers don't block writers and vice versa. In Postgres, each row update creates a new row version (tuple) with xmin/xmax transaction IDs; visibility is decided per snapshot. Dead tuples are reclaimed later by VACUUM.

MVCC, or multiversion concurrency control, lets readers and writers operate concurrently by keeping multiple row versions rather than forcing readers to block behind writes. In PostgreSQL, an UPDATE creates a new tuple version and marks the old version as no longer current for future snapshots. Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot according to its isolation level. Vacuum later reclaims dead tuples that are no longer visible to any active transaction. The payoff is high read concurrency; the cost is table bloat management and vacuum discipline.

queries.prompt: Solve: how many ways can the letters of 'MISSISSIPPI' be arranged?

expected_answers.answer:
`MISSISSIPPI` has 11 letters with repeats:
- M: 1
- I: 4
- S: 4
- P: 2

The number of distinct arrangements is

\[
\frac{11!}{4!\,4!\,2!}
=
\frac{39{,}916{,}800}{24 \cdot 24 \cdot 2}
=
34{,}650.
\]

So the answer is **34,650**.

```python
def parse_log_line(line: str) -> dict:
    parts = line.split()
    record = {
        "timestamp": parts[0],
        "level": parts[1],
    }

    for token in parts[2:]:
        key, value = token.split("=", 1)
        if key in {"status", "ms"}:
            value = int(value)
        record[key] = value

    return record


line = "2026-03-14T10:22:01Z INFO svc=api method=GET path=/users status=200 ms=12"
parsed = parse_log_line(line)
```

```python
{
    "timestamp": "2026-03-14T10:22:01Z",
    "level": "INFO",
    "svc": "api",
    "method": "GET",
    "path": "/users",
    "status": 200,
    "ms": 12,
}
```

queries.prompt: Write a Python function that returns the kth smallest element of a list using quickselect.
Solve: 3x² − 12x + 9 = 0.

expected_answers.answer: Divide by 3: x² − 4x + 3 = 0; factor (x − 1)(x − 3) = 0. Roots: x = 1 or x = 3.

Divide by 3:
\[
x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0.
\]
Factor:
\[
(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0.
\]
So the solutions are
\[
\boxed{x=1 	ext{ or } x=3}.
\]

queries.prompt: Compute ∫ x²·ln(x) dx.

expected_answers.answer: Integration by parts with u = ln(x), dv = x² dx → du = dx/x, v = x³/3. ∫ x²·ln(x) dx = (x³/3)·ln(x) − ∫ (x³/3)·(1/x) dx = (x³/3)·ln(x) − x³/9 + C.

Use integration by parts with
\[
u=\ln x,\quad dv=x^2 dx.
\]
Then
\[
du=\frac{1}{x}dx,\quad v=\frac{x^3}{3}.
\]

So
\[
\int x^2 \ln(x)\,dx
=
\frac{x^3}{3}\ln(x)
-
\int \frac{x^3}{3}\cdot\frac{1}{x}\,dx
\]
\[
=
\frac{x^3}{3}\ln(x)
-
\frac{1}{3}\int x^2 dx
=
\frac{x^3}{3}\ln(x)
-
\frac{x^3}{9}
+
C.
\]

Thus,
\[
\boxed{\int x^2\ln(x)\,dx = \frac{x^3}{3}\ln(x)-\frac{x^3}{9}+C}.
\]

queries.prompt: Write a Python function that returns True iff a number is a perfect square, without using sqrt.

expected_answers.answer:
def is_square(n):
    if n < 0: return False
    lo, hi = 0, n
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        sq = mid * mid
        if sq == n: return True
        if sq < n: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return False

queries.prompt: Compute the dot product and angle between u = (1, 2, 2) and v = (2, 1, −2).

expected_answers.answer:
The dot product is
\[
u \cdot v = 1\cdot2 + 2\cdot1 + 2\cdot(-2) = 2 + 2 - 4 = 0.
\]

Since the dot product is zero, the vectors are orthogonal. Therefore, the angle between them is

\[
\boxed{90^\circ}
\]

or
\[
\boxed{\pi/2 	ext{ radians}}.
\]

queries.prompt: Write a Python function that returns all subsets of a list (the power set).

expected_answers.answer:
```python
def power_set(items):
    subsets = [[]]
    for item in items:
        subsets += [subset + [item] for subset in subsets]
    return subsets
```
For `[1, 2, 3]`, this returns `[]`, `[1]`, `[2]`, `[1, 2]`, `[3]`, `[1, 3]`, `[2, 3]`, and `[1, 2, 3]`. A list of length `n` has `2^n` subsets.

queries.prompt: Given a coin with P(H) = 0.7, what's the probability of exactly 3 heads in 5 tosses?

expected_answers.answer:
Use the binomial formula:
\[
P(X=3)=\binom{5}{3}(0.7)^3(0.3)^2.
\]

That is
\[
10 \cdot 0.343 \cdot 0.09 = 0.3087.
\]

So the probability is **30.87%**.

queries.prompt: Implement a simple stack class in Python with push/pop/peek/is_empty.
Explain how Bloom filters work and what they're good for.

expected_answers.answer: A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure: a bit array of m bits and k independent hash functions. To add an element, hash it k times and set those bits. To query, hash and check whether all k bits are set — if any bit is 0, the element is definitely absent; if all are 1, it's probably present (false positives possible, no false negatives). Good for membership tests when you can tolerate false positives and want very small memory: web caches, malicious-URL lists, dedup of streaming data. Tunable: m and k trade space against false-positive rate.

A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic set. To add an item, it hashes the item several ways and sets the corresponding bits in a bit array. To test membership, it checks whether all those bits are set. If any bit is unset, the item is definitely absent; if all are set, the item may be present. Bloom filters are useful when fast negative lookups matter and false positives are acceptable, such as cache admission, database existence prechecks, and network filtering.

queries.prompt: Compute the convolution of u = [1, 2, 3] with v = [0, 1, 0.5] (discrete, full output).
Prove by induction that 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2 for all integers n ≥ 1.

expected_answers.answer: Base case n=1: LHS = 1; RHS = 1·2/2 = 1 ✓. Inductive step: assume 1+...+k = k(k+1)/2. Then 1+...+k+(k+1) = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k/2 + 1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2, matching the formula for n=k+1. By induction the formula holds for all n ≥ 1. □

We prove by induction that
\[
1+2+\cdots+n=\frac{n(n+1)}{2}.
\]

**Base case:** For `n=1`,
\[
1=\frac{1\cdot2}{2}=1.
\]

**Inductive step:** Assume the formula holds for `n=k`:
\[
1+2+\cdots+k=\frac{k(k+1)}{2}.
\]

Then for `k+1`:
\[
1+2+\cdots+k+(k+1)
=
\frac{k(k+1)}{2}+(k+1)
\]
\[
=
(k+1)\left(\frac{k}{2}+1ight)
=
\frac{(k+1)(k+2)}{2}.
\]

That is exactly the formula with `n=k+1`. Therefore, by induction, the identity holds for all integers `n≥1`.

queries.prompt: Design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Outline data model, key sizing, hot path, and how you'd scale to 10B URLs.

expected_answers.answer: Data model: urls(short_key PK, long_url, owner_id, created_at, expires_at, click_count); counters in Redis or separate table. Short key: base62 over a 7-char space (~3.5T values) is sufficient; use a centralized counter (Snowflake-style ID then base62-encode) to avoid collisions, or random keys with conflict-retry. Hot path: GET /:key → Redis lookup → 301 to long_url; async-increment click counter via a queue to avoid write amplification on hot keys. Storage: ~200 bytes/row × 10B = ~2 TB → shard by hash(short_key) across N shards. CDN/edge-cache for very hot keys. Background jobs for analytics aggregation and expiry. Add abuse signals (rate limits, malware checks) before issuing keys.

A URL shortener needs two core mappings: `short_code -> long_url` for redirects and, optionally, `long_url -> short_code` for deduplication. A practical relational or key-value schema is:

- `short_code` primary key
- `long_url`
- `created_at`
- `owner_id`
- `expires_at`
- `status`
- optional analytics fields stored separately

For 10 billion URLs, Base62 encoding needs 6 characters for about 56.8 billion possibilities, but 7–8 characters is safer once you account for reserved words, growth, and operational headroom. Generate IDs either by allocating numeric IDs and Base62-encoding them or by using random codes with collision checks. Sequential IDs are simpler but leak volume patterns unless obfuscated.

The hot path is redirect lookup:
1. Receive short code.
2. Check in-memory or distributed cache.
3. Fall back to durable store.
4. Return `301` or `302`.
5. Emit click analytics asynchronously.

At 10B URLs, scale reads aggressively with cache, sharding by short code hash, and replicated storage. Keep redirect serving stateless. Analytics should be append-only and asynchronous so they do not slow redirects. The most common mistake is overengineering the write path while underestimating read-heavy redirect traffic and cache behavior.

queries.prompt: Walk through how you'd debug intermittent 502 errors from a production service that talks to a Postgres database.

expected_answers.answer: (1) Scope: is it tied to traffic, time of day, a specific endpoint, or a deploy? Pull error rate by route and instance. (2) Correlate: Postgres logs for connection refusals, deadlocks, statement timeouts; service logs for upstream errors. (3) Connection pool: pool exhaustion? Look for long-running queries holding connections. (4) Network: TCP RSTs, NAT/idle-timeout drops on LB or Postgres side; keepalive settings. (5) DB-side: pg_stat_activity during a spike for locks and waits; IOPS, CPU, replication lag. (6) Service-side: GC pauses, OOM kills, restarts, thread starvation. (7) Mitigate: temporarily raise pool size, set statement_timeout, add circuit breaker + retry-with-jitter. (8) Fix root cause and add a regression test plus alerts on pool saturation and DB wait events.

Start by separating where the 502 originates: load balancer, API gateway, reverse proxy, or application server. Check timestamps and correlate 502 spikes with deploys, traffic surges, dependency latency, and Postgres health.

A disciplined path:
1. Inspect edge and proxy logs for upstream timeout, connection reset, or no healthy upstream messages.
2. Compare request latency distributions before and during failures, especially p95/p99.
3. Check app logs for thread-pool exhaustion, DB pool starvation, timeout exceptions, or crash/restart behavior.
4. Inspect Postgres metrics: active connections, lock waits, slow queries, CPU, I/O, replication lag if relevant.
5. Check app-side DB pool limits versus request concurrency.
6. Trace a failing request through distributed tracing if available.
7. Reproduce under load if possible.

Intermittent 502s near Postgres often come from connection pool exhaustion, slow or blocked queries causing upstream timeouts, overloaded app workers, or aggressive proxy timeout settings. Do not jump straight to “the database is slow” without proving which component is breaking the request chain.

queries.prompt: A train leaves city A at 60 mph toward city B; another leaves B toward A at 80 mph. Cities are 280 miles apart; the first train leaves 30 minutes earlier. When and where do they meet (distance from A)?

expected_answers.answer: In the first 0.5 h, train A covers 30 miles; remaining gap is 250 miles. Closing speed is 140 mph; time to close = 250/140 = 25/14 h ≈ 1.786 h. Distance from A at meet = 30 + 60·25/14 ≈ 30 + 107.14 ≈ 137.14 miles. Total elapsed time from A's departure: 0.5 + 25/14 ≈ 2.286 h.

The first train gets a 30-minute head start:
\[
60 	imes 0.5 = 30 	ext{ miles}.
\]

So when the second train departs, the remaining separation is
\[
280 - 30 = 250 	ext{ miles}.
\]

Their closing speed is
\[
60 + 80 = 140 	ext{ mph}.
\]

Time after the second train leaves:
\[
250/140 = 25/14 \approx 1.786 	ext{ hours}
\]
which is about **1 hour 47 minutes**.

Total time after the first train leaves:
\[
0.5 + 25/14 = 16/7 \approx 2.286 	ext{ hours}.
\]

Distance from city A:
\[
60 	imes 16/7 = 960/7 \approx 137.14 	ext{ miles}.
\]

They meet about **2 hours 17 minutes after the first train departs**, **1 hour 47 minutes after the second departs**, roughly **137.1 miles from city A**.

queries.prompt: Compare optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency control for a high-throughput web app and recommend when to use each.

expected_answers.answer: Pessimistic locks rows before reading/updating, blocking other writers. Good for high-contention writes; downside: lock waits, deadlocks, lower throughput. Optimistic reads without locks, then validates at commit (version column or snapshot isolation). Good for read-heavy workloads or low conflict rates; rollback cost is small. Default to optimistic (snapshot isolation in Postgres/InnoDB plus version columns) for typical web apps; use pessimistic (SELECT FOR UPDATE, advisory locks) for narrowly contended hot rows like inventory decrement or wallet balance.

Pessimistic concurrency control assumes conflicts are likely and prevents them up front, usually with locks. It is appropriate when collisions are frequent, the cost of conflicting updates is high, or a workflow must preserve strict ordering, such as reserving scarce inventory during checkout.

Optimistic concurrency control assumes conflicts are relatively rare. Readers proceed without locks, and writes succeed only if the underlying version has not changed. It scales better under low contention and is common in web apps through version columns or compare-and-swap updates.

For a high-throughput web app, default to optimistic control for most user-profile, content, and metadata updates. Use pessimistic control or transactional locking for narrow, high-conflict critical sections such as inventory decrement, financial balance updates, or workflows where retrying after conflict is unacceptable.

queries.prompt: Critique this design: 'We'll store passwords hashed with SHA-256 and a per-user salt.' What's wrong and what should we do?

expected_answers.answer: SHA-256 is too fast — modern GPUs compute billions/sec, so a stolen DB is brute-forceable even with salt. Single-iteration has no work factor to keep up with hardware. Use a password hash designed for it: bcrypt (cost factor ≥ 12), scrypt, or Argon2id (preferred; memory-hard, GPU/ASIC-resistant). Store algorithm id, params, salt, hash in one record so params can rotate. Add a server-side pepper from secret storage to defeat offline attacks if only the DB leaks. Plus: TLS, rate limiting, MFA, breached-password checks.

The design is not adequate for passwords. SHA-256 is intentionally fast, which makes brute-force guessing cheap on modern GPUs and ASICs even with a per-user salt. A salt prevents precomputed rainbow-table reuse; it does not make a fast hash slow enough.

Use a dedicated password hashing function such as **Argon2id**, **bcrypt**, or **scrypt** with calibrated work factors. Store:
- the algorithm
- salt
- cost parameters
- resulting hash

Argon2id is a strong default because it is memory-hard. Also add rate limiting, MFA support where appropriate, safe password reset flows, and rehash-on-login when upgrading parameters. The problem is not the presence of a salt; it is the use of the wrong primitive.

queries.prompt: A monopolist faces demand Q = 100 − 2P and constant MC = 10. Find the profit-maximizing price and quantity.

expected_answers.answer: Inverse demand: P = 50 − Q/2. Revenue R = 50Q − Q²/2; MR = 50 − Q. MR = MC: 50 − Q = 10 ⇒ Q = 40. Then P = 50 − 20 = 30. Profit-maximizing price = 30, quantity = 40.

Demand is
\[
Q = 100 - 2P.
\]

Invert it:
\[
P = 50 - 0.5Q.
\]

Total revenue:
\[
TR = PQ = (50 - 0.5Q)Q = 50Q - 0.5Q^2.
\]

Marginal revenue:
\[
MR = 50 - Q.
\]

Set `MR = MC = 10`:
\[
50 - Q = 10 \Rightarrow Q = 40.
\]

Then
\[
P = 50 - 0.5(40) = 30.
\]

So the monopolist chooses
\[
\boxed{Q=40,\ P=30}.
\]

queries.prompt: Prove there are infinitely many primes (Euclid's argument).

expected_answers.answer: Suppose finitely many primes p₁,...,p_k. Consider N = p₁·...·p_k + 1. N > 1 has some prime factor q. q cannot be any p_i, since dividing N by p_i leaves remainder 1. So q is a prime not in our list — contradiction. □

Assume, for contradiction, that there are only finitely many primes:
\[
p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n.
\]

Construct
\[
N = p_1p_2\cdots p_n + 1.
\]

Now divide `N` by any listed prime `p_i`. Since `p_i` divides the product, dividing `N` by `p_i` leaves remainder 1. Therefore, none of the listed primes divides `N`.

But every integer greater than 1 is either prime or has a prime factor. Thus `N` must be prime itself or have a prime factor not in the list, contradicting the assumption that the list contained all primes.

Therefore, there are infinitely many primes.

queries.prompt: You inherit a 50k-line Python monolith with no tests. Outline a 90-day plan to make it safer to change.

expected_answers.answer: Days 1–14 (observe): CI, lint (ruff), mypy baseline, coverage on import, structured logging with correlation IDs, error tracking (Sentry), deploy checklist. Identify 5 highest-risk modules from logs/incidents. Days 15–45 (characterize): write characterization (golden-master) tests on those 5 modules — capture inputs/outputs from prod or replay logs. Property tests for pure functions. Thin domain interfaces around riskiest external dependencies for fakes. Days 46–75 (refactor under tests): extract pure logic from I/O, kill globals, remove star imports. Establish module boundaries; flag dependency cycles. Days 76–90 (institutionalize): CI blocks merges without tests on changed code; 'safe to change' label per module by coverage. Document deploy/rollback. 12-month goal: 70% coverage on critical paths, no untyped public APIs, runbook for top 10 alerts.

A sensible 90-day plan has one goal: reduce change risk without freezing delivery.

**Days 0–30: map and stabilize.**
- Identify business-critical flows, fragile modules, deployment process, and top production incidents.
- Add basic observability where absent: structured logs, error reporting, key metrics.
- Create smoke tests around the highest-value workflows, preferably through public interfaces.
- Capture dependency, packaging, and environment setup in repeatable scripts or docs.
- Establish coding guardrails: linting, formatting, type-checking where practical, and CI on every change.

**Days 31–60: build seams and safety nets.**
- Add characterization tests around areas that change often or break often.
- Break obvious god-functions or hidden global state only where needed to enable testing.
- Introduce contract tests around integrations.
- Start measuring coverage by risk, not vanity percentage.
- Require small PRs, explicit rollback notes, and no “drive-by refactor plus feature” bundles.

**Days 61–90: improve architecture selectively.**
- Extract modules only when the boundary is proven by repeated change pain.
- Retire dead code and flaky paths.
- Improve deployment confidence with staging parity, migration checks, and rollback drills.
- Track a small scorecard: change failure rate, lead time, mean time to detect, and test flake rate.

Do not begin with a heroic rewrite. In a 50k-line monolith with no tests, rewrite enthusiasm is usually a risk multiplier.

queries.prompt: Explain how G1 garbage collection in the JVM decides which regions to collect, and the tradeoffs.

expected_answers.answer: G1 divides the heap into equal-sized regions (1–32 MB), each tagged eden, survivor, old, or humongous. Per-region 'liveness' is tracked via remembered sets (RSets) recording inbound references. Young GCs collect all eden+survivor. Mixed GCs collect young + a chosen subset of old: G1 estimates live-data ratio and time-to-collect per old region, then picks regions yielding the most free space per unit pause, subject to MaxGCPauseMillis. Tradeoffs: predictable pauses and good throughput on large heaps vs. RSet maintenance overhead (memory + write barriers); worse on very high allocation churn that defeats incremental old-gen collection.

G1 divides the heap into equal-sized regions rather than fixed young and old contiguous spaces. It tracks which regions contain the most reclaimable garbage and tries to meet pause-time goals by collecting a selected set of regions rather than the entire heap each cycle. Young collections process Eden and survivor regions. During mixed collections, G1 also includes old regions judged to have favorable garbage-to-work ratios.

The core tradeoff is predictability versus throughput. G1 is designed to provide more controllable pause times than older throughput collectors on large heaps, but it pays for that with remembered sets, region accounting, and more complex heuristics. If pause goals are too aggressive or live-set pressure is high, G1 may struggle and fall back into expensive behavior. It is a good general-purpose collector, not a magic fix for allocation abuse or memory leaks.

queries.prompt: Logistic regression and a random forest disagree on test predictions on a small dataset. How would you reason about which to trust?

expected_answers.answer: Fundamentals: same split, scaling, features, reasonable hyperparameters? Then: (1) cross-validated metrics (AUC, calibration, Brier) — single test split is noisy.
