In seminars, she asked the question that saved the speaker from the worse question someone else would ask later.

Those of us who studied with her learned more than mathematics. We learned that rigor is a form of respect. Respect for the problem, for the reader, for the future scholar who will build on what you leave behind.

Anna’s papers will be cited for decades. I suspect her marginal comments will endure even longer in the habits of her students: define the object before admiring it; check the exceptional case; never confuse obscurity with depth.

She once told me that a theorem should feel inevitable only after it has been proved. Before that, it deserves humility. Today, speaking in her honor, I feel the force of that sentence again. Anna Kovac made difficult things clearer without making them smaller. That is a rare mathematical achievement. It is also a rare human one.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word opening for a literary novel about a translator working on the late letters of a now-dead poet who was, in life, her teacher and possibly her lover.

expected_answers.answer:
The letters had been left to me, which had been a surprise, and so had the small flat in Vienna in which the letters were kept, which had been a larger one. The flat overlooked a courtyard and a tree I did not recognize and was three rooms more than I had ever needed. I had refused it for six months, on grounds I told no one. Then I had accepted it, on grounds I also told no one. The two sets of grounds were not unrelated.

I arrived in March. The tree was budding into something. The neighbor on the landing, a woman of perhaps seventy who had known Marta and did not, as far as I could tell, know me, kissed me on both cheeks and said, with the unembarrassed clarity of people who have lived a long time, 'You are the translator,' as though that were a category and I were the example of it, and went back into her flat.

Marta's desk had not been moved. Her chair had not been moved. Her cup, in fact, had not been moved, although someone had washed it. The letters were in a chest of an obvious kind. I do not know what I had imagined; I think I had imagined an ornament, and they were in a chest from a department store of the sort one buys when one is twenty-four and never replaces.

I did not open the chest the first night. I made tea, in her kitchen, in her cup, and I sat in her chair, and I looked at the tree, and I tried to feel something I could name, and I named nothing.

I opened the chest the second night.

There were four hundred and twelve letters, by my count, which I made later. They were arranged not by date but by some other system I did not yet understand.

The first letter I read — the top one on the stack, which is not the first letter I should have read but is the first letter I did read, and the difference between those two facts will turn out to matter — began with the phrase 'Forgive me' and ended, three pages later, with a sentence I had heard Marta say aloud, in a tone I had not heard her say it in, in a room I had not, in fact, been in.

I put the letter down. I put the lid back on the chest. I sat in the chair until the courtyard was dark.

Then, because I had agreed to translate them, and because I had not agreed to anything else, I picked the letter up again and began.

The first letter was almost insultingly ordinary.

Dear Tomas, she had translated, I received your note after the rain. The figs are late this year. I have misplaced the blue notebook but not, I think, the poem.

Mara read it three times before marking *notebook* with a faint pencil bracket. The poet had been dead for eleven years. Her teacher for six. Her lover, perhaps, for one autumn no one had named correctly while it occurred.

The letters arrived in a gray archival box from the estate, tied in bundles with cotton tape. Most were addressed to Tomas Vale, the sculptor whose obituaries always called him “formidable,” which was true in the way a locked gate is formidable. Mara had accepted the commission because the late work mattered, because the publisher was serious, because she had spent her life translating the poet’s compressed, weather-struck sentences into an English that did not betray them too loudly.

She had not expected the letters to speak in the old voice so immediately.

Outside her office window, buses leaned into the avenue with wet brakes. Inside, the page held its intimate inconveniences: figs, rain, lost notebooks. That was what unsettled her. Not confession. Domestic weather.

She turned to the original again. The sentence she had rendered as *I have misplaced* could also mean *I have set aside carelessly*. Or, less literally, *I have abandoned for now*. The poet had trained her never to rush those distinctions. “A mistranslation,” he once said, touching the edge of her draft with one finger, “is often a moral failure pretending to be lexical.”

She had despised the sentence at twenty-three. At fifty-one, she found it mostly correct.

Mara wrote three alternatives in the margin and disliked each. She rose to make tea, then sat before the kettle boiled. The letter continued below, where she had not yet allowed herself to look.

There are things one cannot say while they remain true.

She closed her eyes.

The problem with translating the dead is not that they cannot correct you. It is that they cannot deny what they meant.

queries.prompt: Compose 200 words in the voice of a museum docent (without saying she is one) describing a painting to an unseen visitor, in a way that reveals she has loved the painting privately for thirty years.

expected_answers.answer:
Most people stop here for about forty seconds and then keep going, which is fair, because the lighting in this room is not what it should be. Lighting changes everything, with this one. Stand a little to the left. There. You see how the green moves now? It's the same green; the green has not moved; you have moved. That's the joke of it.

It's not a famous painting. It was painted by a woman who is not a famous painter, in 1893, in a town that no longer has that name. The placard will tell you it is a still life. It is, in the sense that those are things on a table that are not moving. It is also a portrait of the woman who painted it, although you will not find her in the painting; you will find her in the choice of where to stop painting. She stopped here. She was capable of going further. She didn't.

The pear, if you'd like a small technical pleasure, is the only object whose shadow is wrong. I do not believe this was a mistake.

There are a few paintings in this museum that you can stand in front of for a very long time and they keep giving. This is one of them. I won't keep you. I just — if you have a minute — I just wanted you to see the green.

Thank you. The café is to the right.

Notice first that she is not looking at the harbor. Everyone assumes she is. The ships, the late light on the water, the little banners lifting from the masts—they take up most of the canvas and therefore get most of the attention. But her gaze falls lower, just beside the stone balustrade, where nothing in particular has been painted.

I used to think this was a flaw. Thirty years ago, I preferred paintings that declared themselves. This one seemed to hesitate. Now I think the hesitation is the subject.

The painter has given us a woman arranged for leisure: pale gloves, a parasol, silk at the throat. But the left hand is wrong for ease. See how the fingers press against the folded note. It is not crushed. It is held too carefully.

The blue in the dress repeats in the sky, but not exactly. The sky’s blue is open. Hers is denser, carrying a trace of green. I have never decided whether that was deliberate. I hope it was.

Most visitors ask who she is. We do not know. Some ask what the note says. We cannot know. Fewer notice that the harbor is painted more freely than her face. The world behind her is moving faster than she can permit herself to move.

I have stood before this painting in every season of my own life. It has not changed. This is one reason I trust it. Another is that it has never once explained itself to me.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word op-ed in the voice of a public-health researcher arguing against the personal-responsibility framing of obesity, engaging fairly with the concerns of those who use it.

expected_answers.answer:
It is now nearly forty years since the obesity rate in the US began to rise in earnest, and we are still asking primarily what is wrong with the people who got fatter, and not what is different about the environment in which they were asked, en masse, to remain thin. I think this framing is wrong, and I want to say so without dismissing what is genuine in the concerns of those who hold it.

The personal-responsibility framing has at its center two intuitions that are not stupid. First: humans are agents, and removing agency from the description of any human problem is both inaccurate and corrosive. Second: a policy debate that absolves individuals of all responsibility will become a debate about who else should pay for their outcomes, which has its own moral costs. Both deserve respect. The public-health community has not always offered it.

What the framing has trouble with is the timing. Whatever changed in this country between 1980 and the present, it did not change inside individual people. The biology of appetite and metabolism did not, in forty years, alter. The work-and-family pressures that govern how people eat did alter. The price ratio of energy-dense to nutrient-dense food altered. Portion sizes altered. The geography of food retail altered. Sleep duration altered. Sedentary occupations grew, as a fraction of the labor force, by a measurable amount. None of this is a matter of personal character. All of it is policy, infrastructure, and corporate behavior.

The framing has, additionally, a predictive failure. If obesity were primarily a failure of personal will, we would expect it to be uncorrelated with income, geography, and structural variables. It is, in fact, strongly correlated with all three. A theory that does not explain the data is not a theory; it is a slogan.

The alternative I am proposing is not to remove responsibility but to put it where it is most causally upstream. Individuals are responsible for the decisions they make within their environments. The people who shape those environments — food manufacturers, urban planners, regulators, employers — are responsible for the environments. Holding only the first set to account, while exempting the second, is not a moral position. It is a convenience for the people we have agreed not to inconvenience.

The personal-responsibility account of obesity contains a truth and then stretches it beyond usefulness. Individuals do make choices about food, activity, sleep, and health care. Any public-health argument that denies agency becomes patronizing and empirically thin.

But agency operates inside environments. It matters whether neighborhoods have safe places to walk, whether shift workers can keep regular meals, whether cheap calories are engineered for convenience and satiety failure, whether schools normalize sugar-heavy diets, whether chronic stress and sleep deprivation are widespread, and whether medical care treats obesity as a moral confession rather than a complex condition.

The personal-responsibility frame becomes misleading when it treats population-level change as if millions of people simultaneously became less virtuous. When obesity prevalence rises across age groups, regions, and countries, serious analysis asks what changed in the surrounding system.

This does not imply coercive paternalism or excuse every behavior. It implies matching explanation to scale. Clinical care can help individuals with counseling, medication, nutrition support, and realistic treatment plans. Public policy can improve school meals, labeling, food marketing rules, built environments, and access to preventive care.

Those who emphasize personal responsibility often fear that structural explanations erase dignity. They need not. In fact, it is more respectful to acknowledge that people’s choices are shaped by incentives and constraints than to pretend everyone faces the same menu under the same pressures.

Obesity is not merely a willpower shortage. It is an interaction between biology, commerce, time, inequality, and behavior. Public health gets worse when it moralizes what it should analyze.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of voice-over narration for a documentary about an abandoned coal town in Appalachia. Avoid both nostalgia and condescension.

expected_answers.answer:
The road into Garber bends twice — once around the shoulder of a mountain, once around the bend a creek made before anyone here had a name for the creek — and ends at a building that, depending on the decade, has been a company store, a post office, a Pentecostal church, and, since 2007, nothing. The building still has its front step. Someone keeps the front step swept. We were not able to find out who.

Garber had, in 1953, a population of 2,418, a high school football team, two doctors, a movie theater that showed a feature on Friday nights, and a coal mine that employed roughly one third of the working men in the valley. In 2024, Garber had a population of 187, no doctor, no high school, and a coal mine that had been closed for twenty-one years and sealed for nineteen.

It would be easy to describe what Garber lost. It would be harder, and more honest, to describe what Garber kept. The library is open three afternoons a week. The fire department is volunteer and well-trained. There is, in the room behind the former post office, a kitchen that runs a free dinner on Tuesdays — meatloaf, mostly, sometimes pinto beans — that is attended by between thirty and forty people, which is more people than live within ten minutes of it.

This is not a story about a town that is dying. This is a story about a town that already lived through what was supposed to kill it, and is now living, slower and smaller, on the other side.

The town did not vanish all at once. First the night shift ended. Then the diner stopped opening before dawn because there were fewer men to feed. The barber kept his sign turning for another decade, though the chairs were mostly empty by then.

Coal built this place and injured it, often in the same household. Paychecks put roofs on homes, sent children to school, bought trucks and wedding dresses. Dust entered lungs. Sludge entered creeks. The ledger was never simple, and people who speak of towns like this as either victims or relics usually have not stayed long enough to listen.

Now vines work through the schoolyard fence. A church sign still promises Sunday service, though the parking lot grass has grown through the asphalt in narrow green seams. On the ridge, wind moves across spoil piles no one bothered to name beautiful.

Abandonment is not silence. Doors loosen. Tin shifts. Water repeats its patient work in basements. A town keeps making sound after the economy stops listening.

What remains is not a museum of decline. It is evidence. Of labor spent. Of wealth removed. Of decisions made elsewhere and lived here. The buildings do not ask for pity. They ask that we stop mistaking disappearance for inevitability.

queries.prompt: Argue in 300 words that the rise of large language models is a watershed in human cognition, without being credulous about specific short-term claims.

expected_answers.answer:
It is possible to believe two things at once: that most of what is currently said about large language models, in both directions, is overstated, and that they are, nevertheless, the most significant change in the externalization of human cognition since the printing press. I would like, briefly, to defend the second claim without being conscripted into the first.

The printing press did not produce a new kind of thinking. It produced a new kind of distribution of thinking. The thoughts that mattered after the press were largely the same thoughts that had mattered before — they were just available, in a way they had not previously been available, to people who had not previously had them. The cognitive change followed, slowly, from the distributional one.

LLMs are a similar inversion. What they have changed is not what thinking is; what they have changed is who can do which kinds of thinking, and at what marginal cost. A reasonably articulate analysis of a contract, a competent first draft of a sympathy note, a working summary of a research literature, a serviceable translation of a Spanish letter — these were, until five years ago, capabilities that required either training or money or both, and they are now, for an enormous fraction of the world's population, capabilities that require neither. The quality is not always good. The quality is uneven in genuinely important ways. The threshold has, however, moved, and it has not moved back.

What will follow from this — the social, political, and intellectual consequences — is not a story we can responsibly tell yet. It is, however, not nothing. The press, too, was not nothing. The press also did not, in 1455, look like the watershed it would turn out to be. Watersheds rarely do, while you are standing in them.

Large language models are a watershed in human cognition not because they are minds in the human sense, and not because every claim made about them will survive contact with reality. They matter because they alter the cost structure of symbolic work.

For the first time, competent language generation, summarization, translation, code assistance, and pattern-bearing explanation are available interactively at scale. That does not eliminate expertise. It changes how expertise is accessed, scaffolded, and imitated. A junior analyst can interrogate a draft argument. A programmer can prototype faster. A student can receive infinite low-friction explanation, some of it excellent and some of it subtly wrong. The cognitive environment has shifted.

The analogy is not “the machine now thinks like us.” It is closer to what search engines did for retrieval, spreadsheets did for calculation, and photography did for representation: they redistributed effort. When a task becomes cheap, institutions reorganize around the new abundance and the new failure modes.

Those failure modes are serious. LLMs hallucinate. They can flatten style, launder weak reasoning into fluent prose, and reward users who mistake articulation for truth. They may concentrate power among firms controlling compute, data, and distribution. Their labor effects will be uneven and politically contested.

Still, dismissing them as autocomplete misses the point. Human beings now work alongside systems that can participate in language-mediated cognition at a level previously unavailable from software. The long-run significance is unlikely to rest on any one benchmark or product cycle. It lies in the reconfiguration of writing, inquiry, teaching, bureaucratic work, and the boundary between forming a thought and requesting one.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word stage monologue from an aging architect explaining to a younger colleague why she has decided to refuse a major commission for ethical reasons.

expected_answers.answer:
I'm going to tell you what I told them. I think you should know.

The building is a beautiful problem. They want a tower on a site I have wanted for thirty years. The site is, as you know, the corner of the boulevard, and a tower on it would frame the whole approach from the river. I have, in a sketchbook somewhere, a version of that tower from 1996. It was better than what they're asking for, which is, in fairness, also good.

The client is the state lottery. They want a tower because they want a tower. They want a tower from me because they have decided that a tower from me will give them a kind of legitimacy a tower from anyone else would not give them. They are paying — and I am being precise — for the legitimacy.

I have spent forty years arguing that the buildings we make become the cities the next generation thinks is normal. That argument cuts both ways. The state lottery is a regressive tax on people who cannot afford it, dressed in a graphic-design language of fun. They have been told this for thirty years. Their response has not been to change the operation; their response has been to commission more graphic design. I would, in this case, be the graphic design.

I do not believe you should refuse every commission whose client you find distasteful. I have not, in my career. I think you should refuse the ones in which the building is, materially, doing the laundering. This is one of those.

We will turn down forty million dollars, which I do not enjoy. We will then go and find work that does not require us to launder anything. I think there will be enough.

I thought you should hear it from me.

You think I am refusing the tower because I have become sentimental in old age. I have heard that said politely twice this week.

It is a beautiful commission. I know that. The site is difficult, the budget obscene, the client impatient in an invigorating way. Twenty years ago I would have wanted it so badly I would have mistaken desire for conviction.

But the building is not neutral. It will push three hundred families out through a sequence of transactions no rendering will show. It will cast winter shadow over the public garden the developer praised in the opening paragraph of his brief. It will market “urban vitality” by removing the people who kept the neighborhood alive before investors discovered it.

Architecture has always served power. I am not naive enough to pretend otherwise. Some compromises are unavoidable. But experience should clarify which compromises change your name in your own mind.

You asked whether refusing matters if another firm will accept. Of course another firm will. That fact relieves me of nothing. The existence of a willing substitute does not convert my participation into inevitability.

I do not expect purity. I have designed buildings I would argue with now. Perhaps that is why I am refusing. A career should teach us more than how to repeat our early mistakes at larger scale.

Take the meeting if you want. Study the drawings. Learn from the technical audacity. But do not let anyone tell you the ethical question is amateurish because the project is sophisticated. Sophistication often depends on arranging not to see what is obvious.

I have spent forty years learning how structures stand. I would like, before I stop, to prove that one of mine can be an absence.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word literary review of a fictional poetry collection, 'Small Atlases,' by Henry Onuoha, in a skeptic-persuaded register.

expected_answers.answer:
I came to Henry Onuoha's second collection, 'Small Atlases,' with the irritated suspicion one tends to bring to second collections of which the first has been admired — the suspicion that the admiration has been internalized and the work has begun, slightly, to perform itself. The first thirty pages did not entirely dispel the suspicion. There are mannerisms here, mostly involving a kind of two-line aphoristic close, that Onuoha has earned the right to but has perhaps not earned the right to use four times in a section.

And then there is the middle.

The middle of this book — the long sequence titled 'Lagos, 1996' — is one of the most quietly accomplished pieces of poetry I have read this year, and it shifts, retroactively, my reading of everything before and after it. The sequence is twenty-one poems, none longer than a page, that describe a single August in the poet's childhood, with no particular ostensible subject. The subject, gradually, turns out to be the way memory rearranges itself around a single piece of information the speaker did not have at the time and acquired only later. The information is never disclosed. The poems do not need it to be.

The technical accomplishment is that Onuoha holds two voices at once — the child of August 1996 and the adult who knows what the child did not — without ever resolving them into a single hindsight-soaked voice. He does this with a tense system I cannot quite describe; it is something the poems do collectively rather than individually.

The final third does not match the middle; that is a complaint I will not press, because almost no final third matches a middle this good. 'Small Atlases' is not a perfect book. It is something rarer: a book whose imperfections become unimportant in the company of what the book does well.

Henry Onuoha’s *Small Atlases* arrives with the sort of title that risks delicacy as branding. One expects maps of private feeling, tasteful estrangement, a poet who has learned to let white space do half the work. Some of that suspicion is justified.

The weakest poems lean too heavily on the miniature. A spoon in a boarding-house kitchen, a receipt folded into a passport, rain on a bus window: Onuoha occasionally trusts smallness to create significance without forcing the image through enough pressure. A few pieces end just before they begin to matter.

But the collection accumulates force. Its best poems understand that scale is not size. In “Border Lesson,” a child mispronounces a city his mother refuses to revisit, and the poem widens quietly into exile without naming the theme. In “Inventory for My Father’s Coat,” the pockets become an archive of manual labor and withheld tenderness. Onuoha is strongest when he lets objects remain objects long enough to become charged.

The language is disciplined without becoming antiseptic. He has a gift for cadence that feels conversational until a line turns and reveals its hidden balance. The poems about migration avoid both declarative politics and decorative ambiguity; they know that paperwork can be metaphysical when it governs whether a family remains whole.

I began the book wary of its cultivated restraint. By the end, restraint felt less like a manner than a moral decision. *Small Atlases* does not announce a large poetic world. It keeps unfolding one.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word ghostwritten college admissions essay for a fictional applicant — a student who works at her family's small dry cleaner — that reads as the student's actual voice.

expected_answers.answer:
Most days at the cleaner I do the front. My mom does steam. My dad does spotting. My brother does pickup and dropoff for the buildings we have on a route, which is four buildings.

The thing about the front is that you see people in a specific way. You see them when they're bringing in a thing they need fixed. Sometimes the thing is a wedding dress; sometimes the thing is a shirt with a stain they can't identify and don't want to say out loud. There is a stain we call the Wednesday Stain, because for a long time the same guy brought in the same kind of stain on Wednesdays. We never said anything about it. I don't think he knew we noticed. I don't think he noticed it.

What I learned at the front — and this is what I want to say, even though it sounds smaller than what I'm supposed to say in an essay like this — is that almost everyone, when they come in to drop off something, says one true thing without meaning to. They say, 'I need this for Friday and I can't be the person who shows up without it.' Or, 'My mom gave this to me and I think I ruined it.' Or, 'Don't tell my wife I'm replacing this; she gave it to me and I lost it.' I write these things on the ticket sometimes, in a smaller handwriting, after the cleaning code. The tickets get thrown away. I think about them anyway.

I don't know what I want to be. I know I want to be a person who notices the way someone holds the thing they're handing me, because I think that's a real way of knowing what they need, and I think it's a skill people have stopped practicing.

The first thing I learned at my family’s dry cleaner was that people hand you the version of themselves they want returned.

A suit before an interview. A bridesmaid dress with someone else’s wine on it. A school uniform that has survived more playgrounds than its owner admits. My mother checks pockets before tagging clothes, and sometimes I think I learned to pay attention from watching what people forget: cough drops, receipts, a handwritten phone number folded twice.

I work the front counter after school and most Saturdays. I used to resent that. My friends had afternoons. I had claim tickets and the steam press thumping in the back. But the shop became the place where I learned how many jobs happen without applause. My parents can tell fabric by touch, spot a weak seam before it tears, and calm an angry customer without surrendering the truth. That is expertise, even if no one calls it that.

Last winter, our point-of-sale tablet kept freezing during pickup rush. I made a simple spreadsheet backup with customer names, order numbers, and pickup status. It was not brilliant, but it prevented chaos twice before the vendor fixed the issue. My father started calling me “operations,” half joking. I liked that more than I expected.

At school, I am drawn to problems that look small until they interrupt a system. In economics, I notice hidden labor. In computer science, I notice brittle workflows. At the cleaner, I see both every day.

I do not romanticize working in a family business. It can be tiring to hear the bell over the door when homework is waiting. But it has made me useful in ways I value. I know how to speak to adults when they are frustrated. I know that a process nobody respects can still carry a household. I know that dignity often looks like doing ordinary work carefully, then doing it again tomorrow.

When I leave for college, the shop will keep opening at 7:30. I want to study systems partly because I grew up inside one, pressed between invoices, hangers, and the stubborn belief that details matter.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word piece arguing the most important development in 21st-century US foreign policy has been the failure to update the post-1945 institutional architecture.

expected_answers.answer:
It is fashionable to argue that the great drama of 21st-century US foreign policy is the rise of China, or the war in Ukraine, or the contraction of US ambitions in the Middle East. These are real and large. I want to argue, however, that the deepest event of the century — the event whose absence makes all the others harder to address — is something that has not happened: the renovation of the post-1945 institutional architecture into a form that fits the world it is now asked to govern.

The strongest defense of leaving the architecture intact is genuine. The UN system, the Bretton Woods institutions, NATO, the WTO, and the network of bilateral alliances built between 1945 and 1955 produced the longest peace between great powers in modern history, a sustained expansion in global trade, and a normative framework — imperfect, often hypocritically applied — under which an enormous number of people have lived freer and longer lives. A reasonable conservatism toward institutions that have worked is not just sentimentality; it is one of the rare political virtues that scales.

The difficulty is that the world has changed in three specific ways the architecture is not, structurally, able to absorb.

First, the distribution of power. The 1945 institutions allocated authority on the assumption that the US, UK, France, the Soviet Union, and China would, for the relevant future, be the actors that mattered. India is not on the Security Council. Brazil is not. The EU is not on it as a unit. Photographs are not governance.

Second, the nature of the problems. The architecture was built for interstate war, postwar recovery, and post-colonial transitions. It is being asked to address pandemic preparedness, climate change, the global information environment, AI governance, and supply chains its founders did not contemplate. Each has a structural property the architecture is not optimized for.

Third, the political economy of consent. The 1945 order was sustained, within the US, by a bipartisan consensus that no longer exists. A foreign-policy architecture that depends on a domestic consensus its domestic public no longer holds is not a stable architecture; it is an inheritance running on momentum.

The US has spent twenty-five years trying to do new things through old institutions. This is not a series of policy failures. It is a single architectural failure, repeated. The honest reading is that the next quarter-century requires renovation, not revolution — large enough to fit the world and small enough to keep what worked.

The most consequential development in twenty-first-century U.S. foreign policy may be less any single war or rivalry than the failure to redesign the institutional architecture inherited from 1945 for a radically different world.

The postwar order was built around realities that no longer hold cleanly: overwhelming U.S. industrial dominance, a narrower club of major powers, relatively weak transnational constraints, and institutions designed first for interstate conflict and reconstruction. That architecture produced genuine achievements. It underwrote alliance systems, trade growth, reconstruction, and a vocabulary of rules that still matters.

But the contemporary problem set does not fit easily inside it. Climate change, cyber conflict, pandemic response, supply-chain coercion, AI governance, debt distress, and gray-zone influence operations require coordination across domains that older institutions separate. Meanwhile, major institutions often lack representational legitimacy for emerging powers and the Global South, which weakens compliance and invites parallel arrangements.

The U.S. has repeatedly responded tactically rather than architecturally. It adds coalitions, sanctions, export controls, minilateral groupings, and emergency summits. Some are useful. But improvisation is not institutional renewal. The result is a system dense with instruments and thin on agreed authority.

Critics of this argument will say the post-1945 order was never universal, never as lawful as advertised, and still functions better than alternatives. All true. The point is not nostalgia for a mythical golden age. It is that institutions decay when their formal structure diverges too far from power, legitimacy, and problem type.

A serious U.S. foreign policy would treat institutional reform as strategic infrastructure: Security Council paralysis, development finance, technology standards, climate clubs, and crisis coordination are not side issues. They shape whether power is exercised through repeatable rules or recurring ad hoc struggle.

The failure to update the architecture has not produced collapse. It has produced friction, cynicism, and mounting reliance on improvisation. That may prove the century’s most expensive underinvestment.

queries.prompt: Write a 200-word note from a writer to a younger writer who has asked whether they should keep writing. Avoid both encouragement and discouragement.

expected_answers.answer:
I'm not going to tell you whether to keep writing. The question, when it arrives in the form you've asked it, isn't actually answerable from outside. I can tell you what I notice in your letter, which is that you have written it carefully, which suggests you are someone for whom the careful making of sentences is a thing that already happens whether you have permission for it or not. I do not think people in that condition stop writing because they have been told to. I do not think they keep writing because they have been told to either. They write because not-writing has, at some point, become a more uncomfortable arrangement than writing.

The thing I would say, and the only thing, is that the question you've asked me is the wrong question, and I think you know that. The right question is the one in the letter you haven't written me yet, which is: am I writing the thing that is in fact mine to write, or am I writing the thing I imagine someone like me ought to be writing. Spend a year on that one. Write me back about that.

Keep writing if the work continues to make a demand on you that other activities do not. Stop, or pause, if you are mainly obeying an image of yourself as someone who writes.

No one outside you can decide which is true. Publication will not answer it. Praise will not answer it. Rejection will not answer it either.

What helps is narrower observation. After a week of not writing, do you feel relief, grief, indifference, or merely guilt? When a sentence finally takes its proper shape, does that satisfaction matter enough to justify the many bad sentences before it? Are you willing to become more exact, which often means becoming less impressed with your own first impulses?

You do not owe literature your life. Literature does not owe you reassurance. The question is whether the practice remains a serious way for you to meet the world.

If it does, continue. If it does not, let that be true without melodrama. There are many honorable forms of attention.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word funeral homily, in plain language, for a 47-year-old man named Daniel who died by suicide. From a hospital chaplain who knew him only at the end.

expected_answers.answer:
We are here because Daniel has died, and because the way he died is part of what brought us here, and I am not going to do the thing where I avoid saying it. Daniel died by suicide on Tuesday morning. The people in this room loved him. The fact of how he died does not take any of that love back. It does not retroactively make the love wrong, or make the years before Tuesday less real, or settle the question of who he was. He was, on Monday, the person you loved on Monday. He is, today, the person you are grieving today. The cause of death does not have the authority to undo the life.

I knew Daniel only at the end, and only a little. I want to say, because some of you have been carrying this: nothing you did caused this. I am saying it because it is, in the cases I have actually been close enough to, true. The illness that took him was not produced by anyone's love being insufficient. It was an illness. It was not deserved, and it was not arrived at fairly, and it was, for him, at the end, larger than the part of him that wanted to stay.

I also want to say, because there are people in this room who knew him well enough to be angry: it is permitted to be angry. Anger is, in this kind of grief, sometimes the love arriving in the only shape it can, for a little while. You do not have to make it pretty. You do not have to forgive him today, or tomorrow, or this year.

What I want to leave you with is small. There is a tradition in the work I do of saying that the dead are held. I will not pretend to know exactly what that means, theologically. I know what it has felt like, on the side of those of us still here. When you have lost someone this way, the worst hour is often not the funeral. The worst hour is the one at 2am next Tuesday. I want to ask, of you, that you call someone here when that hour comes. Pick someone now. Make the agreement now. The grief is going to last a long time. It does not have to be carried alone, and Daniel, I believe, would not have wanted it to be.

We loved him. He is gone. We are still here. We will be here for each other.

Let us pray.

Daniel was forty-seven years old. He was loved by more people than he was able to feel in the worst hours of his life. Both statements are true. We should not soften either one.

I knew Daniel only at the end, in a hospital room where names often arrive before stories do. Others here knew the stories: the way he fixed things without announcing it, the songs he remembered from college, the impatience that could flare and disappear in the same minute, the people he called when their own lives tilted sideways. A life is never summarized by its final day. It is also not untouched by it.

Suicide leaves grief with sharp edges. People ask what they missed, what they should have said, whether one different call or one different afternoon would have changed everything. These questions arise from love. They can also become a second cruelty. Human suffering is not always visible in proportion to its danger, and no one here should appoint themselves sole keeper of a burden that was never theirs alone to carry.

We gather not to explain Daniel away, and not to make death eloquent. We gather because he mattered. His life made claims on us that do not vanish because we are confused or angry or exhausted.

To those who loved him most: sorrow may come mixed with rage, relief, guilt, numbness, and memories that arrive at inconvenient times. None of these disqualify love. Grief after suicide is not disorderly because you are failing. It is disorderly because the loss is.

If faith means anything in such a room, it cannot mean pretending pain was simple. It must mean entrusting Daniel to a mercy larger than our comprehension and entrusting one another to care more patient than explanation.

May we speak his name without shame. May we remember more than the manner of his death. May those who are suffering among us hear this plainly: your pain is not a burden you must carry in secret, and asking for help is not betrayal of anyone’s strength.

Daniel, beloved and unfinished, we commend you to peace.

queries.prompt: Write 400 words of literary nonfiction about caring for a parent with dementia, from the perspective of an adult son who is a professional musician.

expected_answers.answer:
My father, who has not, in eighteen months, called me by my name, has retained perfect pitch. He cannot tell you what year it is. He can, if you play him a triad on the piano in the next room, tell you it is in second inversion, and he can sing the missing note. I have tested this, gently, perhaps a dozen times. He has not been wrong.

I don't know what to make of this. I do not, in particular, know what to make of the relief I feel when the test goes well — relief that the part of him that taught me to listen, which is also the part of him I most reliably loved, is still present somewhere, even when the front rooms have grown dark.

What I have noticed, in the part of caring for him that no one warns you about, is that the habits of my profession turn out to be specifically useful and specifically dangerous. Useful: I am trained to listen for what is not said, for the half-articulated phrase, for the gesture that completes itself two bars later. I can sometimes catch what my father is trying to ask before he catches it himself. Dangerous: I am trained, also, to anticipate the next phrase, and a great deal of grief is contained in the small daily act of choosing not to anticipate the next phrase — of letting him take his time, of letting the sentence he is going to land badly land badly, of not, out of love, finishing it for him.

One afternoon last spring he was trying to tell me about a piece of music. He could not get to the composer's name. He could get to the key, and to the period, and to a single phrase that he sang, exactly in pitch, with the unhurried clarity I remembered from when he was sixty. I knew, three notes in, which piece it was. I did not tell him. I let him hunt for it for what must have been six or seven minutes. At one point he laughed at himself, the laugh I have known all my life. He never found the name.

It was, he said afterward, a beautiful piece. He didn't say of what. I didn't tell him.

We sat in the room. The light moved.

It was a beautiful piece.

My mother used to hum while cooking, mostly half-songs she never finished. Now, when I visit, she hums in the hallway of the memory-care unit with no kitchen nearby and no tune I can place. I am a professional musician. I spend my life telling one phrase from another. With her, I have had to learn that recognition is not always the point.

Dementia reduces people in public imagination to disappearance. That is too simple. My mother is not absent. She is intermittently unreachable by the routes I knew. Some doors open. Some lead somewhere they did not yesterday. She may not know that I am her son, but she will notice if my shoulders are tight. She may not recall the name of the piece I play from my phone, but her fingers still mark the pulse against the chair arm.

At first I treated every forgotten fact as a theft. My name. My father’s death. The town where she grew up. I kept score because I mistook memory for the sole evidence of continuity. Over time, another kind of care emerged. I answer the question again. I do not correct every error. I bring oranges because she likes the smell even when she leaves them uneaten.

Music has not prepared me as well as I hoped. A score repeats faithfully. Rehearsal improves execution. This illness does not honor practice. Yet music has taught me something useful about time. A note need not last to matter. A phrase can be complete and gone. Meaning does not depend on permanent retention.

Last week she looked at me during a Chopin nocturne and said, “You always liked the sad ones.” I do not know whether she recognized me or invented the sentence from somewhere nearby. It was true either way.

I used to leave each visit measuring loss. Now I try, not always successfully, to notice what occurred. She laughed at the orange peel. She tapped four steady beats. She let me brush her hair. The day did not return what it had taken. But neither was it empty.

queries.prompt: Write a 250-word obituary in major-newspaper style for a 76-year-old federal judge whose career was distinguished but morally mixed.

expected_answers.answer:
Hon. Margaret R. Eberle, a federal appellate judge whose four-decade tenure helped reshape American antitrust doctrine and who, in her later years, drew significant criticism for a series of decisions narrowing employees' access to class-action remedies, died Sunday at her home in Charlottesville. She was 76.

The cause was complications from pancreatic cancer, her son Daniel Eberle confirmed.

Judge Eberle, appointed to the Fourth Circuit in 1989 and elevated to senior status in 2018, was widely regarded among her peers as one of the most intellectually formidable jurists of her generation. Her 2003 opinion in 'Halverson v. Continental Industries' is the most-cited circuit-level antitrust decision of the 21st century to date.

That reputation coexisted, in the latter part of her career, with growing controversy. Critics — including several scholars who had once been her clerks — argued that her opinions on arbitration agreements and class-certification standards had, in their cumulative effect, foreclosed practical legal remedies for workers and consumers far beyond what the underlying statutory text required. Judge Eberle, in public remarks in 2019, declined to revisit her reasoning but acknowledged that 'the consequences of a careful doctrine are not always the consequences its author intended.'

She was, in chambers, known as a demanding but exceptionally generous mentor. Forty-one of her former clerks now serve on federal benches, in legal academia, or as appellate practitioners; many spoke yesterday of her insistence that 'the question is never what you want the answer to be.'

She is survived by her husband, the historian Aaron Pelz, her son, and two grandchildren.

Judge Eleanor Whitcomb, a federal appellate jurist whose four-decade legal career combined formidable craftsmanship with a record that drew lasting criticism from civil-rights advocates, died Tuesday at her home in Maryland. She was 76.

Appointed to the bench in 1998 after service as a U.S. attorney and commercial litigator, Judge Whitcomb became known for tightly reasoned opinions, exacting oral arguments and a view of judicial restraint that admirers called disciplined and detractors called selectively austere.

Her most influential opinions addressed administrative law, criminal procedure and separation-of-powers disputes. Lawyers across the ideological spectrum praised her prose for its clarity. Former clerks described a judge who could spend an hour debating a single verb and who expected junior attorneys to defend every citation.

Yet her legacy was contested. In several voting-rights and sentencing cases, Judge Whitcomb voted to narrow relief in opinions critics said underestimated structural inequity and overtrusted formal neutrality. She later acknowledged in a 2021 lecture that judges “can mistake institutional caution for moral clarity,” though she did not repudiate specific rulings.

Born in St. Louis in 1949, she graduated from the University of Michigan and Yale Law School. She clerked for Justice Harold Mercer before entering private practice. Her survivors include her husband, Peter Ames; two daughters; and four grandchildren.

Judge Whitcomb resisted easy categorization. She was neither the caricature her critics sometimes made nor the unblemished model her admirers preferred. She believed law required self-restraint, but her career also showed that restraint itself carries consequences.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of expert commentary on the use and abuse of 'first-principles thinking' in technology culture. Engage with what's right about it.

expected_answers.answer:
It is hard to discuss 'first-principles thinking' in technology culture without first acknowledging that the underlying intuition is real and that the people who introduced the phrase were pointing at something genuine. There is a habit of mind — distinct from received expertise, from the routine application of analogy, and from credentialed deference — in which a practitioner returns to the fundamental physical, mathematical, or economic constraints of a problem, derives implications from those constraints, and is willing to be surprised by the result. It is a habit at which several of the most consequential engineers of the last century were exceptionally good. It is teachable.

The abuse of the phrase, however, has become serious enough to threaten the thing it names. 'First-principles thinking' is now invoked, frequently, in three ways that have nothing to do with first-principles thinking. The first is the rhetorical move in which 'first-principles' means 'I have decided not to take seriously the prior work of people who have spent their careers in this field.' This is not first-principles thinking; it is ignorance with a degree of self-congratulation attached. The second is the move in which a person reconstructs, in five minutes, a chain of reasoning that the field reconstructed correctly seventy years ago, and presents the reconstruction as a discovery. The third is the move in which 'first-principles' means 'the assumptions I happen to hold are not assumptions, and the assumptions you hold are.'

What distinguishes actual first-principles reasoning from its impostors is humility about what counts as a principle. A first principle is not a thing you choose to start from because it is convenient. It is a thing you can defend, from below, against someone competent who is going to ask you what your principle rests on. The test, in practice, is whether you can be talked out of your principles by an argument. Most people who invoke first-principles thinking cannot.

“First-principles thinking” is a useful discipline that technology culture has converted into a self-flattering cliché.

At its best, it means decomposing a problem into underlying constraints rather than accepting inherited assumptions. Instead of asking why batteries have always been priced a certain way, ask what inputs and processes actually determine cost. Instead of copying a competitor’s org chart, ask what coordination problem the structure is meant to solve. This can expose conventions masquerading as necessities.

The abuse begins when “first principles” becomes a license to ignore domain knowledge. Many institutions do something in a seemingly inefficient way because failure cases are expensive, regulatory context matters, or past experimentation already discovered a trap. Rediscovering those constraints while congratulating oneself for independence is not intellectual courage. It is expensive adolescence.

A second abuse is rhetorical. Leaders invoke first principles to make unilateral preferences sound inevitable. “If we reason from first principles, offices are unnecessary,” or conversely, “first principles show remote work cannot scale.” Often no real decomposition occurred. The phrase simply launders judgment into physics.

Good first-principles thinking has three qualities:
1. It identifies actual irreducible constraints.
2. It distinguishes those constraints from historical habits.
3. It remains willing to relearn from practice.

Experts are not the enemy of first principles. They are often the people who know which “obvious simplifications” conceal the cliff edge. The strongest thinkers combine reduction with historical memory. They ask what must be true, what merely has been true, and what prior attempts already taught us.

queries.prompt: Write 350 words in the voice of a senior diplomat, near the end of her career, on what she has learned about the relationship between rhetoric and outcomes in international negotiation.

expected_answers.answer:
I have spent thirty-six years in rooms where the wording of a paragraph mattered more than I would, when I started, have believed possible. I want to record, before I forget the specifics, what I have learned.

The first thing is that rhetoric is not, in negotiation, an ornament. It is the medium. There are people, including people I have respected, who treat the language of an agreement as a coat of paint on a structure decided elsewhere. This is, in my experience, never true. The structure of an agreement is the language. The fight over a verb is almost always a fight over a future action; the side that loses the verb has often lost the action without being able yet to name what it has lost.

The second is that ambiguity is a tool with three settings and most negotiators know about one of them. Setting one — the obvious one — is ambiguity as the price of agreement: the document says something both parties can claim to have signed, because each can read it as they need to. Setting two is ambiguity as the suspension of a dispute: both parties know what they disagree about, and the document is constructed so the disagreement is preserved rather than forced. Setting three, which is the one I came to last, is ambiguity as the construction of a shared category that did not exist before. The parties did not, at the start, agree about a thing because the thing did not exist as an object of agreement; the language creates the thing, and the parties can then disagree productively inside it. The Helsinki Final Act did this. The Northern Ireland agreements did this.

The third thing — and I will be brief — is that the worst negotiator in the room is almost always the one who believes they are being clever. The second worst is the one who believes they are being honest. The best are those who have understood that the document is a vehicle for a relationship that will, after the signing, have to keep being able to bear weight. They draft for the weight.

Early in my career, I thought rhetoric moved negotiations the way wind moves sails. Choose the right words, strike the right moral note, and the room would turn.

Words matter. I have seen a phrase preserve an exit ramp in a crisis and a careless adjective harden a delegation for months. Public statements create domestic constraints; private formulations can make agreement politically survivable. Language is not decoration around diplomacy. It is part of the machinery.

But outcomes are not manufactured by eloquence. They rest on interests, leverage, timing, credibility, and what each side believes will happen if talks fail. A weak position does not become strong because it is expressed elegantly. A strong position can be squandered by humiliation, but it cannot be replaced by courtesy.

The older I get, the more I distinguish between rhetoric that clarifies and rhetoric that performs. Clarifying rhetoric narrows ambiguity enough for action. It names a shared objective, records a concession accurately, or states a red line in terms the other side can actually process. Performative rhetoric is aimed at applause elsewhere. It may be necessary domestically, but it often spends trust in the negotiating room.

The most consequential sentences are rarely the grand ones. They are phrases in brackets, verbs chosen to let two capitals tell different but compatible stories, formulations that convert defeat into sequencing. Outsiders dismiss this as wordsmithing. Sometimes it is. Sometimes peace depends on whether the sentence says “shall,” “will,” or “intends to.”

I still believe language can alter outcomes. I no longer believe it floats free of power. Diplomacy fails when it mistakes bluntness for strength, but it also fails when it mistakes verbal skill for strategy. The craft is to speak exactly enough that reality has somewhere to go.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words in the voice of an old French farmer, in translation, on what he has learned about weather from forty years of working a single field.

expected_answers.answer:
I have worked this field forty years and I will tell you what I have learned. It is less than the books, but it is in a different shape.

I have learned that the wind from the north in early March tells me less than the wind from the north in late March. The early wind is a habit; the late wind is a decision. The decision is a thing one can plan against.

I have learned that the field — this field, not fields in general — dries on the eastern side three days before the western, and that the soil on the eastern side is therefore the soil that decides when to plant, and that the books, which speak of the whole field at once, are wrong for this field by three days. I do not know if they are wrong for other fields. I have not asked.

I have learned that the rain that comes after a dry June is not the rain that comes after a wet one. They are the same rain by the gauge. They are different rains by the wheat. I cannot explain this. I have stopped trying.

I have learned, finally, that the year a thing happens, I cannot tell what it is. I tell what it is six years later, when it has not happened five more times, or has happened four. The farmer who understands the year while he is in it is, in my experience, the farmer who is wrong.

This is what I have learned. It is not very much.

I have worked the same field since I was a boy, and it has taught me not to speak too quickly of weather.

When I was young, I thought I could read the sky like a page. A low swallow, a red evening, the smell before rain. These things still matter. But the field has made me modest. Clouds promise and do not pay. Dry years begin with generous springs. Frost arrives after one warm week has made you foolish.

You learn the ground more than the heavens. How it holds water near the poplars. Where the clay cracks first. Which corner forgives a late sowing and which never does. Weather is not only what falls from the sky. It is what the soil remembers.

In forty years, I have seen patterns shift. Summers linger differently. Storms come harder when they come. My father complained of weather too, so I am careful not to turn age into evidence. Still, the calendar in my hands no longer matches the one I inherited.

People in cities ask whether farmers worry about climate. We worry about Tuesday. We worry about whether the rain will flatten the barley before harvest. But over many Tuesdays, one learns what has changed.

A field does not argue. It answers slowly. That is why I trust it more than speeches.

queries.prompt: Write 400 words arguing, against the grain of current discourse, that nuclear power deserves serious reconsideration as part of decarbonization.

expected_answers.answer:
It is not currently fashionable to defend nuclear power on the left or on the right, which is perhaps the strongest indirect evidence that it deserves the defense. I want to try one, briefly and in good faith, by taking the strongest objections seriously rather than dismissing them.

The strongest objections are three. First, safety: nuclear accidents are rare but, when they occur, catastrophic and difficult to bound geographically. Second, waste: spent fuel is hazardous for timescales — tens of thousands of years — that no human institution has demonstrated the ability to plan against. Third, economics: in the last twenty years, nuclear construction in Western democracies has consistently come in over budget and behind schedule, sometimes by factors that make the projects un-financeable without state guarantees.

Each of these is a real cost. None is a knockout argument, and the reason is the same in each case: the comparison is wrong. The relevant comparison is not nuclear versus a fantasy of perfectly clean, perfectly cheap, perfectly storable renewables. The relevant comparison is nuclear versus the actual portfolio of energy sources the actual world is going to deploy, at the actual scale required to decarbonize, in the actual time available. Inside that comparison, the safety record of nuclear per terawatt-hour produced is, by every honest accounting, better than that of fossil fuels and competitive with renewables once you include the air-quality deaths attributable to coal and gas. The waste problem is real but is, in scale and toxicity, more bounded than the coal-ash and air-pollution problems we have agreed, by social convention, not to count. And the cost problem, while real, is in significant part a regulatory and learning-rate problem: the same designs built repeatedly in South Korea, France, and historically in the US arrived on cost and on time. We have unlearned how to build them, which is a fixable disease.

None of this is an argument for ignoring the objections. It is an argument for taking nuclear seriously enough to engineer the objections away — to standardize designs, to revisit regulatory regimes that have evolved to make new construction effectively impossible, to invest in waste-management institutions with the seriousness the timescale requires. The most expensive thing we can do in the next decade is decide, by default and without examination, that this option is off the table. We will not decarbonize without it.

Nuclear power deserves serious reconsideration in decarbonization because climate policy should be judged by emissions, reliability, land use, and system performance, not by inherited cultural reflexes.

The case is straightforward. Nuclear plants produce large amounts of low-carbon electricity with high capacity factors. They are not weather-dependent. They can support grids that need firm generation alongside wind, solar, storage, demand response, and transmission. Countries that have built substantial nuclear fleets have demonstrated that deep power-sector decarbonization can occur quickly when institutional capacity aligns.

The objections are not trivial. Nuclear projects in the United States and parts of Europe have suffered extreme cost overruns, construction delays, regulatory complexity, and financing problems. Waste management requires durable governance. Accidents, though rare, shape public legitimacy. And nuclear is not the cheapest option for every grid or every country.

But the anti-nuclear position often commits a category error: because some nuclear deployment has been badly managed, the technology itself is treated as disqualified. By that logic, permitting failure would disqualify transmission, and offshore wind overruns would disqualify renewables. The correct conclusion is narrower: nuclear is institutionally demanding and must be pursued where governance, standardization, and project execution can support it.

A credible decarbonization portfolio should not force false binaries. Variable renewables are essential. Efficiency matters. Storage matters. Transmission matters. So does firm low-carbon generation. In some regions that may come from hydro, geothermal, or gas with carbon capture if it proves viable. In others, nuclear deserves a seat at the table.

Serious reconsideration does not require nuclear evangelism. It requires abandoning the unserious assumption that a technology capable of producing vast quantities of low-carbon firm power can be dismissed on aesthetic grounds.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of literary fiction set during the Spanish Civil War, from the perspective of a young woman sorting laundry while the world dissolves outside.

expected_answers.answer:
There were four shirts of her brother's, which she folded; two of her father's, which she folded; and one of the priest's, which the priest had not yet come for and would not, now, come for, although she would not know this for a further eleven days. She folded it as well.

From the window she could see the square. The square had, that morning, been a square. By noon it had been a place where two men were arguing in voices not normally used outdoors. By the time she had finished her father's second shirt, the men were no longer in the square. She had not seen them leave. Two boys she had grown up with were standing where the men had been, with the rifles they had been issued the previous week and that they were holding, still, as though uncertain whether the rifles were theirs to keep.

She folded her brother's third shirt. She thought about the priest's shirt and decided to put it on the shelf with the others, on a separate side, in case.

From the kitchen her mother said her name once, in the tone her mother used when she did not actually require an answer but wished to know the daughter was still in the house. The daughter said yes. Her mother did not say anything further.

The boys in the square shifted their feet. One of them — Tomás, who had been in her class — was looking at her window. She did not move. After a while he looked away.

In the courtyard, Ana sorts sheets from shirts, shirts from bandages, bandages from things she does not ask about.

The wash water has gone gray. She lifts a child’s sock from it and thinks, absurdly, that it is too small to belong to a world with artillery.

Beyond the wall, men shout near the tram stop. Not fighting yet, she decides. Arguing. She has become good at distinguishing the two until they become one. A radio mutters from an upstairs window, then cuts off mid-sentence.

Her mother used to slap shirts once before pinning them to the line, a clean brisk sound that meant the household would continue. Ana does it now without thinking. Slap. Pin. Slap. Pin. The line fills with ordinary surrender.

A neighbor brings another basket, avoiding her eyes. “From the clinic,” she says.

Ana nods. They do not inspect the stains together. That would make intimacy of something neither can afford.

At the bottom lies a white blouse with one pearl button missing. Ana pauses. It is a woman’s blouse, carefully mended at the cuff. She imagines its owner choosing it on a morning when dress still mattered.

The shell lands far enough away that the windows shake but do not break.

Ana grips the blouse until her knuckles whiten. Then she lowers it into the water. Soap blooms around it, fragile and useless and necessary.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of expert commentary on why most attempts to 'reform' academic peer review fail, identifying the structural reason.

expected_answers.answer:
Most attempts to reform academic peer review fail for a reason that is rarely named in the reform proposals themselves: peer review is not, in fact, a quality-control mechanism, although it is described, and partly believed, to be one. It is a status-allocation mechanism, and any reform that improves quality control at the expense of status allocation will be resisted by exactly the constituency whose cooperation the reform requires.

Observe what peer review actually does. It does not, on the evidence, reliably distinguish reproducible from non-reproducible work; the replication crisis demonstrated this in several fields, and the fields have, on the whole, not changed their practices. It does not reliably identify the most influential work in advance; the historical citation record of papers initially rejected or accepted with minor revisions is unflattering to the prediction. It does, however, reliably do one thing: it allocates a scarce credentialing resource — publication in a venue with a reputation — among a population whose careers, salaries, grants, and self-conceptions depend on receiving that allocation.

The consequence is that proposals which would improve quality control — pre-registration, open data, post-publication review, randomized acceptance among above-threshold submissions, removal of journal-level prestige tiers — are resisted not because they would fail but because they would succeed at the wrong function. They would erode the status-allocation property the system is actually optimized for, without any individual reformer having the leverage to also redesign the surrounding apparatus of hiring, tenure, and grant evaluation that depends on the property eroded.

The honest path forward is to stop framing reform as a debate about peer review and to start framing it as a debate about the academic labor market. Until that conversation happens, the symptoms will keep arguing with each other, and the structure will continue to be safe.

Most peer-review reform fails because it treats review as a workflow problem when it is fundamentally an incentive problem.

The familiar proposals are not foolish: open reviews, double-blind review, portable reviews across journals, reviewer credit, structured rubrics, shorter forms. Many help at the margin. Yet the core system remains strained because reviewing is high-skill labor that is weakly rewarded, unevenly distributed, and performed under time scarcity by people whose careers are measured elsewhere.

Editors need fast decisions. Authors need fair evaluation. Reviewers need time, recognition, and clarity. Institutions reward publications, grants, and prestige far more directly than careful reviewing. The result is predictable: slow reports, superficial reports, overburdened conscientious reviewers, and editorial dependence on informal networks.

Structural mismatch also explains why one reform often worsens another dimension. Open review may increase accountability but reduce candor in hierarchical fields. Double-blind review may limit prestige bias but not solve methodological sloppiness. Reviewer credit may gamify visible participation without improving judgment.

The deeper difficulty is that peer review is asked to do too much: quality control, novelty detection, fraud screening, status allocation, and reputational certification. No modest process tweak can optimize all of these simultaneously.

Real reform would require changing incentives around scholarly labor itself: funding reviewing time, reducing journal prestige bottlenecks, strengthening post-publication review, improving editorial triage, and separating “technically sound” from “field-defining” more clearly. Until the academy rewards review as serious intellectual work rather than volunteer tribute to the publication machine, reform will remain mostly ornamental.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a recommendation letter from a former PhD advisor for a candidate they support but have one specific reservation worth naming.

expected_answers.answer:
Dear Committee,

I write in strong support of Dr. Yusuf El-Sayed's application for the assistant professor position in your department. Yusuf was my doctoral student from 2019 to 2024, and I supervised his dissertation, which is the most technically ambitious piece of work I have advised in fifteen years. The third chapter, in particular, is going to be a paper that the field will be reading in ten years, and I am not, as several of you who know me can confirm, prone to that kind of claim.

I want to be useful to you rather than merely positive, so I will name the one reservation I have, because I think it is the kind of thing that is better discussed than glossed.

Yusuf is, in my honest assessment, currently a better researcher than a teacher. This is not a complaint about his teaching, which his evaluations describe as careful and well-prepared. It is an observation that his strongest teaching mode, at this stage, is the small-group seminar with motivated students, and his weaker mode is the large undergraduate lecture with mixed motivation. He knows this. We have discussed it. He has, in his last year, taken specific steps — including auditing a colleague's large course and attending a teaching workshop — that I expect will make a real difference, but the difference is not yet fully visible in the record you will see.

I mention this because in five years you will not hire the researcher you see today; you will hire the colleague he becomes. On that horizon, I have unusual confidence in him.

Please feel free to call me.

With best regards,
M. Halverson

To the Search Committee,

I am pleased to recommend Dr. Maya Chen for the assistant professor position in computational biology. Maya completed her PhD under my supervision and is among the most intellectually disciplined researchers I have advised.

Her dissertation combined probabilistic modeling with unusually careful biological interpretation. She does not treat datasets as abstractions detached from experimental provenance; she asks where the measurements came from, what assumptions preprocessing introduced, and which conclusions the evidence can actually bear. That habit will make her a strong independent scholar.

Maya has also become an excellent collaborator. In our lab’s joint work with the oncology group, she repeatedly translated across disciplinary vocabularies without flattening either side. Junior students seek her out because she explains rigorously without performing superiority.

I do want to name one reservation candidly. Maya’s standards are high enough that she can sometimes hold a manuscript longer than is strategically wise, revising after the central contribution is already clear. This is not indecision or avoidance; it is conscientiousness carried slightly too far. She has improved substantially in distinguishing “better” from “materially better,” and I expect that growth to continue as she runs her own group.

I support her application strongly. She has originality, judgment, and the temperament to build work that lasts rather than merely accumulates. I would be glad to discuss her candidacy further.

Sincerely,  
Professor Elias Romero

queries.prompt: Write 350 words arguing that the contemporary American obsession with productivity is theological in structure, not merely cultural.

expected_answers.answer:
I want to argue, in a way that does not require you to share the religious vocabulary I am about to use, that the contemporary American relationship to productivity is theological in structure, and that this is why arguments against it that are merely cultural or pragmatic have so little traction.

A theological structure has three features. It tells you what the universe is for, what a person is for, and what counts as a defensible answer to the question of whether your life so far has been well spent. The American productivity discourse satisfies all three. The universe is, on its account, a resource the value of which is realized through human transformation of it; the person is an agent whose dignity is constituted by their transformative output; and a life well spent is a life that can be defended to the imagined ledger-keeper at its end through an inventory of accomplishments. The vocabulary has been secularized — we say 'impact' or 'leverage' instead of 'works' — but the grammar is preserved with a precision that would have impressed any seventeenth-century Calvinist.

A smart critic would say I am over-reading: that productivity culture is more parsimoniously explained as the rational response of a capitalist labor market to incentives, and that no theology is required to predict the behavior. I take this seriously. It explains, however, only the supply side. It explains why employers value productivity. It does not explain why employees experience their own productivity, in private, as a question about whether they are a good person. Markets do not, by themselves, generate that affect. The affect is older than the market and is the part of the system the market is exploiting.

The argument matters because the diagnostic determines the treatment. A cultural ailment is treated with norms; a structural ailment is treated with policy; a theological ailment is treated only by recovering, or constructing, a different account of what a person is for. The first two have been tried. They have not worked. It is at least worth asking whether the third is, in the end, what is required.

The contemporary American obsession with productivity is theological in structure because it does not merely organize time; it assigns moral worth.

In older religious frameworks, a person examined the soul for signs of faithfulness. In productivity culture, one examines the calendar. Empty time becomes suspect. Rest must be justified as recovery for future output. Hobbies become side hustles or self-optimization. Even grief is asked whether it has produced insight.

The theology has doctrines. Salvation appears as mastery over friction. Sin appears as waste. Conversion comes through systems: the app, the routine, the morning protocol, the new stack of habits that will finally reconcile intention and performance. Testimony arrives in before-and-after posts. Saints are founders who sleep little and make strain look chosen.

This is not simply capitalism, though it serves capitalism well. It is existential. Productivity promises exemption from contingency. If every hour is accounted for, perhaps mortality has been answered in ledger form. Perhaps one can prove one deserved one’s life.

Defenders will say ambition is good, discipline is good, and people drift without structure. All true. The problem is not work or seriousness. It is the extension of instrumental judgment into every chamber of experience. A walk is evaluated by steps. Reading by books completed. Friendship by calendar maintenance.

A healthy culture would distinguish fruitfulness from throughput. Some of the most important human goods are inefficient: prayer, courtship, deep study, care for the old, learning an instrument badly, sitting with a child who will not sleep. Their value decays when converted too quickly into output language.

The productivity gospel is powerful because it mimics virtue. It offers order, diligence, and purpose. But beneath those good things it often hides a harsher claim: that existence must continually earn its keep. That is not efficiency. It is bad theology.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of literary fiction in the third person, present tense, about a woman in her 60s who realizes mid-grocery-store that she is no longer afraid of dying.

expected_answers.answer:
Mira is standing in front of the yogurts. There is a yogurt she has bought every Thursday for, she calculates, eleven years. She is looking at the yogurt, and she has just understood — in the way that real understanding always arrives, by accident, while one is doing something else — that she is no longer afraid of dying.
