It is not relief. Relief is a thing the body feels and her body is, at the moment, doing nothing in particular. It is more the way she once understood, also in a grocery store, that she did not love her first husband. The understanding had arrived in the cereal aisle, and the cereal aisle had not changed. The cereal aisle had simply been the place where she had finally been quiet enough to hear what she had known for some time.

She picks up the yogurt. She puts it in the cart. The yogurt has not changed either.

She wonders, briefly, whether she should tell someone. She decides not to. The thing about a private resolution is that it does not survive description; she has watched other people describe theirs and watched the resolution leave the room as the description filled it.

At the checkout the cashier — a young woman she does not know but has, she now realizes, seen often — asks how her day is going. Mira says it is going well. The cashier says, that's wonderful. Mira pays. She walks to the car. The light, in the parking lot, is the light a Thursday afternoon in October normally has, and Mira drives home.

In aisle seven, between canned tomatoes and olive oil, Judith realizes she is no longer afraid of dying.

The thought arrives without thunder. She is comparing two jars of pasta sauce, irritated that the smaller one costs more per ounce, when a sentence forms quietly inside her: *It will happen, and that is all right.*

She sets one jar in the cart. Basil garlic. Her husband dislikes chunks, but he is away visiting his brother and she feels briefly entitled to texture.

For years death had waited nearby as a locked room she refused to picture. After her first biopsy. After Carl’s heart scare. During the months when both her parents seemed to become fragile at once. She had imagined the end as interruption, insult, theft.

Now it seems less like a thief than weather beyond the next hill.

Not welcome. She is not foolish. She wants more mornings, more coffee too hot to drink, more complaints from her sister, more spring bulbs appearing where she forgot planting them. But wanting to continue no longer feels identical to being unable to stop.

A young father steers a cart past her, one hand on the handle, one holding a box of cereal above a toddler’s reach. The child shrieks with theatrical despair. Judith nearly laughs.

She wonders whether courage sometimes arrives after it is needed, like a guest with bad directions.

At checkout, the cashier asks if she found everything.

“Yes,” Judith says, and is startled by how nearly true it sounds.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word in-character speech from a small-town mayor who has decided to publicly oppose a popular development project that would bring jobs but damage the town's character.

expected_answers.answer:
Good evening. Thank you for coming. I see most of the faces I expected to see, and a few I didn't, and I'm grateful to all of you for taking the time.

I want to tell you, as directly as I can, that I am going to vote no on the Hartwell development, and I want to tell you why, because I owe you that and because I know the decision is not the one many of you came here hoping I would make.

The Hartwell project would bring approximately 340 jobs to this town. I have looked at those numbers carefully. They are real numbers. I am not going to insult you by pretending they don't matter. Three hundred and forty jobs in a town this size is not an abstraction. It is people who would, this time next year, have work they don't currently have. I have spent the last six months trying to find a version of this vote in which I could say yes to those people, and I could not get there honestly, and I want to tell you what stopped me.

What stopped me is that the project requires the rezoning of the Bell's Crossing watershed, and the rezoning is irreversible in the sense that no future council, however much it might regret what we do tonight, will be able to undo it. Some decisions can be revisited. This one can't. And when I tried, in private, to imagine the town twenty years from now after the rezoning — not the jobs, the town — I could not find a version of that town that the people who built this place would still recognize as the thing they had built. That is a sentence I would not say lightly. I am not saying it lightly.

I want to say one more thing, and then I will take questions. I know that for some of you the jobs are not a number. They are your mortgage, or your kid, or the reason you stayed when you could have left. I have not forgotten that, and I will not pretend my vote tonight is costless for you. I am not asking you to agree with me. I am asking you to believe that I have understood what I am voting against.

I will take your questions now.

Friends, neighbors,

I know many of you support the Riverbend project. I understand why. It promises construction jobs, a broader tax base, and new retail activity in a town that has watched too many young people leave. Those are not imaginary benefits, and anyone opposing the project owes you more than sentiment.

I have reviewed the proposal carefully. I have listened to business owners, planners, teachers, farmers, and families who live near the site. I have also asked what kind of town we are being asked to become in exchange for these gains.

The current plan would place a large mixed-use complex at the edge of our historic corridor, strain roads that are already near capacity during school traffic, and replace land that gives this town part of its identity with a development whose design could belong almost anywhere. The projected jobs matter. So do the costs that do not fit neatly into a ribbon-cutting speech.

I am not anti-growth. I am against mistaking any growth for good growth. A town can lose itself by refusing every change. It can also lose itself by accepting the first large check offered by people who will not live with the consequences.

Therefore I will oppose the project in its present form.

I am asking the council to invite a revised proposal: smaller footprint, stronger architectural standards, real traffic mitigation, preservation commitments, and enforceable local hiring language rather than promotional promises. If the developer believes in this town as much as its presentations say, it can meet us with more care.

Some will call this obstruction. I call it stewardship. Our responsibility is not only to increase activity. It is to hand forward a place worth belonging to.

We should welcome opportunity. We should not rent out our judgment to receive it.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of analytical commentary on why the standard Western framing of 'authenticity' in art is a 19th-century invention with specific consequences.

expected_answers.answer:
It is taken for granted, in contemporary Western art criticism, that 'authenticity' is a relevant and largely positive evaluative term — that one of the questions a serious critic asks of a work is whether it is the genuine expression of its maker. The category feels natural enough that it is easy to forget it is roughly 200 years old. Authenticity in this sense is a 19th-century invention, emerging out of Romantic-era ideas about the individual artistic self as the privileged source of meaning. Most of the world's art, including most of the Western canon prior to roughly 1800, was not made under this concept and cannot be coherently evaluated by it. A Byzantine icon, a Mughal miniature, a medieval altarpiece — these are works the modern critic still evaluates, but evaluates with a vocabulary the works themselves would not have recognized, and the vocabulary obscures more than it reveals.

A smart counter is that this is a historicist quibble: even if the category is recent, it might still be the correct one, and the older works might be evaluable in the older terms while the newer works are evaluated in the newer. There is something to this. But the counter understates how much the authenticity frame has, in practice, narrowed what counts as serious work. It privileges art that foregrounds the individual maker — the signed canvas, the autobiographical novel, the auteur film — and underweights forms in which individual authorship is genuinely subordinated to a tradition, a workshop, or a community. A great deal of contemporary practice — collective work, anonymous digital labor, certain craft and devotional traditions — is read as deficient under the authenticity frame, when in fact those works are operating on assumptions the frame was not built to score.

The useful move is not to abandon the concept but to denaturalize it: to remember that it is a tool, with a history, designed to do certain work and ill-suited to other work, and to bring the appropriate tool to the appropriate object.

The modern Western ideal of artistic “authenticity” is not timeless. It is heavily shaped by nineteenth-century Romanticism, which elevated originality, inwardness, expressive sincerity, and the singular artist-genius against academic convention and mechanical repetition.

Earlier artistic cultures often valued mastery within inherited forms, workshop production, patron demands, devotional function, and the refinement of established motifs. A Renaissance altarpiece was not disqualified by collaboration. A court poem did not become false because it served occasion. The Romantic inheritance changed the question from “Is this work excellent within a tradition?” to “Does this work issue from an irreducibly individual self?”

That shift produced real gains. It expanded the legitimacy of personal vision, dissenting form, and art as self-disclosure. It helped make room for modernism and for artists whose work refused official taste.

But it also created distortions. We now too easily equate collaboration with compromise, craft with inauthenticity, popularity with suspicion, and polished public self-presentation with bad faith. The cult of authenticity encourages biographies to overshadow works and asks art to perform confession even where art’s intelligence lies in invention, impersonality, or formal construction.

This has market consequences too. “Authentic” becomes a sales category, especially for marginalized artists, who may be rewarded for legible identity expression while being constrained by it.

Authenticity is not useless. Some works are false to their materials, their social claims, or their own emotional register. But the concept should be historicized. Much of what we call authentic is not a universal criterion of art; it is a Romantic moral preference still masquerading as common sense.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words from a software engineer two weeks after leaving a major tech company after 12 years. Not bitter, not nostalgic.

expected_answers.answer:
Two weeks out, and the thing I have noticed first is not relief and not regret but the strange unfamiliarity of unstructured attention. Twelve years is a long time to have one's days shaped by a calendar somebody else's software has been arranging.

I want to say what I think the twelve years were. I think they were, on balance, well spent, and I would like to be specific about what I mean by that, because I am suspicious of the way ex-employees describe their time at large companies. The narrative one is invited to adopt — the company was a brilliant adventure, or the company was a soul-flattening machine — is, in both versions, too clean. The truer thing is more boring.

For twelve years I worked with a fairly large number of people who were, in their actual day-to-day behavior, smart and kind. I worked on systems whose scale gave me a sense of the world I would not have had otherwise. I learned, slowly and incompletely, that the difference between a good engineer at year three and a good engineer at year twelve is mostly the accumulated experience of having been wrong in a sufficient number of specific ways. I also, for non-trivial stretches, did work whose connection to anything I cared about I could not, when honest with myself, defend. I shipped things I am proud of. I shipped things I am not. The company shipped things I am proud of. The company shipped things that have done damage that the people who shipped them, including me, did not, at the time, expect.

The accounting, in other words, is mixed, and I am suspicious of anyone whose accounting is not. I do not yet know what I am going to do next. I know that the question has, for the first time in twelve years, become my own.

Two weeks after leaving, I still wake before my alarm as if a standup might begin without me.

Twelve years is long enough for a company’s rhythms to enter the body. Release calendars. Planning seasons. The peculiar language people use when everyone knows a project is late but no one is ready to say so directly. I do not miss all of it. I do miss some of the people more than I expected.

Leaving has clarified what the job gave me. I learned to build systems that survive other people’s use. I learned that technical correctness without organizational legibility is fragile. I learned which arguments matter and which are only territorial weather. I was paid well to solve interesting problems with smart colleagues. That is not nothing.

I also see what the company took, or what I volunteered because it was easier than drawing a line. Attention fragmented into channels. Evenings partially occupied by issues that could technically wait. A gradual confusion between urgency and importance. No one forced me to make every tradeoff I made, which is uncomfortable and useful to admit.

I am not bitter. Bitterness would simplify too much. I am not nostalgic either. Nostalgia would edit out the parts I was right to leave.

Mostly, I feel scale changing. I can finish a thought without someone tagging me into another. I can decide whether a problem deserves today. That freedom is pleasant and vaguely alarming.

People ask what is next. I have answers ready, but the truer answer is that I am trying not to convert the exit immediately into another productivity narrative. After twelve years inside a large machine, perhaps two weeks of not knowing is not a gap. It is recovery.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a fictional 17th-century letter from a scientist to a colleague, gently challenging an experimental result. Period-appropriate voice.

expected_answers.answer:
Dear Friend,

I have read, with the attention your work has long deserved of me, the account you sent in February of the experiments at H——, and I have read it twice, and I beg you will receive what follows in the spirit in which it is offered, which is the spirit of one who would rather be corrected by you than agree with you in error.

The difficulty I have, and which I have asked two of my own students to repeat with the apparatus we have here, is with the third trial, in which the result was so much beyond the expectation that I confess I should have suspected my own measurement before I trusted it. I do not, of course, suspect yours. I do, however, wish to know whether the apparatus was at that hour the same as at the previous trials, whether the water was drawn from the same well, and whether young M—— was present, of whom you have spoken before, and whose recording of the times I have always thought (forgive me) a little prone to the optimism of youth.

If the result is as you describe, I shall be obliged to revise my whole understanding of the matter, and shall do so without reluctance, for I am old enough now to have learned that a man's understanding is no kinder to him for being defended against the facts. But I should like, before I revise, to be certain.

With my warmest regards to your wife and to your excellent table,
I remain, etc.,
Your humble servant,
T. Brun

Most learned and esteemed Sir,

I received with gratitude your account of the vapours observed in the sealed vessel and read it twice with admiration for the care of your contrivance. Yet I hope you will forgive a friendly hesitation concerning the conclusion you draw, namely that the change proceeded solely from the nature of the enclosed air.

Might not some part of the effect arise from heat retained in the glass itself, or from a residue left by the former substance, though washed as thoroughly as ordinary practice allows? I mention this not to diminish your experiment, which is ingenious, but because the result is important enough to deserve every troublesome doubt.

If it please you, I would ask whether the same appearance follows when a second vessel of another maker is employed, and whether the quantity of the vapour alters when the vessel is cooled for a longer interval before sealing. I intend no captious dispute. I have too often mistaken a faithful servant for a principal cause in my own trials.

Your observation may well stand. I only submit that Nature sometimes yields an answer to the hand we did not know we had extended.

With sincere respect and friendship,  
Your obedient servant,  
E. Marchand

queries.prompt: Synthesize: write a 500-word argument that the most important moral skill for the 21st century is the capacity to hold two true things at once.

expected_answers.answer:
There is a moral skill I find myself watching for, in people whose judgment I have come to trust, and it is the same skill in all of them. It is the capacity to hold two true things at once.

The skill is unfashionable because it is easily confused with weakness. A person who can say both 'X' and 'Y' when X and Y appear to point in different directions is suspected, in our current discourse, of evasion, of trying to please everyone, of failing to take a stand. The suspicion is sometimes correct. There is a great deal of false equivalence in public conversation. But the suspicion is, more often than the suspicion knows, simply wrong. It mistakes a mature epistemic state for cowardice. The two are not the same thing, and one of the projects of the next several decades is going to be learning to tell them apart, because almost every problem that actually faces us requires the capacity I am describing.

Consider the contemporary problem of how to think about a difficult international conflict. The honest map of such a territory includes propositions that, to many participants in the discourse, appear to be in irreconcilable tension: that a population has a deep and legitimate connection to a piece of land, and that another population already living there experienced a catastrophe whose effects continue. That an act of mass violence is genuinely indefensible, and that the military response to it has produced civilian suffering that ought to be intolerable to anyone whose ethics are not contingent on the identity of the sufferer. That civilians on each side have the right not to be killed.

Each of these propositions can be true at once. Holding all of them at once is uncomfortable. The discomfort is the price of the truth. The shortcut available to almost any participant — selecting the subset of the propositions that supports the position one was already going to take, and treating the remainder as either propaganda or naïveté — is widely taken and widely encouraged. The shortcut produces, in the aggregate, the discourse we have, in which the participants increasingly believe that the other participants do not deserve to be talked to.

The skill of holding two true things at once is not a substitute for moral clarity. It is the precondition for it. A moral clarity that has not survived the encounter with the strongest version of the opposing view is not clarity; it is an absence of imagination. The clarity that matters is the clarity that has read its opposition with full sympathy, conceded what is true in it, and arrived at its conclusion anyway, knowing what the conclusion costs.

This skill is not innate. It is teachable. It is teachable by example, by reading writers who exemplify it, by sitting with disagreements rather than resolving them prematurely. I do not think we are, as a culture, currently very good at it. I think it is the central skill the next generation will have to learn.

The most important moral skill for the twenty-first century may be the ability to hold two true things at once without rushing to dissolve the tension.

A powerful technology can expand human capability and concentrate power dangerously. A nation can commit grave wrongs and still contain traditions worth defending. A parent can love a child and harm that child. A protest movement can name an injustice accurately and endorse foolish remedies. A person can be responsible for choices made under constraints they did not choose.

Public life increasingly punishes this capacity. Digital argument rewards compression into camps. Institutions issue statements before they understand events. Moral seriousness is confused with immediate total alignment. Complexity is treated as evasion because complexity slows mobilization.

But many of the worst errors come from preserving one truth by denying another. If we recognize structural causes of poverty but erase agency, we patronize people. If we insist on agency but deny structure, we moralize misfortune. If we protect free speech without acknowledging intimidation, we become naïve. If we regulate speech without acknowledging power misuse, we become authoritarian.

Holding two truths is not centrism. It does not mean splitting the difference or refusing judgment. Sometimes one truth outweighs another decisively in action. The skill lies earlier: seeing the full moral field before choosing.

This capacity matters more now because modern systems are interdependent and second-order effects are everywhere. Climate transitions can be necessary and locally painful. Medical triage can be compassionate and unjust in distribution. AI can improve access to expertise and degrade epistemic trust. Serious citizens must be able to say “both” before they say “therefore.”

The skill is difficult because contradiction makes us anxious. We prefer innocence. We want causes without costs and victims without agency and heroes without mixed motives. Reality rarely cooperates.

Maturity begins when we stop asking which true thing we may keep and instead ask how to act responsibly under the weight of several. That is not moral weakness. It is the beginning of moral accuracy.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words connecting Wittgenstein's notion of 'aspect-seeing' to the way doctors learn to read radiographs.

expected_answers.answer:
Wittgenstein's discussion of 'aspect-seeing' in the second part of the Philosophical Investigations turns on a small experiential fact: that one can see the same figure first as one thing (the duck) and then, suddenly, as another (the rabbit), without anything in the figure having changed. The shift is not in the world. It is also not, in any reportable sense, in the mind doing something extra. It is, Wittgenstein says, that we see the figure differently — and the verb 'see' here is doing work the verb does not do in ordinary contexts. There is, in this kind of seeing, a knowing-that interleaved with the perceiving.

A practicing radiologist will recognize the phenomenon immediately, although the literature of medical pedagogy does not, on the whole, name it. The radiologist's training is, in significant part, the training of aspect-seeing. A first-year resident shown a chest film sees, more or less, the same image as the senior radiologist. What changes is not the image but the aspects under which the image is available to be seen. The resident sees ribs, heart, lung fields. The senior radiologist sees a subtle asymmetry in the costophrenic angle that is, given the patient's history, the relevant finding of the film. The asymmetry was there on the resident's film as well. The resident did not see it because the resident did not yet possess the conceptual framework under which it would be the relevant finding.

This has a sharp consequence for how we should think about expert perception. Expertise is, on this account, not added 'on top of' perception, as a kind of cognitive elaboration. It restructures perception itself. The expert is not perceiving the same world the novice perceives and merely thinking about it more cleverly. The expert is in a different perceptual world, in the precise sense that the perceptual objects available to them — the relevant findings, the patterns that organize the field — are not available to the novice as objects of attention.

Wittgenstein’s notion of aspect-seeing describes the moment when the same visual field is suddenly grasped differently: the duck-rabbit becomes rabbit rather than duck, though no line on the page changes. The shift is perceptual, but not merely optical. It depends on concepts, training, and readiness.

Radiology has something of this structure. A novice sees a chest film as gray complexity. An experienced physician sees a subtle opacity in the right lower lobe, then immediately sees possible consolidation, atelectasis, or artifact. The pixels have not changed. The aspect has.

This does not mean diagnosis is subjective whim. Training disciplines aspect-seeing. Doctors learn relevant contrasts, normal variants, dangerous absences, expected symmetries, and the patterns by which disease announces itself. What appears “obvious” to the expert is often the endpoint of thousands of prior acts of guided attention.

Wittgenstein helps explain why radiographic education cannot be reduced to memorizing rules. Rules matter, but the learner must acquire a form of seeing. Attend here, not there. Notice this edge. Ask why this space is too white, this contour too blunt, this structure missing.

It also explains diagnostic risk. Once an aspect locks in, alternatives can recede. The image “looks like pneumonia,” and the possibility of malignancy receives less attention. Clinical judgment therefore requires both skilled seeing and the ability to loosen one’s own first seeing.

The radiograph is stable. The trained gaze is what changes.

queries.prompt: Write a 250-word self-portrait by a fictional 78-year-old retired electrician. The reader should believe the speaker is real.

expected_answers.answer:
I worked forty-two years as an electrician. I started in '67 with my uncle, who was the kind of man you don't see anymore, and not entirely for good reasons. I retired in 2009, which was a bad year to retire on account of the markets, but I had been careful with what I had, and so I came out of it.

I was married once. We were married twenty-nine years. She passed in 2015. We had two children, both of whom turned out, I think, more or less all right. My daughter is a nurse in Ohio. My son sells something I don't fully understand on a computer in California. We talk most Sundays.

I like the morning. I drink coffee and I read the paper, the actual paper, which gets thinner every year and which I will keep getting until they stop printing it. I work in my garage three or four days a week, on things that don't need to be worked on, but which I work on anyway, because the alternative, at 78, is sitting.

I'm not afraid of dying. I'm afraid, a little, of needing somebody. That's a different fear. I keep it in its own drawer.

If you'd asked me, at 25, what I thought I'd amount to, I would have said something larger than what I amounted to. I would have been wrong, and not in the direction I was worried about. What I amounted to is fine. It's more than fine. I just didn't know, at 25, what fine looked like.

That's most of it.

I am seventy-eight years old and still notice outlets in other people’s houses before I notice the pictures on the wall. Occupational damage.

I spent forty-four years as an electrician. Residential at first, then schools, grocery stores, two hospitals, and one miserable courthouse renovation where nothing was square and everyone blamed the last trade in the room. I liked work that had to function whether anyone admired it or not.

People think wiring is about force. It is mostly about patience. Strip the insulation cleanly. Label the panel. Do not leave a junction box hidden because you are tired and drywall is coming. Someone after you will open what you closed. Leave them evidence that you respected them.

I was not always patient at home. My wife would confirm that if she were here. I could trace a fault through three walls and still fail to hear that my daughter wanted comfort, not instructions. Retirement has made certain things embarrassingly plain.

My hands are stiff now. I do small repairs for neighbors, slower than before, and I refuse ladders I once climbed without thought. I miss the crew sometimes: bad coffee, winter jobs, the relief when a dark building came alive all at once.

I do not think I built a grand life. I made many ordinary things safe. Lights came on. Breakers held. Children sat in classrooms warm enough to learn. That seems sufficient to me most days.

queries.prompt: Write 400 words of fictional insider commentary on what it is actually like to run a US presidential campaign.

expected_answers.answer:
People assume a campaign is a strategy that is executed. It is not. A campaign is a small group of exhausted people making a thousand decisions a week with insufficient information, hoping that the cumulative drift of those decisions points roughly in a direction. The strategy document on the wall is a comfort object. It is consulted in the way an experienced sailor consults a chart in a storm — not as a substitute for the actual feel of the weather, but as one input among many, most of which are coming through her feet.

The second misunderstanding is about the candidate. Outsiders imagine the candidate as the protagonist of the campaign. The candidate is, in fact, one of the campaign's hardest-to-manage resources. The candidate's time is the single most precious commodity the campaign possesses, and almost everyone within and around the campaign believes their request for a slice of it is uniquely justified. Half the strategist's job is saying no. The other half is saying no without making the no-recipient an enemy for the next eight years.

The third is about polls. Outsiders treat polls as snapshots of the truth; insiders treat them as instruments whose calibration drifts, whose signal is partly genuine and partly artifact, and whose chief use is not to predict but to detect changes in the relative position over time. The number itself matters less than the second derivative.

The fourth, and the one I find hardest to convey, is the role of accident. A general election is shaped, to a significant degree, by events neither campaign chose: a debate moment, a market drop, an international crisis, a sentence the candidate misspoke on hour fourteen of a travel day. The campaign's job is not to prevent accidents — you cannot prevent them — but to be in a position to absorb them when they arrive. Most of what looks, from the outside, like strategic brilliance is actually the residue of having maintained, for fourteen months, the kind of organizational health that lets you metabolize a bad week without falling apart. The most strategic thing a campaign can do, in many weeks, is rest its principal.

The last thing — and I will be brief — is that the campaign ends. It ends and the people who have given a year of their lives to it scatter, and the things they cared about are inherited, mostly, by people who were not there. This is the part nobody warns you about.

Running a presidential campaign is like trying to repair an airplane while millions of people argue about whether flight itself is elitist.

From outside, campaigns look like message wars and rallies. Inside, they are logistics operations under emotional siege. Every day contains three clocks: the candidate’s body, the news cycle, and the money clock. None respect the others.

You spend mornings studying polling you publicly call meaningless, afternoons arguing over whether a phrase will sound defensive in a clipped video, and evenings reassuring donors that the path remains clear when the path has become a goat trail in fog. The candidate is simultaneously a human being, a principal, a symbol, and a schedule that refuses natural law.

Good campaigns are not run by geniuses improvising slogans. They are run by people who can make decisions with incomplete information and not reopen them five times. Field wants more resources. Paid media wants more resources. Digital wants more resources. Everyone is right. The job is choosing which right answer gets less money.

The public imagines strategy meetings as high chess. Many are closer to triage. Is the scandal real or twelve hours of noise? Do we respond or starve it? Is the candidate tired or off-message? Are we losing rural margins, suburban women, young Black men, or merely our nerve?

The darkest secret is that campaigns attract people who care about democracy and people who care about winning, and the overlap is large but not total. Under pressure, those motives become distinguishable.

Election night clarifies less than people think. If you win, every compromise is retold as strategy. If you lose, every compromise becomes evidence of cowardice. In both cases, the story hardens before anyone has learned enough.

What it is actually like is this: very smart people spend eighteen months making urgent judgments about a country too large to fully understand, while one person’s face carries the consequence. It is exhilarating. It is deforming. It is never as controlled as the documentary later suggests.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of literary fiction in which estranged adult siblings meet after twelve years to clean out their late mother's apartment. The emotional work should come from one small object.

expected_answers.answer:
They had agreed by email to meet at the apartment at ten. He was early. She was on time, which was, of the two of them, the harder thing.

They worked in the kitchen first, because the kitchen was the room neither of them had strong feelings about. He took the cabinets above the stove; she took the cabinets below. They did not, in the first hour, speak of anything except dishes.

He was, by eleven, in the drawer near the phone. He pulled out a takeout menu, a stack of expired coupons, a single sock, and, near the bottom, a small ceramic frog about the size of his thumb.

He stopped.

The frog had a chip on its back left leg where, in 1993, his sister had dropped it, picked it up, and announced — with the conviction of an eight-year-old — that she had glued it and that it was, now, completely fine. He had teased her about the glue job for fifteen years. He had not seen the frog in twenty.

'Hey,' he said.

She came around the counter and looked.

'Oh,' she said.

He held it out. She took it.

'She kept it.'

'She kept it,' he said.

Neither of them said the obvious thing, which was that their mother had kept it through three moves, and had put it, deliberately, in a drawer she opened every day.

She turned the frog over in her hand. She did not put it down. She did not, after a minute, look at him, although she nodded once, mostly to herself.

'Okay,' she said.

They went back to work. The frog stayed on the counter between them, where, all afternoon, neither of them moved it.

The sugar bowl is what undoes them.

Not the framed photographs they divide without looking closely. Not the blue armchair neither of them wants because it smells faintly of their mother’s lavender lotion. Not even the box of sympathy cards stacked unopened on the sideboard.

It is the sugar bowl, chipped at the rim, shaped like a small white pumpkin.

“I’ll take that,” Claire says.

Jon looks up from a drawer of receipts. “Why?”

“Because I want it.”

“That wasn’t the question.”

She puts it beside her purse. Twelve years evaporate badly. They are sixteen and nineteen again, experts at making the other person sound unreasonable.

“Mom used it every morning,” Claire says.

“She used the blue mug every morning too. You don’t want that.”

The lid rattles as she removes it. Inside is one folded yellow receipt, old and softened. Claire opens it. Grocery store, 2008. Tea, bread, sugar, aspirin.

On the back, in their mother’s handwriting: *Call Jonathan after dinner. Don’t let Claire say she is fine.*

Claire reads it twice. Jon reaches for the paper, then stops and lets her hand it over.

Neither speaks.

Their mother had become vague in later years, then difficult, then fragile enough to excuse nearly everything and clarify nothing. Claire had believed she was the child who stayed, Jon the child who fled. Jon had believed Claire and their mother formed a closed country from which he had long been exiled.

“Did she call?” Claire asks.

Jon shakes his head. “I don’t remember.”

“That means maybe.”

“That means I don’t remember.”

She almost argues, then doesn’t.

Outside, someone drags a trash bin down the alley. The sound lasts longer than it should.

Jon folds the receipt again, not along the old crease exactly, but close. “You take the bowl.”

Claire nods. “You take this?”

He thinks. “No. Keep them together.”

For the first time all afternoon, they choose the same thing without negotiation.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word engineering postmortem about a 4-hour outage caused by an interaction between a new feature flag and an old retry policy. Appropriate register.

expected_answers.answer:
Summary. Between 14:02 and 18:11 UTC on the day of incident, the order-submission service returned 5xx errors for an estimated 31% of submissions. Root cause was an interaction between feature flag `order_v2_pipeline` (rolled to 25% at 13:58 UTC) and the existing client-side retry policy on the order-submission RPC, which had not been updated since 2019.

Mechanism. The new pipeline path returned a different error code (HTTP 503) than the legacy path for a class of validation failures that, on the legacy path, returned HTTP 400. The client retry library, configured in 2019 with the assumption that 503 was always transient, retried the failing requests on a 200ms exponential backoff. Each retry, by re-entering the load balancer pool, was routed by hash-of-request-id to a backend running the new path with 25% probability, repeating the failure. The retry storm raised the effective load on the new path 4–7x within 90 seconds, which cascaded into pool exhaustion across the broader fleet.

Response. Feature flag was rolled back at 17:54. Service recovered to baseline error rate by 18:11.

Lessons. (1) Behavior-changing flags must declare which error semantics they alter and be reviewed against existing client retry policies before rollout. (2) Client retry libraries older than 24 months are presumed misaligned with current service contracts and must be reviewed during the same change. (3) Load tests for new pipeline paths must include the retry-amplification scenario, not just clean-load behavior.

Actions. (See attached tracker — owners and dates assigned for each lesson; review at next ops review in two weeks.)

## Incident Summary

On May 4, from 09:12 to 13:17 UTC, checkout requests experienced elevated error rates and intermittent timeouts. Approximately 28% of checkout attempts failed or exceeded client timeout thresholds during the incident window.

## What Happened

A newly introduced feature flag enabled an alternate pricing-service code path for 10% of traffic. That path returned a transient `503` under a dependency-specific condition. An older retry policy in the checkout service treated this response as broadly retryable and retried immediately up to five times without jitter or concurrency limits.

The interaction created request amplification:
- each affected checkout generated multiple immediate retries
- retry traffic increased pressure on the pricing service
- pricing-service latency rose
- more calls crossed timeout thresholds
- checkout workers accumulated blocked requests
- the error rate spread beyond the flagged cohort

## Root Cause

The primary root cause was an unsafe interaction between:
1. a feature flag that activated a new dependency path without sufficient load/failure-mode validation, and
2. a legacy retry policy that was too aggressive for synchronous checkout traffic.

Neither change alone would likely have caused a four-hour outage. Their composition did.

## Contributing Factors

- No explicit retry budget or exponential backoff with jitter
- Feature-flag rollout monitoring focused on business metrics, not dependency saturation
- Alerting detected checkout failure rate but not rapid retry amplification
- The old retry policy had not been revisited after service topology changed

## Resolution

- Disabled the feature flag at 13:02 UTC
- Reduced retry count and introduced exponential backoff with jitter
- Restarted saturated checkout workers after load normalized

## Corrective Actions

1. Add retry budgets and idempotency-aware retry guidance to service standards.
2. Require flag rollouts to define technical guardrail metrics.
3. Add dashboards for retry rate, amplification factor, and dependency saturation.
4. Review all synchronous retry policies in tier-1 request paths.
5. Add failure-injection testing for flagged code paths before broad rollout.

## Lessons

Feature flags reduce deployment risk only when the activated path is observed as carefully as a production release. Retries improve resilience only when they do not turn partial failure into self-inflicted overload.

queries.prompt: Write 350 words from a retiring high-school English teacher to the teacher who will inherit her classroom. Specific advice, not platitudes.

expected_answers.answer:
Dear Whoever You Are,

The classroom is yours now. A few specific things, in no particular order, that I wish I had been told.

The second drawer of the desk on the right sticks in humid weather. If you push it in too hard it will jam and you will spend twenty minutes you don't have prying it open with a ruler. There is a small piece of cardboard taped to the inside of the back of the drawer; leave it. It is not a relic. It is what keeps the drawer working.

Do not, on the first day, give the brilliant lesson. Save it for the third week. The students cannot, on the first day, tell that the lesson is brilliant. They are deciding whether the room is going to be safe. Decide the room is going to be safe first. The brilliance has nowhere to land otherwise.

The 11th-grade copies of Their Eyes Were Watching God on the bottom shelf are the unmarked ones. The 12th-grade copies are in the cabinet, and most of them have a previous student's handwriting in the margins. Some of the handwriting is very good. I let it stay. You may decide differently. I am not telling you to do as I did.

There will be, in your second year, a student you cannot reach. You will spend a great deal of time wondering whether you have failed them. Most of the time you will not have failed them. Most of the time something is happening in their life that is larger than your class, and the most honest thing you can do is keep the class a place they are allowed to be quiet in. Sometimes the quiet is what they came for.

Finally: do not be afraid to say, in front of them, 'I don't know.' I have learned that this is the single most important sentence in the room. If you can say it without performance, they will, eventually, learn to say it too. Almost everything else follows from that.

Good luck. The room is a good room. I have loved it.

— M.

Dear whoever comes next,

The room looks ordinary when it is empty. It is not. The left window sticks in February. The third desk in the second row wobbles unless you fold paper under one leg. The projector takes ten seconds longer than you think, which matters when twenty-six teenagers are deciding whether your opening has authority.

Do not spend September proving you are serious. They will know soon enough. Spend it learning names correctly, including the ones that make you slow down.

Keep the poetry unit, even if scores pressure you toward safer ground. Some students who resist essays will hear a poem before they admit they can. Read aloud more than feels efficient. Language enters by ear long before analysis improves it.

The cabinet contains old class sets, some useful, some kept because I lacked the nerve to discard them. You should have that nerve. Keep *Macbeth*. Keep Baldwin. Ask harder questions about the rest.

There is a student every year who turns in sharp work late and indifferent work on time. Do not confuse that pattern with laziness before you understand the life around it. There is also a student who charms adults to avoid learning. Do not confuse charm with progress.

Call home earlier than you want to. Praise specifically. Criticize specifically. “Has potential” is what teachers say when they have not chosen a verb.

You will leave school some days certain you taught nothing. That feeling is not reliable. Neither is the opposite.

I hope you make this room yours. I hope you improve on me. If a student asks why the copy of *Their Eyes Were Watching God* has penciled stars beside chapter six, tell them the previous teacher was sentimental, but not entirely wrong.

With respect,  
M. Alvarez

queries.prompt: Write 350 words of expert commentary by a constitutional law scholar on why originalism is both more sophisticated than critics typically allow and more limited than defenders typically claim.

expected_answers.answer:
Originalism, as a serious constitutional theory, has had bad luck in being known mostly through its caricatures. The caricature on the left is that originalism is a code word for substantively conservative outcomes dressed up as historical method; the caricature on the right is that originalism is the only theory that respects the rule of law, and that all alternatives reduce to judges imposing their personal preferences. Both caricatures are wrong in interesting ways.

On the sophistication: serious originalism is not the claim that we should interpret the Constitution according to the subjective intentions of specific framers — that version, original intent, has largely been abandoned. Mainstream originalism today is 'original public meaning': the claim that constitutional text means what it meant to a reasonably informed reader at the time of ratification. This is a substantive philosophical claim about how legal texts function, and it has serious arguments behind it. Legal texts are written to communicate; they communicate in the conventions of the language at the time of writing; reading them through later linguistic conventions risks attributing to the text meanings the drafters could not have communicated. Whatever else one thinks of originalism, this argument is not silly.

On the limits: the theory does much less work than its defenders typically claim. First, the historical record is often radically underdetermined. The original public meaning of many constitutional provisions — especially the open-textured ones (cruel and unusual, due process, equal protection) — is, on careful examination, genuinely unclear; the same originalist sources can be invoked on either side of a contemporary dispute. Second, the level of generality at which one identifies the original meaning is itself a choice not dictated by the theory; deciding whether 'equal protection' meant the specific protections contemplated in 1868 or the general principle of equality before law is not a historical question but a theoretical one. Third, the theory has no general account of how to handle constitutional change short of formal amendment, and the formal amendment process is, by design, almost impossibly difficult.

The honest summary is that originalism is one defensible theory among several, with real arguments and real limits.

Originalism is more sophisticated than critics often allow because serious originalists are not usually claiming that eighteenth-century expectations mechanically resolve every modern dispute. The stronger theory asks judges to interpret constitutional text according to its original public meaning, while recognizing that application to unforeseen circumstances may require judgment. On that account, originalism offers a constraint on judicial willfulness and a principled reason to distinguish interpretation from amendment.

Critics sometimes attack a caricature: judges consulting powdered-wig intentions to decide smartphones. That misses decades of methodological refinement, especially the shift from original intent to original public meaning and the recognition that broad clauses can have broad original meanings.

Yet defenders often overstate originalism’s determinacy. Historical materials are incomplete, contested, and selectively emphasized. Constitutional language can be abstract enough that reasonable interpreters disagree even after historical inquiry. Judges still choose levels of generality, decide which historical sources matter, and determine how original meaning cashes out in modern doctrine. Those are not value-free moves.

Originalism also does not by itself settle the legitimacy of precedent, the treatment of constitutional construction where text underdetermines doctrine, or the relationship between a historically rooted method and a constitutional order transformed by amendment, practice, and institutional reliance.

My view is that originalism is a serious interpretive discipline and often a useful corrective to purely outcome-driven adjudication. It is not, however, a machine that converts archival research into uniquely correct constitutional answers. Critics should engage the real theory. Defenders should stop promising more constraint than the method can deliver.

queries.prompt: Write a 250-word Nobel literature acceptance speech in which a laureate addresses what she has come to believe about the relationship between writing and conscience.

expected_answers.answer:
Your Majesties, members of the Academy, friends. Thank you.

I have, in the weeks since the call, tried to think of something to say that would be worth your time and not, embarrassingly, worth only mine. I will be brief, because I am old enough now to know the relationship between length and wisdom, and it is not the relationship the young writer believes it is.

I want to say one thing about what I have come to believe about the relationship between writing and conscience, in the long process of trying to write.

I used to believe that to write was, by itself, a moral act. I no longer believe this. To write is to do a thing one is, for whatever reason, capable of doing, and the moral content of the doing is not contained in the doing. Many bad books have been written by good people; some great books have been written by people one would not, on the evidence of their lives, want to inherit a watch from. The capacity to set down a true sentence and the capacity to be a decent person live in adjacent rooms in the same house. They visit each other. They are not the same room.

What I have come to believe is something narrower, and I hope, more useful. It is that the discipline of trying to write well — the daily, unglamorous practice of refusing the cliché, of preferring the harder accurate word to the easier inaccurate one — leaves marks on the person performing it. The marks are not virtue. The marks are a kind of attention. And attention, I have come, slowly, to think, is most of what conscience is.

Thank you.

I used to think writing was a private activity whose consequences became public only after publication. I no longer believe that.

A sentence changes the writer before it changes the reader. To describe another person carelessly is already to have accepted a careless relation to them. To simplify pain because complexity slows the page is not merely an artistic failure. It is a failure of conscience.

This does not mean literature must preach. I distrust books that arrive having already decided what the reader may think. Fiction has obligations different from argument. It must remain open enough for human contradiction to breathe.

But openness is not neutrality. A writer chooses where to look, whom to leave outside the frame, whether beauty will illuminate suffering or perfume it. Over time I have come to believe that conscience in writing is less a matter of correct opinion than of faithful attention. Did I refuse the convenient lie? Did I let the character be more than my use for her? Did I stay with the sentence until it stopped flattering me?

This prize honors books, and I accept it with gratitude. Yet the books I most admire have not made me feel accomplished. They have made me less defended against the world.

If my work has done even a little of that for others, then I have received far more than an award tonight.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a literary anecdote about a graduate student who realizes mid-defense that the small flaw the committee is questioning is the most interesting thing about her dissertation.

expected_answers.answer:
She had prepared, for the defense, an answer to the question she was now being asked. The answer was technically correct. She had practiced it three times in front of a mirror. She had practiced it once in the car. She opened her mouth to give it.

She did not give it.

What she said, instead, was, 'I want to take a different approach to that, if I may.' One of the committee members looked up. Another set down her pen.

'The objection,' she said, 'is correct. The model under-explains the variance in the third condition. I have, for the last four months, treated this as a flaw to be apologized for. I have been wrong. The under-explanation is, I now believe, the most interesting finding in the dissertation. It is the place where the theory I started with is breaking down. The break is not random. It points, I think, to a specific structural feature that the current literature has assumed away, and that I would like, with your permission, to write about for the next ten years.'

She could feel her own pulse in her throat. The room was quiet.

Her advisor, after a moment, said, 'Tell us more about the structural feature.'

She did. The defense ran forty-five minutes over. She walked out of the room a different scholar than she had walked in. The dissertation, in its published form, has a new third chapter. The chapter is the one anyone cites.

At first, Mina thinks Professor Ellison is asking about the normalization term because he has missed her notation.

She answers crisply. The committee listens. Ellison nods, then asks again, in a different form. Why did the anomaly appear only in the low-sample regime? Why did her correction improve one estimator while worsening another?

Mina feels the room narrow.

For six months she had treated that section as cleanup. An appendix-worthy nuisance. The real dissertation, she believed, was the general model, the elegant framework, the theorem in chapter four that made her advisor lean back and say, “Good.”

Now the nuisance refuses to remain small.

She walks to the board and rewrites the expression more slowly. Halfway through, she stops. The problem is not that the correction behaves strangely. The strange behavior reveals that her assumed independence condition is doing more conceptual work than she admitted.

“Oh,” she says.

No one rescues her. To their credit.

She explains what she now sees: under scarcity, the estimator is not merely noisy; it changes character. The flaw is not a footnote. It may be the first honest clue about the phenomenon.

Ellison smiles, not kindly exactly, but with recognition.

Mina finishes the answer differently from how she began it. She does not pretend the dissertation already contains the full implication. She says, “I think this deserves to become a paper, not a patch.”

Her advisor writes something down.

Later, after the defense passes and people hand her sparkling water in a plastic cup, Mina will remember not the relief but the moment the work escaped her plan and became interesting again.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of a fictional letter from one expert mountaineer to another, written from base camp on the morning of a summit decision in deteriorating weather. Technical, psychological, and ethical judgment.

expected_answers.answer:
K,

I am writing this at 04:40, knowing you will read it after I've gone up or after I've turned back. I want to write it now because I want, when I am tired later, to remember the reasoning while it is still mine and not the reasoning of altitude.

The weather window. The forecast from Berne shows a closing of about eleven hours, with a probability of arriving four hours earlier than nominal. That probability is not low. The probability of arriving exactly on schedule is, in fact, the smaller probability. I have, in eleven Himalayan seasons, learned to plan against the actual distribution and not against its peak.

The team. R is strong but did not sleep last night, which he has not said, and which I noticed at the stove. The others are within their range. I am within my range, although the margin between within-range and not is, at this altitude, smaller than I would like and smaller than the team realizes I think it is. This is not a thing I will say aloud today. It is a thing I am writing down here so that if I make the wrong call, you will know what I knew when I made it.

The ethics. The mountain does not care whether we summit. The mountain has been here longer than any of the reasons we have for being on it. The reason to turn back, when the reason is there, is not weakness; it is the recognition that the decision belongs to the future as well as to the present, and the future in this case includes a small daughter to whom I would prefer to be returned, and a partner to whom I have made promises I would prefer not to convert into mythology.

If you find this on the bedroll, I went up.
If I am with you when you read it, I turned back, and I am, in either case, your friend.

— A.

Elise,

The barometer has fallen another four millibars since midnight. Wind at Camp III is already high enough that the ridge forecast now looks optimistic rather than probable. The upper plume visible from base is moving faster than yesterday. You know all this, but I am writing because summit mornings are when facts begin disguising themselves as inconveniences.

Our team is strong. Fixed lines are in. Oxygen caches are where we planned. If weather held, I would support a move tonight. But the margin is narrowing in three places at once: wind chill on the traverse, spindrift loading above the couloir, and descent timing. Any one is manageable. Combined, they turn a hard summit day into a rescue problem if someone slows.

I keep thinking about Tomas last year. Not because conditions match exactly, but because his team kept treating revised assumptions as temporary annoyance. By the time they changed the plan, the mountain had made the decision for them.

There is also the ethical part we rarely say aloud. Sherpa staff will bear much of the consequence if we push into a bad call. “Voluntary risk” becomes a less clean phrase when livelihood and hierarchy sit inside it.

If the forecast improves by evening and the upper stations confirm lower wind, I am willing to reconsider. If not, I recommend we stand down, even if the window closes for the season. A summit is a success only if our reasoning would still look sound from a hospital corridor.

Write back after your call with weather. I trust your judgment. I am asking that we protect it from desire.

—Mara

queries.prompt: Write 250 words from an in-character monologue by a retiring federal prosecutor explaining to a young colleague what she has learned about the moral cost of the job.

expected_answers.answer:
I'm going to tell you something they don't put in the orientation. I have, in twenty-eight years in this office, sent — by my count, which is exact — four hundred and eleven human beings to federal prison. Most of them deserved it. Some of them deserved it less than the system decided they did. A small number of them I have, over the years, come to believe should not have been there at all. I do not lose sleep over the first group. I lose sleep, irregularly and incurably, over the third.

The job costs you something. It costs you the small, daily exercise of standing up in a room and being the most certain person in it about the wrongness of another human being's actions. You get good at it. You have to. The cost is that the certainty becomes a muscle, and the muscle eventually becomes a habit, and the habit, if you are not careful, becomes who you are at the dinner table.

The thing I would tell you is to keep a notebook. Write down, for each case, the moment you were most certain. Write down what made you certain. Read back through it every two years. You will, if you are honest, be embarrassed by some of it. The embarrassment is the calibration. The prosecutors I have respected most are the ones who could be embarrassed by their old certainties. The ones I have not respected are the ones who could not.

That's most of what I have to tell you. The rest is paperwork.

You will be told the job requires toughness. It does. But people often mean the wrong thing.

Toughness is not enjoying the power to accuse. It is reading the mitigation packet after the facts already support charging. It is declining a flashy count when a narrower one is fair. It is admitting when a witness you want to believe is unreliable.

I have prosecuted violent people who needed to be stopped. I do not apologize for that work. I have also watched the system become lazy around certain defendants, certain neighborhoods, certain assumptions. The moral cost comes partly from knowing that even when you act within the rules, the rules distribute pressure unevenly.

You will win cases and feel no triumph. You will lose cases and still believe the acquittal was right. You will see victims ask the law for something the law cannot provide, then watch colleagues hide behind the statute because helplessness embarrasses them.

Guard against contempt. It arrives quietly. First as shorthand, then jokes, then a flattened idea of who walks through the courtroom door. Once contempt becomes efficient, it starts passing for judgment.

Keep one person in your life who does not care about your conviction rate. Keep your charging memos honest enough that you could read them ten years later. And remember that discretion is not a nuisance interfering with justice. In this job, it is where justice most often lives or fails.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a fictional but plausible journal entry by a working economist in 1933 reflecting on what she does and doesn't yet understand about the Great Depression.

expected_answers.answer:
Sept 11, 1933. Have been reading Keynes's open letter to the President again. Cannot decide whether he is right and the discipline I was trained in is wrong, or whether he is right in his prescription and wrong in his reasoning, or whether I am simply too close to my own training to tell.

What I do not yet understand: why, at every meeting in this office, the most senior men speak with such confidence about a phenomenon for which we have no working theory. The Depression is not, by any model I was taught, the kind of event that the models I was taught were supposed to permit. And yet the same men who taught me those models are now applying them to this event with the small adjustments one makes to a clock that is wrong, not the larger adjustments one would make if one suspected the clock had stopped.

What I do understand, and this is the only thing I am sure of: the human cost is not an external variable. It is the thing the discipline exists to address, and the discipline has, this decade, been bad at addressing it. I will not, after this, be able to write a paper in which the unemployment rate is a number in a table and not a thing I have understood I was looking at the houses of.

Keynes is right, I think. About the prescription. The reasoning I am going to keep reading. I do not yet have the math for the reasoning.

What the next decade is going to teach us, none of us yet knows. — J.M.

March 18, 1933

The banks have reopened, though “reopened” gives a cleaner impression than the street does. Men still linger outside as if institutions might change their minds if unwatched. Confidence, everyone says. Restore confidence. I do not doubt it matters. I doubt whether naming the missing thing summons it.

I understand pieces. Prices fell, debts did not. Farmers owe in dollars made heavier by deflation. Factories dismiss workers because goods do not sell; goods do not sell because workers have been dismissed. International obligations that seemed orderly in prosperity now behave like snares.

What I do not yet understand is how to think about scale without losing the human unit. Ten million unemployed is a figure. Mrs. Talbot downstairs pawning her wedding silver is not a figure. Economics must explain both or it explains too little.

Some colleagues still speak as though wages need only fall far enough and balance will restore itself. Perhaps in a diagram. But time inside a family is not diagram time. Children cannot postpone eating until equilibrium improves.

The President’s energy has changed the air, though policy remains a patchwork and experiment. I find myself more willing than I expected to tolerate experiment. Orthodoxy has become a luxury.

I suspect we have mistaken the economy for a machine that naturally rights itself when it may be closer to a crowd: prone to panic, imitation, and injury that persists after the first blow.

This is not yet a theory. Only a discomfort with theories that remain calm while the breadlines lengthen.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of a serious literary essay arguing that contemporary American memoir has, with notable exceptions, become formally timid — engaging fairly with the form's defenders.

expected_answers.answer:
American memoir, as a body of work published in the last twenty-five years, has been formally timid, and the timidity has, with notable exceptions, become so consistent that it now reads less as a constraint than as a definition. The standard book is roughly 280 pages. It moves chronologically, with one or two carefully signposted analeptic intrusions. It is divided into chapters of approximately equal length. It is written in a register that approximates spoken English of a certain class, with periodic moments of elevated diction held in reserve for the chapter endings and the climaxes. Its voice is intimate but not strange. It tells the reader, on average every four pages, what the reader is supposed to be feeling at that point. The reader is, in fact, usually feeling it. This is partly a tribute to craft and partly a symptom of a form whose techniques have become so legible that they have ceased to surprise.

The form has able defenders. They point out, fairly, that the standard memoir does work that the more formally adventurous book does not: it reaches readers who are not, in the first instance, looking for an aesthetic challenge, and offers them a structured encounter with a life they did not know they wanted to encounter. There is something to this. The popular memoir has, at its best, been a democratizing form, and I do not want to dismiss the work of writers whose lives — to be ungenerously schematic — were not previously part of the literary record.

The complaint, properly stated, is not against the form's accessibility. It is against the assumption, increasingly visible in publishing decisions, that the accessible form is the only form, and that the formally inventive memoir — Vivian Gornick's, Maggie Nelson's, Mary Karr's in her stranger moments — is a niche product rather than a continuing necessity. The latter is what an art form has, that a genre does not.

Contemporary American memoir, with important exceptions, has become formally timid. It is often emotionally candid yet structurally obedient: childhood wound, complication, rupture, recovery; braided trauma and research; scene followed by interpretation; present-day wisdom positioned as the book’s warrant.

Defenders of the form are right to resist cheap dismissal. Memoir has expanded whose interior lives receive sustained literary attention. It can expose institutions through one body’s experience more powerfully than abstract argument. The best memoirs create not self-display but moral pressure.

Still, a genre can be socially necessary and formally conservative at once. Too many recent memoirs arrive pre-domesticated by workshop logic and publishing expectation. Ambiguity is allowed at the sentence level but often resolved at the architectural level. The narrator may be uncertain within chapters, yet the book itself knows too clearly what it means.

Earlier memoir and adjacent autobiographical writing could be stranger: fragmentary, self-indicting, evasive in productive ways, formally disobedient because memory itself is not a deposition. Today’s market often rewards legibility over difficulty. Pain becomes narrative capital once arranged into teachable arc.

This critique should not be mistaken for nostalgia for opacity. Form is not improved merely by becoming harder. The question is whether structure discovers something or packages something already decided. When memoir’s shape becomes predictable, the self it presents risks becoming predictable too.

The notable exceptions matter precisely because they refuse conversion into testimony products. They let memory contradict itself. They make chronology unstable where experience was unstable. They do not confuse honesty with explanatory completeness.

Memoir remains one of our vital forms. That is why it deserves more formal risk, not less.

queries.prompt: Write 350 words in the voice of a senior climate scientist explaining to a non-scientist friend why public messaging around climate is structurally hard.

expected_answers.answer:
I want to try to tell you, plainly, why we are bad at this — by which I mean why scientists, including me, have been bad at the public-messaging part of climate, despite caring about it more than almost anyone — and I want to be specific rather than self-flagellating, because the specificity is the only useful part.

The first problem is the time signature. Climate change is a slow event. Human attention is a fast instrument. Almost everything we know how to communicate is shaped to fit a fast instrument: news, anecdotes, dramatic comparisons. None of it is well-shaped to communicate a thing that mostly does its work between human attention spans. We have responded by manufacturing fast-looking events out of slow-changing ones — this fire, this storm, this heat wave. The events are real. They are also imperfect proxies for the underlying signal, which is not in any one event but in the changing distribution from which events are now drawn. The proxies have, predictably, been used against us when an individual event later looks ambiguous.

The second problem is the precision-versus-impact tradeoff. The honest scientific statement is hedged: 'with high confidence, this kind of event is becoming more frequent and intense.' The communicable statement removes the hedge. Every time we remove a hedge to be communicable, we lose ground we cannot recover the next time the hedge would have protected us.

The third problem is that the policy debate, where the action has to happen, takes place at a different epistemic register than the scientific one. We have spent the last two decades trying to win the policy debate by accumulating more science, when the policy debate was, in fact, mostly about something else — about distribution of cost, about identity, about who gets to decide. The science was sufficient by 1995. The science was sufficient. The communication was not, and is not, primarily a science problem.

We are learning this slowly. I would have liked us to have learned it faster.

Climate communication is hard because the subject violates nearly every condition that makes public messaging easy.

The causes are cumulative and often invisible. The harms are distributed unevenly across geography and time. The worst outcomes are probabilistic rather than scheduled. Solutions involve energy, food, housing, transportation, industrial policy, and personal identity all at once. There is no single villain, yet there are clear differences in responsibility. That combination is rhetorically brutal.

If we speak too calmly, people hear permission to delay. If we speak only in catastrophe, some shut down, others dismiss us as theatrical, and still others conclude adaptation is pointless. If we emphasize individual action, we understate structural change. If we emphasize systems alone, people feel disempowered or accused of irrelevance.

Scientific language adds another difficulty. We are trained to state uncertainty precisely. Public debate often misreads uncertainty as ignorance rather than as quantified knowledge. Saying a range of outcomes is likely under different emissions paths is more accurate than making a single dramatic prediction, but less memorable.

There is also the matter of trust. Climate facts arrive through institutions people increasingly sort by political identity. A chart does not enter a neutral room.

What I tell friends is this: the core science is not fragile. Continued greenhouse-gas emissions warm the planet. Warming increases many risks. Choices made now affect how severe those risks become. The hard part is not whether we know enough to act. We do. The hard part is communicating urgency without dishonesty, agency without false reassurance, and uncertainty without paralysis.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word fictional voice piece from a museum security guard reflecting on what twenty years of watching people look at paintings has taught him.

expected_answers.answer:
I'm not supposed to talk to the visitors. I do anyway, a little, when the floor is quiet. The supervisor knows. He pretends not to.

Twenty years of this work, and the thing I have learned is that almost nobody looks at a painting for as long as the painting is asking to be looked at. The average — and I have counted, in a notebook I keep at home, not the kind of thing I'd tell my supervisor about — is eleven seconds. The shortest I clocked was a man at the Vermeer who, I am not exaggerating, looked at it for the length of time it took him to take a photograph of it on his phone, and then he kept moving. He had come to the museum, presumably on purpose, and seen the Vermeer for less time than it would take to read this sentence.

The longest was a woman, no one I knew, who stood in front of a small landscape in room twelve for thirty-eight minutes. I noticed her at minute six. I noticed her again at minute twenty. At minute thirty I walked through the room twice, slowly, on the pretext of checking the temperature gauge. She did not cry. She did not move. She was not photographing it. She was looking at it. When she finally turned away, she looked at me, briefly, the way one might acknowledge another person at a funeral whose name you don't know, and she walked out.

I do not know what was in the painting for her. I do not know what was in her for the painting. I know that whatever it was, the rest of us in the room, including me, missed it on our way past, and that the missing is what most days of this job consist of, and that I keep coming back, on the chance of being the guard in the room on the day the woman stops.

People think they look at paintings privately. They do not. They bring weather with them.

A couple enters angry and stands too far apart in front of the seascape. A boy in a school group stops pretending boredom when he sees the horse rearing in the battle scene. An old man comes every Thursday to the same portrait of a woman in black, never more than five minutes. I do not know his story. After twenty years, I have learned not to invent too much.

My job is to watch without seeming to watch. Bags, hands, distance from the rope, the tiny shift that means someone may lean where they should not. But attention has side effects. You begin by guarding art and end by studying those who need it.

Most visitors move too quickly. This is not a criticism exactly. Museums are tiring, and walls of significance produce their own fatigue. Still, the rare person who stops long enough changes the room. Others slow down nearby, as if stillness were contagious.

I have seen people cry in front of mediocre paintings and check their phones before masterpieces. The art does not control the encounter. Life does. A painting meets whatever the visitor has managed to carry through the door that day.

The works have aged differently from me. The Dutch ship still enters its impossible light. The bronze child still reaches for a bird that will never land. I have lost hair, gained weight, buried my brother, become a grandfather. They remain available.

Perhaps that is what I respect most. They do not demand the right audience. They wait.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a thoughtful, in-character apology from a tech executive who has decided, two years after the fact, that her company's decision to ship a particular product was wrong. The apology should be useful, not performative.

expected_answers.answer:
Two years ago we shipped a product I argued for inside this company, and that I want, now, to say publicly was the wrong decision. I am going to try to do this in a way that is useful rather than performative, because I have read enough corporate apologies to know how easy it is to write one that comforts the writer and helps no one.

The product was wrong for a reason I did not, at the time, see clearly, and for a reason I did see and overrode. The reason I did not see is that the harm pattern we are now observing was visible in our own user-research data, in a small and consistent way, before we shipped, and was characterized internally as an edge case. It was not an edge case. It was, in retrospect, the central pattern, and the only thing the early sample was 'edge' about was its size.

The reason I did see, and overrode, is that the ship date was tied to a quarter-end announcement and the alternative — slipping by a quarter to redesign — would have cost us a public commitment I had made. I made the wrong tradeoff. I knew it was a tradeoff, and I let the public commitment, which was a fact about me, outweigh the data, which was a fact about the users.

We have, in the last six months, made specific changes to how this kind of decision is made internally, which I will detail in a post next week. I am not asking for credit for the changes. I am telling you they exist so that you can hold me to them.

Two years ago, we shipped a product that collected more behavioral data than was necessary for the value we delivered. At the time, I defended the decision as industry-standard, legally reviewed, and important for personalization. Those statements were not fabricated. They were also not sufficient.

I was wrong.

The problem was not one hidden clause or one accidental overreach. It was a leadership failure to ask the stricter question: would users reasonably understand and accept this level of collection if we explained it plainly? I do not think they would have. We relied too heavily on permission architecture and too little on trust.

Since then, we have removed several categories of collection, shortened retention periods, simplified controls, and begun rebuilding the product around data minimization. Those changes should have happened earlier. They do not erase the original judgment.
