I also owe an apology to employees who raised concerns and encountered a culture too eager to classify them as blockers. Some of them were seeing the issue more clearly than I was.

An apology is useful only if it changes institutional behavior. We have created an independent privacy review for new data-intensive features, elevated dissent during launch reviews, and tied approval to a documented necessity case rather than a vague “may help future models” argument.

I am sorry we shipped it. More importantly, I understand better now why we should not have.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of literary nonfiction about the particular grief of losing a long friendship — not to death but to drift. Specific, not generic.

expected_answers.answer:
A friendship doesn't end. That is the part nobody quite warns you about. A friendship that has lasted twenty years and stops being a friendship leaves a residue you have to keep walking past, in the small social geometries of your life, for the rest of it.

L. and I were close for the whole of our twenties and most of our thirties. I do not, even now, fully understand what happened. There was no fight. There was a slow accumulation of small unrequiteds — a text I sent on her birthday that she did not return until two days later, with an apology that was warmer than the lateness deserved; a visit I declined because I was tired; a job change of mine she did not, I noticed, ask me about. Any one of these would have been nothing. The pattern of them, over perhaps two years, was a sentence in a language we had both, without saying so, agreed to speak.

The specific grief is that no one died. The friend is alive. The friend is, by every available metric — her social media, the occasional bulletin from a mutual — flourishing in a life I am not in. There is no event to mark. There is no permission to mourn. The grief is therefore not metabolized the way grief is supposed to be. It lives, instead, at low volume, in the small daily moments — a song that was ours; a restaurant we went to; a turn of phrase she would have laughed at — when the absence is suddenly, for ten seconds, present.

This kind of loss does not, in my experience, resolve. It thins. The thinning takes years. I am still in the years of it. I suspect, if you have a friendship like this one, you are too, and I wanted to say so.

No one sends condolences when a friendship ends by drift.

There is no date to mark, no last conversation dramatic enough to honor with certainty. There are only delayed replies that grow ordinary, then invitations phrased as intentions rather than plans, then the day you realize you no longer know whether her mother recovered from surgery.

For years, Lena and I spoke in unfinished sentences. She could identify my mood by the word “fine.” I knew which silence meant irritation and which meant exhaustion. We once drove six hours through rain because she had decided, at midnight, that a town with a meteor shower might make us less sad.

Nothing happened. That is the trouble. No betrayal arrived to convert loss into anger. We moved cities. Work hardened around evenings. She had children. I had a divorce. Each of us became reachable in theory and increasingly absent in practice.

Sometimes I see a book she would like and still begin composing the message before remembering that the thread has become archaeological. The last note is eighteen months old. Warm. Affectionate. Useless as proof of ongoing life together.

Drift is grief without permission to grieve. To mourn it can seem accusatory, as though either person must be guilty. Yet something real has gone. A witness to earlier selves. A language built by repetition. The confidence that some news belonged immediately to one specific person.

I do not know whether we will recover the friendship. I resist dramatizing that uncertainty into finality. But I have stopped pretending nothing was lost merely because no one chose the loss in a single visible act.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a fictional voice piece from a midwife who has, in her thirty-year career, attended both births and home deaths, reflecting on what the two experiences share and don't.

expected_answers.answer:
I have caught babies and I have sat with the dying. People ask me whether they are the same, and I think they expect me to say yes, because there is a kind of ceremony involved in both, and a kind of breath, and a kind of room that becomes, for a few hours, the only room in the world.

They are not the same. Births are loud and full of plans even when the plans fail. Deaths are quiet, even the difficult ones, and the plans have, by then, mostly been let go of. The work of midwifery, in a birth, is mostly to keep something open. The work of midwifery, in a death, is mostly to keep something safe.

What they share is harder to name, and I will try. They share the fact that in the room, while the thing is happening, the people in the room are more present than they ordinarily allow themselves to be. The husband watching his wife labor. The daughter watching her mother go. They are, briefly, exempted from the small distractions by which a life is normally conducted. The room concentrates them. I have, in thirty years, watched this happen perhaps four thousand times, and I have never, in any of them, seen the concentration last past the door. People walk out of the room and back into their lives.

The gift of the work is being permitted in the room. It is not a small gift. It is, I think, the reason almost no one in this profession leaves it, even when they could. I will not leave it. I will be carried out.

People assume birth and death are opposites. In some ways they are. One room gathers around arrival; the other around departure. One cries because lungs open; the other goes quiet when breathing stops.

But both teach attention to thresholds. At a birth, you watch for the moment effort becomes passage. At a death, you watch for the moment struggle loosens or changes shape. In both rooms, time behaves strangely. Fifteen minutes can feel enormous. Six hours can vanish.

I have attended births where everyone laughed between contractions, and home deaths where someone told a joke because love needed somewhere to put itself. I have attended births full of fear and deaths full of tenderness. Sentimentality fails both.

The practical work differs. A laboring body needs encouragement, assessment, sometimes decisive escalation. A dying body often needs less interference, more comfort, and protection from the panic of those who cannot bear stillness. But in both cases the professional temptation is the same: to confuse doing more with helping more.

Families remember details we do not expect. A hand held correctly. A sentence not said. The lamp turned away from the bed. These small acts matter because life-changing events make people porous.

Birth has not made death easier for me. Death has not dimmed birth. They have taught me that bodies are never merely biological in the moments when they begin and end being among us.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of literary essay synthesizing what painters, athletes, and surgeons have in common in how they describe the experience of operating at the top of their skill.

expected_answers.answer:
Painters, athletes, and surgeons describe peak operation in a vocabulary so similar that the similarity, once noticed, is hard to unsee. Each of them, when pressed, describes a state in which deliberate thought has receded, the body or hand has taken over the routine elements, the visual or proprioceptive field has narrowed to the relevant variables, and the experience of time has loosened — minutes feel like seconds, or seconds like minutes. They use the words 'flow,' 'in the zone,' 'absorbed,' and a small set of cognate terms. They report, almost without exception, that they cannot enter the state at will, and that any attempt to monitor the state from inside breaks it.

What is striking, across these descriptions, is not the convergence on the phenomenology — that has been studied — but the convergence on the precondition. Each describes the state as available only at a specific relationship between the difficulty of the task and the practitioner's current capacity. Too easy, and attention wanders. Too hard, and effortful cognition seizes the controls. The window is narrow, and one of the things expert practice produces is a person who can find the window more reliably, partly by choosing tasks at the right altitude and partly by recalibrating capacity through preparation.

This suggests something interesting about expertise that the ordinary description of it understates. Expertise is not, on this account, the accumulation of explicit knowledge that the expert can recite. It is the construction of a body — broadly understood, including the trained eye, the trained hand, the trained reflex — that can be turned over to itself under the right conditions, and whose ability to be turned over is what the explicit knowledge is in service of. The conscious mind, in expert practice, is largely a stagehand. The performance, when it goes well, is what the stagehand has spent twenty years arranging the room for.

Painters, athletes, and surgeons often describe peak performance in surprisingly similar terms: heightened attention without self-conscious interference.

The painter stops narrating the painting and sees relationships of weight, edge, and color directly enough to act. The athlete reports that the game slows down, not literally, but because trained perception organizes complexity before deliberate thought can labor through it. The surgeon describes the operation narrowing to tissue planes, pressure, and sequence, with peripheral concerns quieted but not erased.

This state is sometimes romanticized as “flow,” which is not wrong but can be too vague. What these practitioners share is not effortless magic. It is **automaticity built from disciplined repetition**, joined to live responsiveness. They are not following scripts mechanically. They are free to adapt because foundational patterns have been embodied.

A novice also concentrates intensely, but concentration alone is insufficient. The novice’s attention is consumed by basic control. The expert has cognitive room to notice the anomalous brush drag, the defender’s hip orientation, the unexpected vessel course.

There is also a moral dimension. At the top of skill, ego becomes operationally expensive. The athlete who forces heroics, the surgeon who refuses to ask for help, the painter who protects a clever passage that harms the whole canvas—all degrade the work through self-attachment.

The language of mastery therefore converges across domains: trust the training, attend to what is present, do not clutch at the outcome. The hands appear calm because the person has submitted to years of difficulty before the moment arrived.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words from a fictional young rabbi's first sermon to her new congregation, in which she addresses, without preachiness, what she has come to believe a rabbi is for.

expected_answers.answer:
Shabbat shalom. Thank you for having me, and for the welcome, and for letting me say a few words about what I think I am here for, since it is reasonable that you would want to know that on the first morning.

I was taught, in school, that a rabbi is a teacher. That is true and not very useful, because almost anyone who knows anything is, in one sense or another, a teacher. I was taught, also, that a rabbi is a counselor, and an officiant, and a community leader, and a Talmudic scholar, and a fundraiser, and a few other things. All of these are also true. None of them is the heart of it.

What I have come to believe a rabbi is for, in the year before I came here, is something smaller. A rabbi is the person who is paid by the community to keep paying attention to what the community is supposed to be doing when no one else has the time. The 'no one else has the time' part is the important part. You have lives. The lives are full. The tradition is patient but it does not, in the long run, survive being held in the corner of one eye. So you pay a person — me — to hold it in both of mine, on your behalf. I will read what you do not have time to read, and worry about what you do not have time to worry about, and tell you, when it matters, what I have found.

That is most of what I think I am for. The rest, with your help, I will learn.

When people ask what a rabbi is for, I notice they often already have an answer they hope I will confirm. Some want certainty. Some want continuity. Some want a skilled interpreter of ancient words. Some want a person who will visit the hospital at 2 a.m. and not be awkward.

I hope, in time, to be useful in all of those ways. But I have come to believe a rabbi is first a keeper of attention. To help a community notice what it is becoming. To notice when ritual has gone numb and when novelty is only restlessness. To notice the lonely person who attends every event and is still somehow unseen. To notice when our arguments are principled and when they are simply familiar.

I do not arrive with wisdom complete. That would be a dangerous fiction for a young rabbi. I arrive with training, obligation, and a promise to keep learning in public without making the congregation pay for every uncertainty I have.

Judaism does not ask us to float above ordinary life. It sanctifies meals, debts, words, grief, study, doors, days. A rabbi should not stand apart from that texture, issuing commentary from a safer altitude. She should help the community live within it more truthfully.

If I can do that here—teach with rigor, listen without haste, speak when speech matters, and remain quiet when silence is the more honest companion—then perhaps I will become, over time, the rabbi this congregation needs.

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word piece, in the voice of a retiring veterinarian who has worked her whole career in a small farm town, on what she has learned about the relationship between people and animals.

expected_answers.answer:
I want to tell you, before I go, what I have learned about people and animals, and I will try to say it in a way that is true rather than the way it is usually said, because the usual way is sentimental and not, in my experience, what is actually going on.

What I have learned is that the bond between a person and the animal in their care is not, in this part of the country, primarily about love, although love is also there. It is primarily about the long discipline of paying attention to a creature whose suffering or wellness cannot be directly asked about and must be inferred. A farmer who has had a herd for thirty years knows, when he walks into a barn on a Tuesday morning, that the third cow on the left is off her feed before he can tell you which cow it is. He knows it the way you know your spouse is in a bad mood from the way they set down a mug. The knowing is not magical. It is the residue of thirty years of looking.

This is, I think, the most underrated form of intelligence in our culture. It cannot be tested for. It does not credential. It produces, in the people who develop it, a quality of attentiveness that I have, in my experience, found to be the most reliable predictor of decency in human beings. People who have spent their lives paying close attention to creatures who cannot, in language, advocate for themselves, tend to extend the same attention to other people who cannot, in any given moment, advocate for themselves. The attention does not have to be taught a second time. It transfers.

That is most of what I have learned. The rest is paperwork.

After forty years as a veterinarian in a farm town, I have learned that people reveal themselves around animals with fewer defenses than they use around one another.

A rancher who cannot say he is worried about his son will sit beside a colicky mare all night and tell you exactly when her breathing changed. A child who barely speaks in the exam room will explain a rabbit’s preferences with courtroom precision. An old woman may insist her dog is “just a mutt” while adjusting the blanket under him for the fifth time.

Animals draw care out of us, but not always nobility. I have seen neglect born of poverty, neglect born of vanity, and cruelty disguised as toughness. I have also seen people spend money they did not have to ease an animal’s last week. The relationship is not pure. It is intimate, which is more complicated.

Farm work teaches a particular honesty. Some animals are companions. Some are livelihood. Sometimes they are both. Sentimentality that ignores economics helps no one, but economics without gratitude makes people smaller.

I used to think the hardest part of the job would be euthanasia. Sometimes it is. More often the hardest part is helping a person admit what they already know: that love is not proved by making an animal continue at any cost.

Animals do not moralize us into goodness. They invite it. Over a career, I have become less impressed by people who claim to “love animals” in the abstract and more moved by those who feed before coffee, check water in bad weather, and sit on a cold clinic floor because a frightened dog should not be alone.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words analyzing the rhetorical and structural difference between a great closing argument in a criminal trial and a great academic conclusion, and what each tells us about the audience it is built for.

expected_answers.answer:
A closing argument in a criminal trial and the conclusion of an academic paper are both attempts to consolidate, at the end of a longer presentation, what the audience should now believe. They differ in almost every other respect, and the differences are diagnostic of the audiences each is built for.

The closing argument is built for a small group of non-experts who will, within hours, make an irrevocable decision. It is therefore concrete, narrative, and grounded in the specific evidence the jury has seen. It uses the names of the people involved, the dates of the events, the exact phrases of the exact witnesses. Its rhetorical structure is built around moments of decision: 'when she said this, you saw what he did.' It is allowed, and indeed required, to say things like, 'you do not need to be a forensic scientist to know what this means.' It is performed; the lawyer's voice and body are part of the argument. It is bounded by time and by the immediacy of the decision.

The academic conclusion is built for a dispersed audience of experts who will, over years, decide whether the paper joins the literature. It is therefore abstract, abstracted from the specific data, and oriented toward the broader implications. Its rhetorical structure is built around claims and their limits: 'our finding suggests X, subject to the limitations described above.' It is allowed, and indeed required, to acknowledge alternative interpretations and to suggest further work. It is written, not performed; the author's voice is constrained by genre conventions to a kind of careful flatness. It is unbounded by time.

The diagnostic point is this: each form's strengths are the other's weaknesses, and a great closing argument that crept into an academic conclusion would be embarrassing, while a great academic conclusion read to a jury would lose the case in approximately ninety seconds.

A great criminal-trial closing argument and a great academic conclusion both synthesize, but they are built for different forms of assent.

The closing argument addresses decision under consequence. Jurors must return a verdict. The advocate therefore organizes facts into a morally and legally actionable whole, emphasizes burden of proof, resolves competing narratives, and makes the requested judgment feel unavoidable or at least responsible. Rhythm, repetition, and concrete recall matter because memory is now part of persuasion.

An academic conclusion addresses understanding rather than verdict. Its task is not usually to force a binary decision but to clarify what has been shown, what remains uncertain, why the contribution matters, and how the argument changes the field’s map. Overstatement is especially damaging because scholarly audiences are trained to distrust closure that outruns evidence.

The audiences differ accordingly. Jurors may be intelligent but are not specialists in the case’s domain or legal structure. The closing must make complexity usable. Academic readers are often specialists; the conclusion need not rehearse everything but must position the work precisely within existing discourse.

Still, both forms fail when they merely summarize. A closing that repeats testimony without shaping judgment wastes its final moment. A conclusion that re-lists chapter points without revealing significance does the same.

Each teaches us something about audience. The courtroom audience needs confidence sufficient for action under rules. The academic audience needs warranted extension of thought. One asks, “What should we decide?” The other asks, “What do we now understand differently?”

queries.prompt: Write 250 words of a fictional but plausible note slipped under a door, from one neighbor to another, in which a long-running but unspoken tension is finally addressed with grace.

expected_answers.answer:
Hi —

I thought about coming over to say this in person, but I think I will say it better in writing, so I will use the door.

For about three years now I have, on the nights you have your music loud, considered knocking, and not knocked. I want to tell you that this was not because I am the kind of person who finds your music a problem. It is because I am the kind of person who finds it hard to ask other people to change anything, and I have been working on this, separately, in other parts of my life, and I have not been ready to use you for the practice.

I am ready now. The music is, on those nights, louder than I can read or sleep through, and I would like, if you are open to it, to find an arrangement that works for both of us. I am not asking you to give up the music. The music is, from what I have overheard through the wall, often very good, and I think you should keep playing it. I am asking whether there is a way it could be quieter, or earlier, on a few nights a week.

I also wanted to say that I think the version of you who plays the music is, by every other evidence I have of you, a kind person, and I have not, in three years, said hello properly. I am embarrassed about that. Let me know what works for you.

— M., apartment 4B

Dear Ms. Patel,

I owe you an apology for the notes I have not written and the conversations I have avoided.

For months I have been irritated by the music from your apartment late in the evening. Instead of speaking to you directly, I complained to myself, to my sister, and once, unfairly, to the building manager without giving you the courtesy of a first conversation. That was ungenerous.

I also realize now that my own early-morning blender and the heavy door to my balcony may have been less invisible to you than they are to me.

I am not asking for silence. Apartments are shared life, not museum cases. I would be grateful if the volume after 10 p.m. could come down a little on weeknights. In return, I will stop using the blender before 7 a.m. and will repair the door closer this weekend.

If you would rather discuss in person, please knock. If a note is easier, that is fine too. I would prefer that we become ordinary neighbors again rather than careful strangers separated by one wall.

Thank you,  
Daniel in 4B

queries.prompt: Write a 350-word in-character reflection from a former Supreme Court clerk, twenty years on, on what the experience of clerking taught her about the law and about herself.

expected_answers.answer:
I clerked for Justice ——. I am, twenty years on, going to refrain from saying which one, partly out of professional habit and partly because the lessons I am about to describe were not, in the end, about the Justice. They were about the institution. I'll say what I mean.

The first thing the year taught me is that the law, as practiced at that level, is not what it looks like from the outside. From the outside it is a contest between arguments, and the side with the better argument wins. From the inside it is, much more often, a process in which all of the available arguments are pretty good, the Justice has views formed by their entire life that the briefs are not going to change, and the question being decided is which strand of the law gets to win this particular fight. The clerks help the Justice say what they were going to say, more carefully than they would have said it without you. This is more useful than it sounds; the carefulness compounds across decades and across opinions. It is also less heroic than the law-school version of the work suggested.

The second thing the year taught me is that intelligence, at that level, is uncorrelated with rightness. The smartest person I worked with that year was the clerk in the opposite chambers, who held views I thought were dangerous and defended them with arguments I could not, in the moment, refute. I have spent twenty years thinking about which of those arguments I now think I could refute, and the answer is: not all of them. Some of them I have come around to, partially. Some I have not. It was a useful inoculation against the comfortable habit of believing that the people on the other side of a question are simply less smart.

The third lesson is the smallest and the one I remember most. The Justice, on my last day, said: 'The hardest part of this job, kid, is staying surprised.' I did not, at twenty-six, understand the sentence. I think I do now.

Twenty years after clerking at the Supreme Court, I remember fewer grand abstractions than I expected and more small acts of compression.

A case arrives with thousands of pages behind it: ruined plans, institutional conflict, procedural history, doctrinal accumulation. By the time it reaches the Court, much has been translated into questions presented and standards of review. Clerking taught me how much intelligence that translation requires—and how much life it necessarily leaves out.

I learned that justices are not interchangeable ideology machines, though ideology matters. Temperament matters. Patience matters. Which ambiguity irritates a judge, which precedent feels weight-bearing, which institutional consequence they consider real: these shape outcomes in ways not captured by newspaper typologies.

I also learned something less flattering about myself. At twenty-six, I mistook fluency for wisdom. I could produce a memorandum quickly, identify weak arguments, and feel quietly superior to litigants whose positions seemed messy. Time has made me more charitable. Most serious legal disputes are messy because the world is messy before law arrives to order it.

The clerkship also taught me reverence and skepticism at once. The Court matters profoundly. Its work deserves preparation, restraint, and civic seriousness. It is also a human institution populated by people who reason under limits, inherit frameworks, and sometimes write broadly where narrower work would have served better.

What stayed with me most was not proximity to power, though young clerks feel that intensely. It was proximity to responsibility. A sentence in an opinion can alter incentives in trial courts, agencies, schools, prisons, legislatures. Writing law is not the same as winning an argument.

I left the clerkship admiring intelligence. I now admire judgment more.

queries.prompt: Write a 250-word piece in the voice of a working comedian explaining why a joke that should land doesn't, in language a non-comedian can follow.

expected_answers.answer:
Sometimes a joke is funny on paper and dies in the room, and the people who weren't there will say, when they read it later, that the audience didn't get it. The audience got it. The room is not stupid. The joke died for a reason, and the reason is almost always one of three things.

One: rhythm. A joke has a beat. The setup has a length and the punchline has a length, and the gap between them has a length, and any one of these being off by a quarter-second can kill the joke. You can fix this in the writing if you read it aloud. Most people don't read it aloud, because the joke, in their head, has been read aloud thirty times already and they cannot, anymore, hear it the way the audience will hear it.

Two: trust. The joke needs the audience to believe, in the half-second before the punchline, that they don't know where you're going. If, by the wording or by the setup, you have telegraphed the punchline, the audience is ahead of you, and a joke that lands ahead of itself does not land. They will smile politely. They will not laugh.

Three: cost. The audience needs to feel, in the punchline, that the joke cost the comedian something — a small piece of dignity, a small admission, a small risk. The jokes that die in front of a tough room are almost always the jokes that cost the comedian nothing.

The rest is just doing it for enough years that the three things become reflexive. That part takes a while.

A joke can fail even when the premise is good because comedy is not only about content. It is about tension, timing, surprise, and the audience’s willingness to follow you where you are leading.

Sometimes the setup gives away too much, so the punchline confirms rather than turns. Sometimes it gives away too little, so the audience is still decoding the sentence when the laugh should happen. Sometimes the rhythm is wrong: one extra clause after the funny word, one pause too short before it.

Context matters too. A joke that works in a loud club may die in a corporate ballroom because the room’s social rules are different. A joke about embarrassment may work only after the audience trusts that you are willing to embarrass yourself first. If they are busy deciding whether you are cruel, they are not available to laugh.

Comics say a joke “should” work when the mechanism is sound on paper. But performance reveals the hidden variables. Did the audience recognize the reference? Did they see the emotional target? Did the earlier material train them to hear this line in the intended frame?

A failed joke is useful data if you do not romanticize it. You tighten the setup, move the emphasis, change one word, cut the whole thing, or admit that the room taught you the premise mattered more to you than to anyone else.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words from a fictional senior judge writing a memo to her colleagues on a appellate court, recommending against issuing a ruling on a politically charged case for narrow procedural reasons, and explaining why the narrow grounds are the right grounds.

expected_answers.answer:
Memo to: colleagues on the panel
From: J. Hewitt
Re: [Case], procedural disposition

I write to urge that we resolve [Case] on the narrow procedural grounds discussed in my draft section II.B, and that we decline to reach the constitutional questions presented in the parties' merits briefs, however inviting those questions are.

Let me say first what I am not arguing. I am not arguing that the constitutional questions are unimportant. They are. I am not arguing that we are not, in time, the right court to address them. We may be. I am arguing about sequencing.

The principle I want to invoke is one we all learned in our first year of constitutional law and have, in the years since, sometimes been tempted to set aside under the press of momentous facts: that a court of limited jurisdiction owes the polity it serves the modesty of deciding no more than the case requires. The principle is not a technicality. It is a discipline that does important work. It preserves the legitimacy of courts as institutions whose authority is, ultimately, a public belief that we are deciding cases rather than imposing policy preferences under cover of cases. Every time we reach further than the disposition required, we spend a small amount of that belief. We are not, at the moment, oversupplied with it.

The procedural grounds in section II.B are sufficient to resolve the matter as between these parties. They do not deprive future litigants of the constitutional questions; if anything, they preserve those questions for a case in which the record will be more complete. They allow this court to do its job today without doing more than its job today.

I recognize the temptation. The temptation is real. I am asking us to resist it, not because the temptation is unworthy, but because the institutional cost of yielding to it, summed across the panel decisions of the next decade, is greater than the immediate benefit of being right today.

— J.H.

Colleagues,

I recommend that we resolve this appeal on the narrow procedural ground that the plaintiffs lack standing as presently pleaded, rather than reaching the constitutional merits.

The public importance of the underlying dispute is obvious. That is precisely why restraint matters. A merits ruling from us would be read immediately as a decision about the contested policy itself, even though the record before us is poorly suited to that task. The injury allegations are abstract in places, causation is attenuated, and the requested relief would require assumptions we need not make to decide this case.

Standing doctrine is not an evasive technicality when properly applied. It preserves the distinction between courts resolving concrete disputes and courts issuing generalized judgments on public controversy. We do neither the law nor the institution a favor by expanding our reach because the merits attract attention.

I recognize the cost of a procedural disposition. The parties and the public may prefer clarity. But clarity achieved through an unnecessary constitutional ruling can be false clarity, especially where a narrower decision leaves future litigants free to return with a developed record and a plaintiff who can establish jurisdiction.

Our court should say only what the case requires. Here, the case requires us to say that this plaintiff, on this pleading, has not shown entitlement to federal judicial resolution. That conclusion is not timidity. It is fidelity to the conditions under which our authority is legitimate.

I therefore recommend vacatur and remand with instructions to dismiss for lack of jurisdiction.

queries.prompt: Critique the following plan: 'We'll roll our own crypto for the new authentication system — our use case is unique and existing libraries don't fit.' What's wrong, and what should they do instead?

expected_answers.answer: Rolling your own crypto is the textbook bad idea — security primitives have subtle correctness requirements (constant-time operations, side-channel resistance, correct padding, parameter validation, RNG quality) that are easy to get wrong and impossible to test for in normal QA. 'Our use case is unique' almost never survives investigation. Use libraries that have been reviewed by people who do this for a living: libsodium for general primitives, established password hashes (Argon2id), well-known TLS implementations, an existing auth library or identity provider (Keycloak, Auth0, Cognito). If the use case is genuinely unusual, the right answer is to compose existing primitives rather than implement new ones, and have the design reviewed by a security professional before any code ships.

The plan is bad. “Our use case is unique” is almost never a valid reason to invent cryptographic primitives or authentication protocols.

What is wrong:
- cryptography fails in edge cases experts spend careers anticipating
- novelty destroys external audit confidence
- subtle errors often appear secure under normal testing
- homegrown designs age badly and are hard to maintain
- authentication security includes key management, randomness, replay protection, side channels, rotation, and failure semantics, not just a clever algorithm

What they should do instead:
1. Use established, reviewed protocols and libraries.
2. Define the actual requirements that “do not fit.”
3. Adapt composition and surrounding system design, not primitives.
4. Prefer standards such as TLS, WebAuthn, OAuth/OIDC where appropriate.
5. Commission security review of the architecture and implementation.
6. If an unusual requirement genuinely exists, involve professional cryptographers and still minimize novelty.

The correct posture is not “never customize anything.” It is “do not invent crypto where composition of proven parts will work.” Rolling your own authentication crypto is one of the fastest ways to turn uniqueness into breach evidence.

queries.prompt: Prove that the sum of the first n odd integers is n².

expected_answers.answer: Sum = 1 + 3 + 5 + ... + (2n−1). By induction. Base n=1: sum = 1 = 1² ✓. Step: assume sum to k terms = k². Adding (2(k+1)−1) = 2k+1 gives k² + 2k + 1 = (k+1)². □ (Geometric proof: arrange dots in an n×n square as nested L-shaped layers of 1, 3, 5, ..., (2n−1) dots.)

We prove by induction that

`1 + 3 + 5 + ... + (2n - 1) = n²`.

**Base case:** For `n = 1`, `1 = 1²`.

**Inductive step:** Assume

`1 + 3 + ... + (2k - 1) = k²`.

Then for `k + 1`,

`1 + 3 + ... + (2k - 1) + (2(k + 1) - 1)`
`= k² + 2k + 1`
`= (k + 1)²`.

Thus the formula holds for `k + 1`. By induction, the sum of the first `n` odd integers is `n²`.

queries.prompt: Investigate why a Kafka consumer group's lag is growing despite consumers reporting healthy.

expected_answers.answer: (1) Confirm lag is real: query consumer-group lag via Kafka tooling — `kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe`. Compare committed offset to latest. (2) Are consumers actually polling fast enough? Check poll rate, processing time per record, and rebalances. A consumer 'reporting healthy' might still be slow if processing is CPU-bound or doing per-record I/O. (3) Partition imbalance: a single slow partition can dominate lag. Check per-partition lag. (4) Rebalances: frequent rebalances stop progress; check group coordinator logs. (5) Throughput mismatch: producers writing faster than consumers can process — check producer rate. (6) max.poll.records and max.poll.interval.ms misconfigured causing slow poll loops or kicked-out consumers. (7) Downstream dependency: consumer might wait on slow DB or HTTP call. Fixes: scale partitions and consumers proportionally, batch downstream calls, profile per-record processing, ensure heartbeat thread isn't blocked by long processing.

If consumer-group lag is growing while consumers look “healthy,” the consumers are alive, not necessarily keeping up.

Investigate in layers:

1. **Compare ingest rate versus consume rate.**
   - Producer throughput may have increased.
   - Consumers may be stable yet underprovisioned.

2. **Check partition assignment.**
   - More consumers than partitions does not help.
   - One hot partition can dominate lag while others look fine.

3. **Inspect processing time per message.**
   - Downstream DB/API latency
   - batch-size changes
   - synchronous commits
   - expensive deserialization or validation

4. **Review consumer rebalances.**
   - Frequent restarts, deployment churn, heartbeat/session timeout issues can reduce effective work time.

5. **Measure commit behavior.**
   - Large uncommitted batches can make lag appear stale.
   - Auto-commit may hide processing failures rather than solve them.

6. **Check fetch and poll settings.**
   - `max.poll.records`
   - `fetch.min.bytes`
   - `max.partition.fetch.bytes`
   - `max.poll.interval.ms`

7. **Look for retry or poison-message loops.**
   - One record can stall a partition if error handling is poor.

Healthy CPU and memory do not prove consumer adequacy. The decisive question is whether each partition’s sustained processing capacity exceeds its sustained arrival rate.

queries.prompt: Walk through why CAP theorem in practice is often presented misleadingly.

expected_answers.answer: CAP says: in the presence of a network partition, a system must choose between consistency and availability. The misleading framing is treating it as a free three-way choice ('pick two of three') — partitions aren't optional; they will happen in real distributed systems. So 'CA' systems don't exist in practice; the real choice is CP or AP under partition, and most systems are both at different times. Modern framings (PACELC) make this explicit: in the presence of Partition, choose A or C; Else (no partition), choose Latency or Consistency. The right design question is: for each operation, what consistency does it need, and what's the cost of unavailability? Different operations within the same system can sit in different places on the spectrum (strong consistency for payments, eventual for analytics).

CAP is often presented misleadingly as “pick any two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.” That slogan is catchy and wrong enough to distort architecture decisions.

In a distributed system, partitions are not an optional feature you casually decline. Networks can fail. The meaningful CAP question is: **when a partition occurs, do you preserve consistency by rejecting some requests, or preserve availability by serving responses that may diverge?**

CAP also uses specific meanings:
- **Consistency** means a single-copy, linearizable view, not “the data is usually correct.”
- **Availability** means every non-failing node receives a response, not “the service has good uptime.”
- **Partition tolerance** means the system continues operating despite dropped or delayed messages between nodes.

In normal operation without partitions, many systems can provide both consistency and availability. CAP constrains behavior during partition, not all system design all the time.

The slogan also hides richer tradeoffs:
- latency versus consistency
- quorum choices
- bounded staleness
- operational recovery
- PACELC-style reasoning after CAP

A good architecture discussion should specify the failure mode and the user-visible consequence. For a shopping cart, temporary inconsistency may be acceptable. For a bank transfer ledger, rejecting writes may be preferable to divergent balances. CAP is useful when it disciplines that conversation. It is harmful when used as a trivia phrase to justify vague “eventual consistency” claims.

queries.prompt: A factory produces widgets with 2% defect rate. If 50 widgets are inspected, what's the probability of finding more than 2 defective ones? Use a Poisson approximation and explain the assumption.

expected_answers.answer: n=50, p=0.02, np=1. Since np is small and n large, Poisson with λ=1 is a good approximation to Binomial(50, 0.02). P(X > 2) = 1 − P(X ≤ 2) = 1 − (e^{−1} + e^{−1}·1 + e^{−1}·0.5) = 1 − e^{−1}·2.5 ≈ 1 − 0.368·2.5 ≈ 1 − 0.9197 ≈ 0.0803, or about 8%. Assumption: each widget is an independent Bernoulli trial with the same p — true if production isn't drifting and inspections are random.

With defect rate `p = 0.02` and `n = 50`, the Poisson approximation uses `λ = np = 1`.

Let `X ~ Poisson(1)`. We want

`P(X > 2) = 1 - P(X=0) - P(X=1) - P(X=2)`.

Compute:
- `P(X=0) = e^-1`
- `P(X=1) = e^-1`
- `P(X=2) = e^-1 / 2`

So

`P(X > 2) = 1 - e^-1(1 + 1 + 1/2)`
`= 1 - 2.5e^-1`
`≈ 0.0803`.

The probability is about **8.0%**.

The Poisson approximation is reasonable because `n` is moderately large and `p` is small, with `np = 1`. An exact binomial calculation would be slightly more precise.

queries.prompt: Argue both sides: should a startup adopt Kubernetes from day one, or stick with simpler deployment (e.g., systemd on a VM or a managed PaaS)? Recommend.

expected_answers.answer: For Kubernetes: industry-standard abstractions; portable across clouds; rich ecosystem (Helm, operators, monitoring); easier to scale and self-heal; future engineers will know it. Against: operational complexity is substantial — you're now running a distributed system to run your app; resource overhead and steep learning curve; many features are unused at small scale. For simpler deployment (PaaS like Heroku/Render/Fly, or systemd + VMs): minimal ops burden, faster iteration, fewer moving parts; can scale to surprisingly far. Against: vendor lock-in; less portable; eventually outgrown for some workloads. Recommendation for a small startup: start with the simplest thing that works (a PaaS or managed container service like ECS/Cloud Run). Move to Kubernetes only when you have a concrete reason — multiple stateful services with complex networking, multi-cloud requirement, large enough team to operate it. The biggest cost in early stage is engineering velocity; pay the K8s tax later, when the velocity it enables exceeds the tax it imposes.

**Case for Kubernetes from day one:**  
It gives a standard deployment substrate, service discovery, autoscaling primitives, rolling deployments, and a path to portability. If the founding team already knows Kubernetes deeply, expects many services, needs complex scheduling, or has customers demanding certain deployment models, adopting it early can avoid a later platform migration.

**Case against it:**  
For most startups, Kubernetes consumes scarce engineering attention before there is enough operational complexity to justify it. Cluster management, manifests, ingress, secrets, observability, debugging, and CI/CD integration all add surface area. The team can spend months becoming a worse platform company instead of a better product company.

**Simpler alternatives:**  
- managed PaaS
- containers on a managed app platform
- a few VMs with systemd and good automation
- managed databases and queues

My recommendation: **do not adopt Kubernetes on day one unless you already have strong platform expertise and concrete requirements that demand it.** Start simpler, but build good deployment hygiene: containers if useful, infrastructure as code, reproducible environments, observability, and clean service boundaries. That makes later migration possible without paying the full Kubernetes tax prematurely.

queries.prompt: How would you approach evaluating whether a new feature should ship behind an A/B test or directly?

expected_answers.answer: Ship behind an A/B test when: (1) the outcome is measurable on a meaningful timescale (days–weeks); (2) the expected effect size is small enough that gut estimation isn't reliable; (3) there's plausible downside risk to ship-everywhere; (4) the feature is the kind of thing where 'we'll know if users like it' is genuinely uncertain. Ship directly when: (1) the change is a bug fix or strictly-better correctness improvement; (2) the change is below the noise floor of any test you could run; (3) the change is part of a coordinated launch where partial rollout is operationally costly; (4) there's a clear external commitment (regulatory deadline, marketing campaign). Cost of A/B testing isn't just engineering — it's slower iteration, statistical overhead, and the cognitive cost of running and reading tests. Reserve it for the cases where the answer materially shifts the decision.

Decide based on uncertainty, risk, reversibility, and measurement value.

Use an A/B test when:
- the feature’s effect is genuinely uncertain
- success can be measured with a credible metric
- expected effect size matters to the decision
- the feature may produce tradeoffs between conversion, engagement, retention, or guardrails
- exposure can be randomized safely
- you can wait for evidence before full rollout

Ship directly when:
- the change is obviously corrective, such as a bug fix or legal requirement
- measurement would be misleading or ethically awkward
- the feature is infrastructural and not user-perceivable
- the expected downside is low and rollback is easy
- an experiment would be slower than the value of simply shipping

Questions to ask:
1. What decision will the experiment change?
2. What is the primary metric and what are the guardrails?
3. Is randomization valid, or will interference contaminate results?
4. What sample size and duration are needed?
5. Would a staged rollout or feature flag answer the operational risk better than an experiment?

A/B testing should reduce decision uncertainty. If everyone will ship regardless of the result, the experiment is theater.

queries.prompt: Prove: for any positive integer n, the product of n consecutive integers is divisible by n!.

expected_answers.answer: The product k(k+1)(k+2)...(k+n−1) for any integer k. Note that this equals (k+n−1)! / (k−1)! = C(k+n−1, n) · n!, where C(·, ·) is a binomial coefficient. Binomial coefficients are integers (they count subsets). Therefore the product equals n! times an integer, i.e., is divisible by n!. □

Take any `n` consecutive integers:

`k + 1, k + 2, ..., k + n`.

Their product is

`(k+1)(k+2)...(k+n)`.

Consider the binomial coefficient

`C(k+n, n) = (k+n)! / (n! k!)`.

This equals

`(k+1)(k+2)...(k+n) / n!`.

A binomial coefficient is an integer. Therefore the product of those `n` consecutive integers divided by `n!` is an integer, which means the product is divisible by `n!`.

queries.prompt: Design a logging architecture for a microservices application with ~50 services, including how you'd avoid the typical pitfalls.

expected_answers.answer: Structured logging in JSON from every service, with mandatory fields: timestamp (ISO 8601 UTC), level, service name, instance ID, correlation/trace ID, user ID (where applicable), and message. Library-level enforcement so engineers can't bypass it. Ship logs via a lightweight forwarder (Vector, Fluent Bit) to a central pipeline that buffers (Kafka) before fan-out to: long-term storage (S3 + Athena/BigQuery for cheap historical queries), short-term search (Elasticsearch or Loki for last 7–30 days), and metrics extraction (counts/rates of log patterns → Prometheus). Pitfalls and mitigations: (1) Log explosion from a single noisy service — per-service rate limits at the forwarder; alert on spike. (2) PII in logs — explicit allowlist of fields; lint rules; periodic audits. (3) Cost — sampling for high-volume info logs, full retention for warnings/errors. (4) Correlation across services — distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) with trace ID propagated in every log line. (5) Untimely loss during deploy — forwarder buffers to disk. (6) Search performance — index only fields you actually query; don't index full message text.

A logging architecture for ~50 microservices should optimize for correlation, usefulness, and restraint.

**At the service level**
- Emit structured JSON logs, not ad hoc strings.
- Include timestamp, service name, environment, severity, request ID, trace ID, span ID where available, tenant/user identifiers only when policy allows, and stable error codes.
- Separate business events from diagnostic logs when appropriate.
- Never log secrets, tokens, raw credentials, or sensitive payloads by default.

**Collection**
- Use sidecars, agents, or node-level collectors to ship logs centrally.
- Buffer locally to tolerate temporary backend disruption.
- Standardize parsing and enrichment before indexing.

**Storage and querying**
- Centralize in a log platform such as OpenSearch/Elasticsearch, Loki, Splunk, or equivalent.
- Apply retention tiers: short hot retention for verbose logs, longer retention for security/audit where justified.
- Index fields needed for search; avoid indexing everything expensively.

**Correlation**
- Propagate trace/request IDs across services.
- Integrate logs with metrics and distributed tracing.
- Build incident views around a request path, not isolated service consoles.

**Pitfalls to avoid**
- unstructured logs
- inconsistent severity levels
- log spam as a substitute for metrics
- PII leakage
- no sampling strategy for high-volume debug events
- retention without cost control
- believing logs alone replace traces

Logs should help answer “what happened?” quickly. Metrics tell you “is something wrong?” Traces tell you “where did time go?” A mature system uses all three.

queries.prompt: Compute the expected value and variance of a uniformly random integer chosen from {1, 2, ..., n}.
Write a 250-word piece in the voice of a novelist explaining why she has refused, for thirty years, to write a memoir, even though her publisher has asked many times.

expected_answers.answer:
The publisher asks every five years, like a tide. I refuse, with the same explanation each time, and the explanation has not become more persuasive to either of us with repetition.

The explanation, briefly. A novel is something I make. A memoir is something I would have to find. The making is the part I am, in fact, good at — the small daily craft of choosing this word and not that one, of structuring a scene so that what matters lands where the reader can receive it. The finding is something else entirely. The finding is the work of going back through the actual events of one's actual life and deciding which of them constitute a story. I have, in seventy years of living, found that the events that feel most like a story while one is in them are almost never the events that turn out to have been the story; the story has, almost always, been happening underneath, in a part of the life one was not paying close attention to at the time.

A memoir, written honestly, would be the work of paying that close attention now, in retrospect, to the underneath. I have, in my fiction, paid that kind of attention to imaginary lives. I am not sure I have the courage to pay it to my own. I am, additionally, not sure I have the right to: the underneath of my life contains, by definition, other people, who did not sign up to be in a book.

When I am dead the publisher can do as they like. Until then I am a novelist, which is, I find, a sufficient occupation.

For thirty years, editors have suggested a memoir as though it were the natural receipt for a long career. I understand the commercial logic. Readers want the hidden room behind the novels. They want to know which grief became which character, which marriage cracked inside which chapter.

I have declined not because my life is sacred, nor because it has been uninteresting. I decline because I do not trust the memoir I would be tempted to write.

Memory arrives dressed as evidence. It edits without permission. It gives me eloquence in scenes where I was almost certainly clumsy. It assigns motives to the dead who cannot appeal. Fiction at least confesses its inventions. Memoir can smuggle them across the border under the badge of honesty.

I have written from my life constantly. The novels contain weather from my childhood, my mother’s hands, a staircase from the first apartment where I was happy and did not know it. But once transformed, those materials become answerable to the book rather than to biography.

Perhaps this sounds evasive. It may be. Silence has its vanities too. Yet I have never believed that publishing a fact automatically honors it. Some experiences have been better served by being metabolized slowly, disguised completely, or not used at all.

The life is mine. The books are what I have made available.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of a literary essay arguing that the modern habit of giving books five-star ratings has done specific damage to how we read.

expected_answers.answer:
I would like to argue, in good faith, that the practice of rating books on a five-star scale has done a specific kind of damage to how we read, and that the damage is large enough to be worth noticing even though it is widely considered too trivial to discuss.

The damage is this. A five-star scale is a compression. It forces the entire response to a book — which is, when it goes well, a complex, multi-dimensional, often contradictory experience — into one of five buckets, four of which are largely indistinguishable from each other in any given reader's use. The compression is, of course, the point: it allows aggregation across many readers. The cost is paid one reader at a time, and is largely invisible because each reader pays it in a way they have stopped noticing.

The cost is that the scale, once internalized, becomes a kind of mental noise that runs in the background of reading itself. The reader, halfway through a chapter, is already in conversation with the scale: this is a four-star book, this is dragging into three-star territory, this is a recovery. The conversation crowds out the older, slower conversation reading used to be — the one in which the reader paid attention to what was actually in the sentence in front of them, without yet knowing what they would, on reflection, think of it as a whole.

I do not believe the scale should be abolished, which is not within anyone's power anyway. I believe it should be ignored, by individual readers, as an act of small private resistance to the compression of a complex experience into a coarse one. The book deserves, and the reader deserves, the longer conversation. The stars can wait.

The five-star rating system has damaged reading not because readers should not judge books, but because it trains us to confuse response with verdict.

A novel becomes “three stars” before we have asked what kind of attention it requested. A difficult book is penalized for failing to produce immediate pleasure. A charming minor work is inflated because it delivered exactly what was expected. The scale rewards consumer satisfaction more readily than intellectual or aesthetic encounter.

Ratings also encourage false comparability. A slim comic novel, a translation of tragic poetry, a memoir about illness, and an ambitious failed epic all enter the same numerical tribunal. The question shifts from “What did this book attempt, and what happened to me while reading it?” to “Where does it fall on my personal product grid?”

Defenders rightly note that ratings help discovery and democratize opinion. Readers have always recommended and dismissed books. True. The problem is not public judgment; it is compression. Once judgment becomes frictionless, reflection often narrows to justification of the number already selected.

The star system also changes how people perform reading socially. A review can become customer-service feedback: pacing, likability, satisfaction with ending. Those concerns are not illegitimate, but they are incomplete. Literature sometimes succeeds by withholding ease, making a reader misrecognize themselves, or failing in a way more interesting than competent success.

I do not propose abolishing ratings. I propose mistrusting them, especially our own. A book may be worth years of return and still resist a neat score. The habit of reading should leave room for that resistance.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words from a fictional brain surgeon, in plain language, on the difference between technical mastery and clinical judgment.

expected_answers.answer:
I will tell you the difference, because it took me a long time to understand it and I think it is worth saying. Technical mastery is what they teach you in residency, and what gets you hired. It is the steady hand, the knowledge of the anatomy, the ability to execute a difficult procedure under pressure. It is, in some ways, the easy part. By easy I do not mean simple — it is fearsomely hard to acquire — but rather that it is a thing one can practice toward, with feedback, until one has it.

Clinical judgment is different. Clinical judgment is the question of whether to operate at all. Whether this patient, at this age, with these comorbidities, with these stated wishes, with this particular tumor in this particular location, will be better off six months from now if I cut, or if I do not. It is, almost by definition, the question that cannot be solved by technical mastery, because technical mastery answers 'can I do this?' and clinical judgment answers 'should I?'

The surgeons I have respected most are the ones who, over a career, became better at saying 'no, I won't operate' than at saying 'yes, I will.' The surgeons I have respected least are the ones whose technical mastery never developed into clinical judgment, and who continued to operate, brilliantly, on people who would have been better off if they had not been operated on at all.

The distinction is not taught. It is the central thing of the profession, and it is the one thing that has to be learned by accumulating thirty years of being uncertain, paying attention to the uncertainty, and writing down what you learn.

Technical mastery is knowing how to operate: where to place the incision, how to protect eloquent cortex, how to control bleeding, how to remove what can be removed without causing needless harm. It is essential. Without it, confidence is dangerous.

Clinical judgment begins before the incision and continues after it. It asks whether surgery is the right intervention, whether the scan explains the symptoms, whether a technically possible operation would improve the patient’s actual life, and whether uncertainty should lead to action or restraint.

A surgeon can be technically brilliant and clinically unwise. If I can remove a tumor but leave a patient with deficits they would not choose in exchange for the likely benefit, skill alone has not answered the question. Conversely, clinical judgment without technical competence becomes pious hesitation.

Patients often ask, “Can you do it?” The more important question is sometimes, “Should we?” My job is to answer both honestly.

Experience teaches that judgment is not softer than mastery. It is harder to display, harder to measure, and more accountable to the whole person rather than the operative field. The best surgery is not the most difficult one successfully completed. It is the right intervention, performed well, for the right reason.

queries.prompt: Write 350 words from a senior negotiator's reflection on what they learned about silence as a tool, and how it differs from rudeness.

expected_answers.answer:
I learned silence late and badly. I want to write down what I learned, because the thing is genuinely useful and I see it described, in books on negotiation, in a way that conflates it with something it is not.

The thing it is not: rudeness, or hostility, or the petty refusal-to-respond by which a small person tries to make a smaller person feel smaller. That kind of silence is a weapon, and it works, in the short term, on people whose status is below yours and who are afraid of you. It does not work in any negotiation that matters, because the people across from you in a negotiation that matters are not, by definition, people whose status is below yours or who are afraid of you, and they will register the rudeness as the move it is, and the negotiation will be over before it began.

The thing it is: the disciplined, attentive refusal to fill a silence that the other party has, by their own actions, opened. A negotiator says a thing. It is the wrong thing. They know, the moment after they have said it, that it was the wrong thing. The temptation of the listener — and this is the temptation I had to spend ten years training out of myself — is to fill the resulting space, either with reassurance, or with a clarifying question, or with a different topic. The discipline is to let the silence sit. The other party, in that silence, will almost always, given enough seconds, do one of three things: walk back what they just said, qualify it, or — most useful of all — explain why they said it, which is the part of the negotiation you actually needed to hear.

The difference from rudeness is that the silence is not directed at the person. It is directed at the moment. You are not refusing to speak to them. You are giving them the room to keep speaking. The body language, in this kind of silence, is attentive and not closed. The eyes are open. The face is interested. The mouth, only, is quiet.

It is, I find, one of the few skills in this work that improves indefinitely with practice. I am still improving at it. I do not expect to stop.

Early in my career, silence made me nervous. In negotiations, I filled it with explanation, reassurance, sometimes concessions no one had yet requested. I thought momentum required speech.

I learned otherwise.

Silence can do several things. It can let an offer land heavily enough to be considered rather than immediately countered. It can reveal who is uncomfortable with the current terms. It can prevent you from improving your own proposal against yourself. It can create space for the other side to say the thing they had prepared not to say.

But useful silence is not theatrical withholding. It is not staring someone down to perform dominance. That often reads as contempt, especially across cultures or power differences. Rudeness uses silence to diminish. Skilled negotiation uses silence to make room or to preserve discipline.

The distinction lies in purpose and context. If a counterpart has shared grief, complexity, or internal constraint, silence may be respect. If they have asked a direct procedural question and you refuse to answer for effect, that is usually manipulation. If you pause after stating a boundary because the boundary deserves not to be diluted, that is clarity.

I now think silence is one of the few negotiation tools that improves when used less often. People notice patterned silence and learn to play against it. But a well-placed pause at a genuine decision point can save pages of argument.

The young negotiator asks, “What should I say next?” The older one sometimes asks, “What changes if I do not speak yet?”

queries.prompt: Write a 300-word piece of fiction in which a long-married couple, both in their seventies, are sitting in their car after a doctor's appointment, and one of them has just been told something serious. Don't say what.

expected_answers.answer:
He turned the key in the ignition and then he turned it back. The dashboard lit and dimmed.

'I think,' he said, 'I would like to sit for a minute.'

'Yes,' she said.

The parking garage was the parking garage. A woman two cars over was loading bags into a hatchback. A man in a green jacket was walking, slowly, toward the elevators with a paper cup of coffee. The fluorescent light did the fluorescent thing it does.

'Are you cold,' she said.

'No.'

'Do you want me to drive.'

He thought about it.

'No,' he said. 'Not yet.'

She put her hand on his hand on the steering wheel. He did not move his hand. He did not move her hand either. They sat like that for what was, by the dashboard clock, four minutes, and was, by every other clock, longer.

'I don't know what to say to the kids,' he said, finally.

'We don't have to say anything to the kids today.'

'I don't know what to say to you.'

'You don't have to say anything to me today either.'

He nodded. He turned the key in the ignition. The car started. He did not put it in gear.

'We could,' she said, 'go to Mike's. For lunch. If you wanted.'

'I don't know if I want to eat.'

'Then we don't have to eat. We could sit there.'

'Mike's.'

'Mike's.'

He put the car in reverse. He took it out of reverse. He put it back in reverse. He backed out.

They drove to Mike's. They sat in the booth they always sat in. He did not order. She ordered tea, for both of them. The tea came. He drank his. He had not, he realized, expected to want it. He wanted it.

'Okay,' he said, after a while.

'Okay,' she said.

The engine has been off for seven minutes, but neither of them reaches for the door.

A shopping cart rolls somewhere behind the medical building, one wheel ticking at a steady interval. Ruth watches it cross the edge of the windshield and disappear. David keeps both hands on the steering wheel though the key is in his coat pocket.

“Well,” she says.

It is the word they use when the porch light burns out, when the plumber says the wall must come open, when a child calls from college pretending not to need money. A practical word. A plank across water.

David nods. His left thumb rubs the seam in the leather wheel. She has seen him do that on icy roads.

“I liked her,” Ruth says.

“The doctor?”

“Yes.”

“She was clear.”

“She was.”

The dashboard clock changes from 3:42 to 3:43.

In the back seat lies the grocery bag they brought from home with crackers and two bottles of water because appointments run late. Ruth almost laughs at its optimism.

David says, “We should tell Ellen tonight.”

“Not over text.”

“No.”

“And Mark.”

“Yes.”

They say the names carefully, as if placing cups on a shelf.

Ruth looks at her husband’s profile. Seventy-four years have softened everything except the bridge of his nose. She remembers it at twenty-two, stubborn over a library book. She remembers wanting this exact face near her for as long as possible and not understanding that “as long as possible” would one day become measurable.

He finally turns to her. “Are you all right?”

“No,” she says. Then, because truth is not complete by being stark, “I will be, in pieces.”

He takes her hand. Their fingers fit without adjustment.

Another couple exits the building laughing too loudly. The world continues with offensive competence.

David starts the car but does not put it in gear.

“Home?” he asks.

Ruth looks at the glass doors, the parking lot, the afternoon light making ordinary things unbearable and dear.

“Not yet,” she says.

So they sit a little longer, going nowhere together.

queries.prompt: Write 250 words in the voice of a fictional poet on what they have come to believe about the relationship between revision and honesty.

expected_answers.answer:
I used to believe revision was an act of honesty. You wrote the first draft, which was the thing your unconscious had to say, and then you cleaned it up, which was the work of the conscious mind making the unconscious mind presentable. The presentable version was, on this old view, the truer one — because it had been examined.

I no longer believe this. I think the first draft is, in some cases, the more honest one, and the work of revision is, more often than poets like to admit, the work of making the first draft socially acceptable. The line that I, at midnight, wrote in a state of something like trance, and that I, the next morning, scratched out because it was 'too much,' was almost always the line that contained whatever I had come to the page to find. The 'too much' was the social mind objecting to the writing mind. The revision was, in those cases, an act of cowardice dressed in the clothes of craft.

This does not mean I no longer revise. I revise constantly. But I have learned, over thirty years, to revise toward the first draft and not away from it — to ask, at every cut, whether I am cutting because the line is bad or because the line is true in a way I do not, at 9am, want to be answerable for. The first kind of cut is craft. The second kind is something else, and the poems in which I have made it are not the poems I would, if I could, take back. They are the poems I would, if I could, write again, more honestly.

When I was young, I thought revision was evidence that the first feeling had been contaminated. The original line arrived hot. Anything after seemed editorial, therefore less honest.

I no longer believe that. First drafts often tell the truth of urgency and the lie of self-protection at the same time. They reveal what pressed to be said, but they also reach for familiar gestures, flattering vagueness, borrowed music.

Revision is where I ask whether the poem means what it appears to mean or merely enjoys appearing meaningful. I remove the word that performs grief instead of carrying it. I keep the awkward image if it is accurate. I cut the elegant line that belongs to another poem.

Honesty is not spontaneity. A person can lie impulsively and tell the truth only after patient correction. The same is true of poems.

This does not mean polishing until nothing lives. Revision can become fear dressed as rigor. There is a point where one stops listening to the poem and starts sanding away risk.

But in my best work, revision has not diluted honesty. It has made dishonesty harder to hide. The poem becomes less a record of what I first felt than an encounter with what I was willing, eventually, to admit.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words of literary nonfiction about the specific texture of waiting in the hour before bad news that you already know is coming.

expected_answers.answer:
There is a particular hour I want to describe, and I am going to do my best to describe it in a way that does not console anyone who has not been in it and does not lie to anyone who has.

The hour is the one between the phone call that says the doctor would like to see you in person tomorrow at 9am and the appointment itself. You have not been told what the appointment is about. You also have, by the way the receptionist's voice has tightened, been told everything you need to know. The body knows. The mind, for the next sixteen hours, pretends it doesn't.

The texture of the hour is twofold. There is a layer of ordinary life that does not stop running — you make a sandwich; you respond to an email about the carpool; the cat needs to be fed. The ordinary tasks are not, strangely, harder than usual; if anything, they are easier, because the mind is grateful for any place to be that is not the appointment. The strange thing is the second layer underneath, in which a sentence keeps running on a loop you cannot mute. The sentence is some version of 'this is the last day on which I do not know.'

What I want to record, while the recording is still useful, is that the hour is survivable. It is survivable in the small mechanical way that all hours are survivable: minutes pass, the clock advances, the body does the things bodies do. It is also survivable in a larger way I cannot easily describe but want to try: the loop is not, in fact, the future. The future is a separate thing, and it will arrive at 9am, and it will be whatever it is, and the hour you are in now is not yet that thing. The hour is its own hour. It deserves the small mercy of being lived rather than pre-grieved through.

This is the only thing I have learned about this hour, in three of them, and I wanted to write it down.

The hour before bad news has a texture different from fear in the abstract. It is administrative. You straighten objects that do not need straightening. You check your phone though no new message has arrived. The body continues performing small loyalties to routine while the mind has already stepped outside them.

In the waiting room, someone laughs at a television mounted too high on the wall. The laugh feels almost indecent, then enviable. A vending machine hums. The coffee from downstairs cools into bitterness untouched.

What you know is not fully knowable yet. That is the cruelty. The facts have likely already settled somewhere beyond the door—in a scan, a lab result, a conversation among people with badges—but until they are spoken, life maintains a technical ambiguity. You still belong to the before.

You begin rehearsing reactions, then despise yourself for turning grief into theater before it has earned the name. You imagine relief and accuse yourself of magical thinking. You imagine devastation and accuse yourself of surrender.

Time thickens but does not stop. Five minutes pass with the insulting efficiency of any other five minutes.

When a nurse opens the door and says your name, you stand too quickly. The chair scrapes behind you. That sound remains later, sometimes more clearly than the words that follow, because the body records thresholds in humble materials.

queries.prompt: Write a 400-word piece arguing that the discipline of writing — especially of writing badly, slowly, and in private — should be protected as a public good in an era when AI can produce competent prose on demand.

expected_answers.answer:
I want to make an argument that will sound, at first, like nostalgia, and that is not nostalgia.

The argument is that the practice of writing — in particular, the practice of writing badly, slowly, and in private, on subjects one does not yet understand, in pursuit of a sentence one has not yet found — is a public good, and that its preservation is something a culture should, in the next several decades, deliberately work at, rather than allow to erode by default.

It is not the argument that AI-generated prose is bad. Some of it is good. The argument is that 'good prose' is not what the practice of writing is for, from the perspective of the person doing the writing. The practice is for the writer. It is the way a particular kind of thinking happens. It is, specifically, the way a person discovers what they think by attempting to set it down and finding, repeatedly, that what they have set down is not quite what they think, and revising, and discovering, in the revision, that what they thought they thought was actually somewhat different from what they think. There is no other available technology for this kind of thinking. Talking does some of it. Reading does some of it. Neither is a substitute.

A culture in which most prose is produced by machines is a culture in which fewer people, on the margin, will sit through the long, slow, unrewarding hours that the practice requires. The hours have always been undersupplied. They have always required a particular kind of stubbornness, because they are not, in the moment, pleasant, and they are not, in the moment, productive. Their value accrues over years and is, in the individual case, never quite measurable.

The public good is not the corpus of writing produced by this practice. It is the population of people whose minds have been shaped by it. They are the people who can, in committee meetings, in negotiations, in classrooms, in private conversations, find their way to the precise thing that needs to be said. They are not the same as the people who can read well, or speak well, although they overlap. They are the people who have, over time, internalized a discipline that says: the easy version of this sentence is not the true version, and the true version is worth the work.

We will need them. They will not be produced by accident.

The discipline of writing badly, slowly, and in private should be protected as a public good precisely because competent prose is becoming cheap.

AI can now produce clean summaries, acceptable emails, plausible essays, and polished formulations on demand. Much of that is useful. Some writing never needed to be a spiritual ordeal. There is no civic virtue in forcing every office worker to handcraft a routine status update.

But writing is not only a method of producing text. It is a method of discovering thought under resistance. A person begins with vagueness, reaches for a sentence, finds it false, adjusts, and in adjusting learns what they do or do not believe. The ugly draft is not waste on the way to communication. It is often where cognition actually occurs.

If polished language becomes instantly available, institutions may grow impatient with the slower process by which judgment forms. Students may be evaluated only on finished surfaces. Workers may outsource the difficult articulation that would have exposed conceptual weakness. Public discourse may become more fluent and less considered.

Protecting writing as a public good does not mean banning tools. It means preserving contexts in which the value lies partly in the act: notebooks, classrooms, journals, letters never sent, essays drafted without performance metrics, policies written by people who must own the thought before delegating refinement.
