There is also an equality argument. If only people with leisure continue practicing sustained private thought through writing, while everyone else is nudged toward generated adequacy, we risk stratifying not just expression but interior development.

Societies need people who can compose themselves as well as compose documents. The first kind of composition is slow, frequently embarrassing, and resistant to automation because its product is not merely the paragraph. It is the person who had to become more exact in order to write it.

queries.prompt: Write 300 words from a fictional, retiring federal public defender on what she has come to believe about the criminal-justice system she has worked inside for thirty-five years.

expected_answers.answer:
I am going to tell you what I have come to believe, after thirty-five years in this office, and I am going to try to do it without sounding either cynical or naïve, because both temptations are real and both are, I think, wrong.

What I have come to believe is that the system I have worked inside is genuinely better than its critics say in one specific way, and genuinely worse than its defenders say in two specific ways, and I will name all three.

The better-than: I have, in this office, watched many individual prosecutors, judges, probation officers, and police officers, in many individual cases, exercise discretion in favor of the person in front of them, at small cost to themselves, on grounds of nothing more than that the person in front of them was a person. This kind of mercy is not visible from outside the system, because it does not produce documents. It happens in declined charges, in undocumented diversions, in plea offers that go slightly below what the office would have accepted. It is real. The system would be much worse without it, and the people who say the system contains no mercy at all are wrong.

The worse-than, first: the system is, in the aggregate, very bad at distinguishing people who should be in prison from people who should not, and it is especially bad at this distinction when the person in question is poor. The good intentions of individual actors do not aggregate into a good outcome at scale. This is a structural fact, not a moral one.

The worse-than, second: the system protects itself, institutionally, against feedback. It does not, by its own design, easily learn from its errors. The errors are processed as the system processes everything, which is by moving them through the system again. This is the thing I have been least able to make peace with, and the thing I would, if I could, change. I have not been able to change it. I do not know whether anyone can. I am going to retire still hoping that someone can.

After thirty-five years as a federal public defender, I no longer believe the criminal-justice system is best understood by asking whether it contains good people. It contains many. Judges who listen. Prosecutors who exercise restraint. Marshals who treat frightened families decently. Defense lawyers who work until language blurs.

The problem is that decent individuals operate inside machinery that processes human crisis at volume. Poverty arrives in court as failure to appear, untreated addiction as supervised-release violation, mental illness as disruption, childhood damage as prior history. The system translates suffering into categories it can manage, then congratulates itself for consistency.

I have represented people who caused terrible harm. Public defense does not require pretending otherwise. It requires insisting that the government prove its case, that punishment remain bounded by law, and that a human being is not identical to the worst fact in an indictment.

What has changed me most is not acquittal or loss. It is repetition. The same neighborhoods. The same overcharged leverage in plea bargaining. The same clients accepting years because trial risk is intolerable. The same families trying to look respectable in hard benches.

I do not think the system is wholly corrupt. That would be easier. It is more troubling: it is often sincere, procedurally careful, and morally insufficient.

Still, I have seen moments worth defending. A judge lowering his voice because a defendant is terrified. A prosecutor disclosing weakness quickly. A client after sentencing asking whether I think his daughter will still visit. Law matters in those moments because power matters.

Retirement has not made me cynical. It has made me exact. Justice is not what the system calls itself. It is what remains protected when the person before it has the least status, the least money, and the least ability to make anyone uncomfortable on their behalf.
